text: This is a book dealing with how to write careful, rigorous mathematical proofs. The book is paired with code in the computer formalization language Lean .
html: This is a book dealing with how to write careful, rigorous mathematical proofs. The book is paired with code in the computer formalization language Lean.

text: The book’s focus is on technique, not on theory-building: rather few theorems are proved and then referred back to later. Instead, the core of the book is in examples. Over two hundred problems appear with solutions as examples in the text, and several hundred more problems appear without solution as exercises for the reader. Each problem and each solution is presented both in standard mathematical prose and in Lean (the book should be read with a computer open to the corresponding file of Lean code), and since these two “languages” are almost equally foreign for students who are new to proofs, most solutions are annotated with informal commentary.
html: The book’s focus is on technique, not on theory-building: rather few theorems are proved and then referred back to later. Instead, the core of the book is in examples. Over two hundred problems appear with solutions as examples in the text, and several hundred more problems appear without solution as exercises for the reader. Each problem and each solution is presented both in standard mathematical prose and in Lean (the book should be read with a computer open to the corresponding file of Lean code), and since these two “languages” are almost equally foreign for students who are new to proofs, most solutions are annotated with informal commentary.

text: People have been expressing mathematical arguments in words for thousands of years, and mathematical language is by now very standardized, with many conventions to let mathematicians communicate with each other efficiently and unambiguously. The primary goal of this book is to teach you to read and write standard mathematical English prose.
html: People have been expressing mathematical arguments in words for thousands of years, and mathematical language is by now very standardized, with many conventions to let mathematicians communicate with each other efficiently and unambiguously. The primary goal of this book is to teach you to read and write standard mathematical English prose.

text: Computer systems called interactive theorem provers (also known as proof assistants or systems for formalization ) offer an alternative way to express a mathematical argument. Lean , an open-source project developed at Microsoft Research and elsewhere since 2013, is one such system, but they have existed since the early days of computing .
html: Computer systems called interactive theorem provers (also known as proof assistants or systems for formalization) offer an alternative way to express a mathematical argument. Lean, an open-source project developed at Microsoft Research and elsewhere since 2013, is one such system, but they have existed since the early days of computing.

text: Interactive theorem-proving systems such as Lean have become steadily easier to use over the years, but they are not yet actually easy to use: they can be fussy and unforgiving, just like any other computer programming language. So why does this book propose that you use such a system in your first encounter with proofs?
html: Interactive theorem-proving systems such as Lean have become steadily easier to use over the years, but they are not yet actually easy to use: they can be fussy and unforgiving, just like any other computer programming language. So why does this book propose that you use such a system in your first encounter with proofs?

text: First: Like it says on the tin, writing proofs in an interactive theorem prover is interactive. At each point in your proof, you can see a visual representation of the proof state : what you know (your hypotheses ) and what you are currently working towards (your goal(s) ). If you are new to writing mathematical proofs, you may be surprised at how hard you find it to distinguish hypotheses from goals on paper after a few steps alternating between forward and backward reasoning, or how easily you lose track of one case in a case division. Lean’s live-updating visual representation of the proof state frees you from needing to keep this in your working memory.
html: First: Like it says on the tin, writing proofs in an interactive theorem prover is interactive. At each point in your proof, you can see a visual representation of the proof state: what you know (your hypotheses) and what you are currently working towards (your goal(s)). If you are new to writing mathematical proofs, you may be surprised at how hard you find it to distinguish hypotheses from goals on paper after a few steps alternating between forward and backward reasoning, or how easily you lose track of one case in a case division. Lean’s live-updating visual representation of the proof state frees you from needing to keep this in your working memory.

text: Second: Computer formalization systems, fussy unforgiving programming languages that they are, throw syntax errors when you make a mistake. Feedback is instant and you can keep iterating until you have something that works. Writing a solution in Lean ensures that it is completely correct: no substitutions of an inequality under a minus sign, no divisions by zero, no terms dropped in the algebra. This is especially useful in doing proof-based mathematics: make a small error halfway through a calculus problem and the rest of the solution probably won’t change much, but make a small error halfway through a proof and the rest of the solution may be useless.
html: Second: Computer formalization systems, fussy unforgiving programming languages that they are, throw syntax errors when you make a mistake. Feedback is instant and you can keep iterating until you have something that works. Writing a solution in Lean ensures that it is completely correct: no substitutions of an inequality under a minus sign, no divisions by zero, no terms dropped in the algebra. This is especially useful in doing proof-based mathematics: make a small error halfway through a calculus problem and the rest of the solution probably won’t change much, but make a small error halfway through a proof and the rest of the solution may be useless.

text: Finally: You interact with Lean using tactics which each perform a single step of a certain mode of reasoning. The tactics which are taught in this textbook (some custom-written for this book) each constitute a single permissible “atom” of reasoning in the mental world of the book. This makes objective something which in a prose-only textbook can be subjective: what constitutes a fully-detailed proof. What’s more, the “atoms” I offer you are designed to nudge you toward certain styles of mathematical argument structure 1 which are conventional in standard mathematical prose but which students are often slow to adopt.
html: Finally: You interact with Lean using tactics which each perform a single step of a certain mode of reasoning. The tactics which are taught in this textbook (some custom-written for this book) each constitute a single permissible “atom” of reasoning in the mental world of the book. This makes objective something which in a prose-only textbook can be subjective: what constitutes a fully-detailed proof. What’s more, the “atoms” I offer you are designed to nudge you toward certain styles of mathematical argument structure 1 which are conventional in standard mathematical prose but which students are often slow to adopt.

text: This is a book about mathematics which uses Lean as a tool. It is designed so that the learning curve is flatter for the Lean than it is for the mathematics 2 – partly by a careful choice of exercises, and partly by writing in a Lean “dialect” of my own, with a limited Lean vocabulary 3 which is just flexible enough for the mathematical needs of the book. (A summary of the major differences can be found in the appendix Transitioning to mainstream Lean .) My hope is that most of your intellectual effort in solving the problems will be devoted to the mathematics, not to Lean’s implementation details or quirks of syntax.
html: This is a book about mathematics which uses Lean as a tool. It is designed so that the learning curve is flatter for the Lean than it is for the mathematics 2 – partly by a careful choice of exercises, and partly by writing in a Lean “dialect” of my own, with a limited Lean vocabulary 3 which is just flexible enough for the mathematical needs of the book. (A summary of the major differences can be found in the appendix Transitioning to mainstream Lean.) My hope is that most of your intellectual effort in solving the problems will be devoted to the mathematics, not to Lean’s implementation details or quirks of syntax.

text: You are ready to read this book if (1) you know high school algebra inside out and (2) you have experience learning to carry out complicated mathematical algorithms. I have in mind, as a typical reader, a first- or second-year university student who has just taken Calculus II. But no calculus is actually used in this book.
html: You are ready to read this book if (1) you know high school algebra inside out and (2) you have experience learning to carry out complicated mathematical algorithms. I have in mind, as a typical reader, a first- or second-year university student who has just taken Calculus II. But no calculus is actually used in this book.

text: The main novelty of this text is the “bilingual” presentation, juxtaposing prose mathematics with Lean code. But the design choices enforced by this presentation have shaped the text in other ways.
html: The main novelty of this text is the “bilingual” presentation, juxtaposing prose mathematics with Lean code. But the design choices enforced by this presentation have shaped the text in other ways.

text: Chapter 1 contains an unusually careful treatment of “calculational proofs”. These proofs are a natural starting point for the book because they translate easily to Lean, but they are also worthy of attention as a topic which students at this level often struggle with. 4
html: Chapter 1 contains an unusually careful treatment of “calculational proofs”. These proofs are a natural starting point for the book because they translate easily to Lean, but they are also worthy of attention as a topic which students at this level often struggle with. 4

text: Chapter 2 and Chapter 4 constitute a slow march through the rules of natural deduction , solving problems about \(\mathbb{N}\) , \(\mathbb{Z}\) , \(\mathbb{Q}\) and \(\mathbb{R}\) which feature increasingly many of the logical connectives and quantifiers. The requirement to translate everything to Lean keeps the book strictly honest through these chapters. The typical intro-to-proof textbook does not have this guardrail, and will commonly make minor abuses here – for example, presenting a good example of a proof by cases which implicitly also uses a not-yet-covered proof technique such as filling a witness to an existential.
html: Chapter 2 and Chapter 4 constitute a slow march through the rules of natural deduction, solving problems about \(\mathbb{N}\), \(\mathbb{Z}\), \(\mathbb{Q}\) and \(\mathbb{R}\) which feature increasingly many of the logical connectives and quantifiers. The requirement to translate everything to Lean keeps the book strictly honest through these chapters. The typical intro-to-proof textbook does not have this guardrail, and will commonly make minor abuses here – for example, presenting a good example of a proof by cases which implicitly also uses a not-yet-covered proof technique such as filling a witness to an existential.

text: Logic is not taught explicitly until Chapter 5 , by which stage readers will already be comfortable with the various logical connectives/quantifiers and with translating mathematical statements back and forth between words and symbols. This permits the logic chapter to be relatively brief, with a focus on the concept of logical equivalence (presented primarily using natural deduction, to link with Chapters 2 and 4 , rather than using truth tables). 5
html: Logic is not taught explicitly until Chapter 5, by which stage readers will already be comfortable with the various logical connectives/quantifiers and with translating mathematical statements back and forth between words and symbols. This permits the logic chapter to be relatively brief, with a focus on the concept of logical equivalence (presented primarily using natural deduction, to link with Chapters 2 and 4, rather than using truth tables). 5

text: The other chapters of the book are more recognizable as the usual subject matter of a first course on proofs. Sufficient familiarity with Lean has been established, and the mathematical presentation is no longer constrained.
html: The other chapters of the book are more recognizable as the usual subject matter of a first course on proofs. Sufficient familiarity with Lean has been established, and the mathematical presentation is no longer constrained.

text: Chapter 3 covers the basic notions of elementary number theory. This chapter uses only a limited toolbox of reasoning techniques, to permit it to be placed as a break between Chapter 2 and Chapter 4 . Number-theoretic definitions and theorems continue to appear as examples in subsequent chapters, and the presentation of the subject concludes in Chapter 7 with the big results of Greek mathematics: infinitude of primes, Euclid’s lemma, and the irrationality of the square root of two.
html: Chapter 3 covers the basic notions of elementary number theory. This chapter uses only a limited toolbox of reasoning techniques, to permit it to be placed as a break between Chapter 2 and Chapter 4. Number-theoretic definitions and theorems continue to appear as examples in subsequent chapters, and the presentation of the subject concludes in Chapter 7 with the big results of Greek mathematics: infinitude of primes, Euclid’s lemma, and the irrationality of the square root of two.

text: Chapter 6 covers induction. The treatment is fairly comprehensive, including induction and recursion relative to various nontrivial well-founded relations on \(\mathbb{Z}\) , \(\mathbb{N}\times \mathbb{N}\) and \(\mathbb{Z}\times \mathbb{Z}\) .
html: Chapter 6 covers induction. The treatment is fairly comprehensive, including induction and recursion relative to various nontrivial well-founded relations on \(\mathbb{Z}\), \(\mathbb{N}\times \mathbb{N}\) and \(\mathbb{Z}\times \mathbb{Z}\).

text: Finally, Chapters 8 , 9 and 10 cover functions, sets, and relations, in that order – we take the type-theoretic point of view that functions are the primitive notion and sets and relations are defined as functions into \(\left[\operatorname{true}/\operatorname{false}\right]\) .
html: Finally, Chapters 8, 9 and 10 cover functions, sets, and relations, in that order – we take the type-theoretic point of view that functions are the primitive notion and sets and relations are defined as functions into \(\left[\operatorname{true}/\operatorname{false}\right]\).

text: This book is based on lecture notes from a course I taught in Spring 2023 at Fordham University. There were 20 students in the course, mostly first- and second-year students, with a median background of Calculus II. Many but not all had also taken a first course in computer programming.
html: This book is based on lecture notes from a course I taught in Spring 2023 at Fordham University. There were 20 students in the course, mostly first- and second-year students, with a median background of Calculus II. Many but not all had also taken a first course in computer programming.

text: The course had 75-minute classes, twice a week for 13 weeks, and in this time covered about 80% of the material in this book. A typical class structure might look like this:
html: The course had 75-minute classes, twice a week for 13 weeks, and in this time covered about 80% of the material in this book. A typical class structure might look like this:

text: The homework assignments for the course are available on request. They are relatively short (5-7 problems per week), but students were required to submit almost all problems both in writing and in Lean. Most students required support in office hours or by email to complete the homework assignments.
html: The homework assignments for the course are available on request. They are relatively short (5-7 problems per week), but students were required to submit almost all problems both in writing and in Lean. Most students required support in office hours or by email to complete the homework assignments.

text: The course also featured oral examinations at the 5- and 10-week marks. These were 20-minute one-on-one interviews assessing Lean fluency: students solved previously-unseen Lean exercises (different exercises for each student), explaining their reasoning aloud. The grade breakdown for the course was 25% homework, 20% oral examinations, and 55% traditional written examinations (a midterm and a final, both completely Lean-free).
html: The course also featured oral examinations at the 5- and 10-week marks. These were 20-minute one-on-one interviews assessing Lean fluency: students solved previously-unseen Lean exercises (different exercises for each student), explaining their reasoning aloud. The grade breakdown for the course was 25% homework, 20% oral examinations, and 55% traditional written examinations (a midterm and a final, both completely Lean-free).

text: Clearly, the combination of in-class work with instructor circulating, homework support in office hours and by email, and oral examinations adds up to a significant amount of time spent interacting with individual (or small groups of) students. The student:staff ratio 20:1 was sustainable. I suspect that to go beyond this ratio, one would need very strong students or an experienced and enthusiastic TA.
html: Clearly, the combination of in-class work with instructor circulating, homework support in office hours and by email, and oral examinations adds up to a significant amount of time spent interacting with individual (or small groups of) students. The student:staff ratio 20:1 was sustainable. I suspect that to go beyond this ratio, one would need very strong students or an experienced and enthusiastic TA.

text: The students ran Lean in a cloud development environment, to avoid needing to install Lean on their own computers. I used Gitpod for this (an alternative is GitHub Codespaces ) – see the README of the book’s code repository for brief instructions on how to start using Gitpod. The students’ Lean homework was automatically graded using a Gradescope auto-grader (an alternative is GitHub Classroom ). The Lean community’s teaching advice webpage provides instructions and troubleshooting for setting up this kind of course infrastructure.

text: If you are looking for the reverse, Mathematics in Lean is the canonical introduction to mathematical Lean. But note that that book expects more mathematical experience than this one does: writing idiomatic Lean code, even to prove elementary statements, requires some mathematical maturity.
html: If you are looking for the reverse, Mathematics in Lean is the canonical introduction to mathematical Lean. But note that that book expects more mathematical experience than this one does: writing idiomatic Lean code, even to prove elementary statements, requires some mathematical maturity.

text: The algebraic-reasoning tactic vocabulary of ring , rw , numbers (AKA norm_num ), rel (custom-written for this book but now in mathlib proper), extra (custom-written) and cancel (custom-written) suffices for pretty much all algebraic reasoning over the integers, including nonlinear inequalities. The training in this vernacular presented over the course of Sections 1.2 - 2.1 pays off later, by avoiding the need to invoke the endless lemmas such as mul_le_mul_of_nonneg_left , pow_pos or le_of_pow_le_pow by name. Other custom automation lightly streamlines work with induction principles, well-foundedness justifications, product types, and sets. In total, fewer than fifty lemmas are invoked by name in the book.
html: The algebraic-reasoning tactic vocabulary of ring, rw, numbers (AKA norm_num), rel (custom-written for this book but now in mathlib proper), extra (custom-written) and cancel (custom-written) suffices for pretty much all algebraic reasoning over the integers, including nonlinear inequalities. The training in this vernacular presented over the course of Sections 1.2 - 2.1 pays off later, by avoiding the need to invoke the endless lemmas such as mul_le_mul_of_nonneg_left, pow_pos or le_of_pow_le_pow by name. Other custom automation lightly streamlines work with induction principles, well-foundedness justifications, product types, and sets. In total, fewer than fifty lemmas are invoked by name in the book.

text: It’s in fact quite possible to get through the equality -heavy reasoning of many intro-to-proof courses without really mastering this mode of reasoning, but it’s nearly impossible to reason about inequalities without mastering calculational proofs, and students who don’t pick up the skill in an intro-to-proof course will find it come back to haunt them when they reach real analysis.

text: This book begins in the familiar world of numbers: \(\mathbb{N}\) , the natural numbers (which in this book include 0); \(\mathbb{Z}\) , the integers; \(\mathbb{Q}\) , the rational numbers; and \(\mathbb{R}\) , the real numbers. We solve problems which feel pretty close to high school algebra – deducing equalities/inequalities from other equalities/inequalities – using a technique which is not usually taught in high school algebra: building a single chain of expressions connecting the left-hand side with the right.
html: This book begins in the familiar world of numbers: \(\mathbb{N}\), the natural numbers (which in this book include 0); \(\mathbb{Z}\), the integers; \(\mathbb{Q}\), the rational numbers; and \(\mathbb{R}\), the real numbers. We solve problems which feel pretty close to high school algebra – deducing equalities/inequalities from other equalities/inequalities – using a technique which is not usually taught in high school algebra: building a single chain of expressions connecting the left-hand side with the right.

text: We start with proofs of equalities. Here is a typical example of the technique mentioned.
html: We start with proofs of equalities. Here is a typical example of the technique mentioned.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be rational numbers and suppose that \(a - b = 4\) and \(ab=1\) . Show that \((a+b)^2=20\) .
html: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be rational numbers and suppose that \(a - b = 4\) and \(ab=1\). Show that \((a+b)^2=20\).

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: We call the above proof a proof by calculation . The goal was to show that \((a+b)^2=20\) , and we established this by writing down a chain of equalities, which starts with the expression \((a+b)^2\) (top left) and ends with \(20\) (bottom right). The proof, implicitly, has three steps:
html: We call the above proof a proof by calculation. The goal was to show that \((a+b)^2=20\), and we established this by writing down a chain of equalities, which starts with the expression \((a+b)^2\) (top left) and ends with \(20\) (bottom right). The proof, implicitly, has three steps:

text: 1. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }(a+b)^2=(a-b)^2+4ab}\) : this is a purely algebraic rearrangement – after expanding out and simplifying, both sides are the same quantity, \(a^2+2ab+b^2\) .
html: 1. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }(a+b)^2=(a-b)^2+4ab}\): this is a purely algebraic rearrangement – after expanding out and simplifying, both sides are the same quantity, \(a^2+2ab+b^2\).

text: 2. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }(a-b)^2+4ab=4^2+4\cdot 1}\) : this is a pure substitution step, using the known facts that \(a-b=4\) and \(ab=1\) .
html: 2. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }(a-b)^2+4ab=4^2+4\cdot 1}\): this is a pure substitution step, using the known facts that \(a-b=4\) and \(ab=1\).

text: 3. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }4^2+4\cdot 1=20}\) : this is another purely algebraic step.
html: 3. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }4^2+4\cdot 1=20}\): this is another purely algebraic step.

text: This is the most common style of presenting an equality proof in advanced mathematics textbooks and in research mathematics. There is a trade-off: it usually takes more work for you, the writer, of the proof, to organize a proof in this style, but the resulting proof is short and easy to check, which is courteous to your readers.
html: This is the most common style of presenting an equality proof in advanced mathematics textbooks and in research mathematics. There is a trade-off: it usually takes more work for you, the writer, of the proof, to organize a proof in this style, but the resulting proof is short and easy to check, which is courteous to your readers.

text: In Section 1.3 we will discuss the question of how to come up with a proof in this style. For now, let’s focus on how to understand them.
html: In Section 1.3 we will discuss the question of how to come up with a proof in this style. For now, let’s focus on how to understand them.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Let \(r\) and \(s\) be real numbers, and suppose that \(r + 2 s = -1\) and \(s = 3\) . Prove that \(r = -7\) .
html: Let \(r\) and \(s\) be real numbers, and suppose that \(r + 2 s = -1\) and \(s = 3\). Prove that \(r = -7\).

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: This proof implicitly has four steps, which successively transform the left-hand side, \(r\) , to the right-hand side, \(-7\) :
html: This proof implicitly has four steps, which successively transform the left-hand side, \(r\), to the right-hand side, \(-7\):

text: 1. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }r=(r+2s)-2s}\) : a purely algebraic rearrangement.
html: 1. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }r=(r+2s)-2s}\): a purely algebraic rearrangement.

text: 2. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }(r+2s)-2s=-1 - 2s}\) : this is a pure substitution step, using the known fact that \(r + 2 s = -1\) .
html: 2. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }(r+2s)-2s=-1 - 2s}\): this is a pure substitution step, using the known fact that \(r + 2 s = -1\).

text: 2. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }-1-2s=-1 - 2 \cdot 3}\) : this is a pure substitution step, using the known fact that \(s = 3\) .
html: 2. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }-1-2s=-1 - 2 \cdot 3}\): this is a pure substitution step, using the known fact that \(s = 3\).

text: 4. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }-1 - 2 \cdot 3=-7}\) : this is another purely algebraic step.
html: 4. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }-1 - 2 \cdot 3=-7}\): this is another purely algebraic step.

text: You might wonder what flexibility there is in presenting a proof in this style. It is common to put each expression of the proof on its own line, so that the first expression isn’t all alone on the left. This is certainly acceptable, and is useful if the expressions involved are very long, so the extra space is needed.

text: Finally, notice that the calculations end with a period. The whole calculation is considered to be a single sentence, and the period ends it.

text: In this book, we will learn to write every proof in two ways: in words, as humans have done for thousands of years, and in a computer system called a proof assistant , an approach which was first experimented with in the 1960s and is still quite unusual. We will be using the proof assistant Lean 4 , developed since 2014 at Microsoft Research and elsewhere by a team led by Leonardo de Moura, and its standard mathematical library Mathlib .

text: Problem

text: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be rational numbers and suppose that \(a - b = 4\) and \(ab=1\) . Show that \((a+b)^2=20\) .
html: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be rational numbers and suppose that \(a - b = 4\) and \(ab=1\). Show that \((a+b)^2=20\).

text: The code {a b : ℚ} sets up two variables a and b whose type is ℚ , which is the standard mathematical notation for the rational numbers (mnemonic: “quotients”).
html: The code {a b : ℚ} sets up two variables a and b whose type is ℚ, which is the standard mathematical notation for the rational numbers (mnemonic: “quotients”).

text: The code (h1 : a - b = 4) (h2 : a * b = 1) sets up two hypotheses , for the two facts given to us in the problem statement, \(a - b = 4\) and \(ab=1\) . Hypotheses in Lean have names, here h1 and h2 , so that we can refer back to them later. Notice that multiplication must be written explicitly, using the symbol * , whereas on paper multiplication is inferred by writing two variables next to each other like \(ab\) .
html: The code (h1 : a - b = 4) (h2 : a * b = 1) sets up two hypotheses, for the two facts given to us in the problem statement, \(a - b = 4\) and \(ab=1\). Notice that multiplication must be written explicitly, using the symbol *, whereas on paper multiplication is inferred by writing two variables next to each other like \(ab\).

text: After the colon, : , comes the goal , the statement we have been asked to prove: (a + b) ^ 2 = 20 , i.e. \((a+b)^2=20\) . The symbol ^ is used in Lean for raising to a power.
html: After the colon, :, comes the goal, the statement we have been asked to prove: (a + b) ^ 2 = 20, i.e. \((a+b)^2=20\). The symbol ^ is used in Lean for raising to a power.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be integers and suppose that \(a = 2b + 5\) and \(b = 3\) . Show that \(a = 11\) .
html: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be integers and suppose that \(a = 2b + 5\) and \(b = 3\). Show that \(a = 11\).

text: Since the goal in this problem is to show that \(a=11\) , we already know that the solution will look something like this:
html: Since the goal in this problem is to show that \(a=11\), we already know that the solution will look something like this:

text: Casting around for ideas, we see that one of the hypotheses, \(a = 2b + 5\) , has \(a\) on the left-hand side. So we put that as the first step of the calculation, and the rest of the steps write themselves.
html: Casting around for ideas, we see that one of the hypotheses, \(a = 2b + 5\), has \(a\) on the left-hand side. So we put that as the first step of the calculation, and the rest of the steps write themselves.

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: More commonly, none of the hypotheses have a left- or right-hand side which exactly appears in the goal. Here is an example.
html: More commonly, none of the hypotheses have a left- or right-hand side which exactly appears in the goal. Here is an example.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Let \(x\) be an integer and suppose that \(x+4=2\) . Show that \(x=-2\) .
html: Let \(x\) be an integer and suppose that \(x+4=2\). Show that \(x=-2\).

text: Since the goal in this problem is to show that \(x=-2\) , we already know that the solution will look something like this:
html: Since the goal in this problem is to show that \(x=-2\), we already know that the solution will look something like this:

text: Our only hypothesis has an \(x+4\) on the left-hand side, so we create an \(x+4\) in our goal by adding and subtracting \(4\) from \(x\) , like this: \(x=(x+4)-4\) . Then the rest of the proof works out easily.
html: Our only hypothesis has an \(x+4\) on the left-hand side, so we create an \(x+4\) in our goal by adding and subtracting \(4\) from \(x\), like this: \(x=(x+4)-4\). Then the rest of the proof works out easily.

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: Sometimes we need to perform this process, of “creating” one side of a hypothesis inside the goal, more than once.
html: Sometimes we need to perform this process, of “creating” one side of a hypothesis inside the goal, more than once.

text: That seems almost too slick, right? In my scratch work, I came up with this solution using the method of polynomial long division , which you may have been taught before: when \(z ^ 4 - z ^ 3 - z ^ 2 + 2 z + 1\) is divided by \(z^2-2\) it gives a quotient of \(z ^ 2 - z + 1\) and remainder of \(3\) . text: Proofs by calculation are also well-suited to proving inequalities: that is, facts featuring an \(<\) , \(\le\) , \(>\) or \(\ge\) . Consider the following worked example:

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Let \(x\) and \(y\) be integers, and suppose that \(x + 3 \le 2\) and \(y + 2x\geq 3\) . Show that \(y>3\) .
html: Let \(x\) and \(y\) be integers, and suppose that \(x + 3 \le 2\) and \(y + 2x\geq 3\). Show that \(y>3\).

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: The goal was to show that \(y>3\) , and we established this by writing down a chain of inequalities, which starts with the expression \(y\) (top left) and ends with \(3\) (bottom right). The proof, implicitly, has five steps:
html: The goal was to show that \(y>3\), and we established this by writing down a chain of inequalities, which starts with the expression \(y\) (top left) and ends with \(3\) (bottom right). The proof, implicitly, has five steps:

text: 1. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }y=(y+2x)-2x}\) : algebraic rearrangement.
html: 1. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }y=(y+2x)-2x}\): algebraic rearrangement.

text: 2. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }(y+2x)-2x\geq 3-2x}\) : this uses the given fact that \(y + 2x\geq 3\) together with the general rule that an inequality is preserved under subtraction ( \(A\geq B\) implies \(A-C\geq B-C\) ).
html: 2. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }(y+2x)-2x\geq 3-2x}\): this uses the given fact that \(y + 2x\geq 3\) together with the general rule that an inequality is preserved under subtraction (\(A\geq B\) implies \(A-C\geq B-C\)).

text: 3. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }3-2x=9-2(x+3)}\) : algebraic rearrangement
html: 3. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }3-2x=9-2(x+3)}\): algebraic rearrangement

text: 4. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }9-2(x+3)\geq 9-2\cdot 2}\) : this uses the given fact that \(x + 3 \le 2\) together with the general rules that an inequality is preserved under multiplication by a positive number (if \(C\geq 0\) then \(A\geq B\) implies \(CA\geq CB\) ) and that an inequality is reversed when it is subtracted ( \(A\geq B\) implies \(C-B\geq C-A\) ).
html: 4. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }9-2(x+3)\geq 9-2\cdot 2}\): this uses the given fact that \(x + 3 \le 2\) together with the general rules that an inequality is preserved under multiplication by a positive number (if \(C\geq 0\) then \(A\geq B\) implies \(CA\geq CB\)) and that an inequality is reversed when it is subtracted (\(A\geq B\) implies \(C-B\geq C-A\)).

text: 5. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }9-2\cdot 2>3}\) : this is a numeric fact which can be proved by direct calculation and comparison.
html: 5. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }9-2\cdot 2>3}\): this is a numeric fact which can be proved by direct calculation and comparison.

text: Think carefully about steps 2 and 4. They look very similar to the steps which, in proofs-by-calculation of equalities, we have called “substitution steps” (in Lean, rw ). For example,
html: Think carefully about steps 2 and 4. They look very similar to the steps which, in proofs-by-calculation of equalities, we have called “substitution steps” (in Lean, rw). For example,

text: But they are not just straight substitutions; instead they are using all the rules mentioned about the preservation or reversal of inequalities under subtraction, multiplication, etc. There will be some situations when there is no relevant rule. For example, from \(x \le y\) you can in general conclude neither that \(\sin x \le \sin y\) nor that \(\sin x \ge \sin y\) .
html: But they are not just straight substitutions; instead they are using all the rules mentioned about the preservation or reversal of inequalities under subtraction, multiplication, etc. There will be some situations when there is no relevant rule. For example, from \(x \le y\) you can in general conclude neither that \(\sin x \le \sin y\) nor that \(\sin x \ge \sin y\).

text: Also look closely at the relation indicated in each step. The first step features an \(=\) , the second a \(\geq\) , the third a \(=\) , the fourth a \(\geq\) , and the last a \(>\) . In each case this reflects the particular reasoning used to establish that step. The final result is a \(>\) , since \(>\) takes precedence, so to speak, over \(\geq\) and \(=\) (and furthermore \(\geq\) takes precedence over \(=\) ). That is, if you know that \(A\geq B\) and \(B>C\) , then they combine transitively to a \(>\) relation: \(A>C\) .
html: Also look closely at the relation indicated in each step. The first step features an \(=\), the second a \(\geq\), the third a \(=\), the fourth a \(\geq\), and the last a \(>\). In each case this reflects the particular reasoning used to establish that step. The final result is a \(>\), since \(>\) takes precedence, so to speak, over \(\geq\) and \(=\) (and furthermore \(\geq\) takes precedence over \(=\)). That is, if you know that \(A\geq B\) and \(B>C\), then they combine transitively to a \(>\) relation: \(A>C\).

text: Here’s another worked example of a proof by calculation of an inequality.
html: Here’s another worked example of a proof by calculation of an inequality.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Let \(r\) and \(s\) be rational numbers, and suppose that \(s+3\geq r\) and \(s+r \leq 3\) . Show that \(r\leq 3\) .
html: Let \(r\) and \(s\) be rational numbers, and suppose that \(s+3\geq r\) and \(s+r \leq 3\). Show that \(r\leq 3\).

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: The goal was to show that \(r\leq 3\) , and the proof, implicitly, has three steps:
html: The goal was to show that \(r\leq 3\), and the proof, implicitly, has three steps:

text: 1. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }r=\frac{(s+r)+r-s}{2}}\) : algebraic rearrangement.
html: 1. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }r=\frac{(s+r)+r-s}{2}}\): algebraic rearrangement.

text: 2. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }\frac{(s+r)+r-s}{2}\leq \frac{3+(s+3)-s}{2}}\) : we are “substituting” the given facts \(s+3\geq r\) and \(s+r \leq 3\) , using rules about the preservation of inequalities under addition, and under division by positive numbers (and also, implicitly, the fact that 2 is positive).
html: 2. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }\frac{(s+r)+r-s}{2}\leq \frac{3+(s+3)-s}{2}}\): we are “substituting” the given facts \(s+3\geq r\) and \(s+r \leq 3\), using rules about the preservation of inequalities under addition, and under division by positive numbers (and also, implicitly, the fact that 2 is positive).

text: 3. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }\frac{3+(s+3)-s}{2}=3}\) : algebraic rearrangement.
html: 3. \(\underline{\text{Proof that }\frac{3+(s+3)-s}{2}=3}\): algebraic rearrangement.

text: This time the first step features the relation \(=\) , the second a \(\leq\) , and the third a \(=\) , and the “net result” is the relation \(\leq\) .
html: This time the first step features the relation \(=\), the second a \(\leq\), and the third a \(=\), and the “net result” is the relation \(\leq\).

text: In the following problem, note the use of shorthands such as \(0<A\leq 1\) and \(x,y\leq B\) to express several related inequalities concisely. You can look at the Lean statement which follows to see what these shorthands mean more explicitly.
html: In the following problem, note the use of shorthands such as \(0<A\leq 1\) and \(x,y\leq B\) to express several related inequalities concisely. You can look at the Lean statement which follows to see what these shorthands mean more explicitly.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Let \(u, v, x, y, A\) and \(B\) be real numbers. Suppose we know that \(0<A\leq 1, B\geq 1, x,y\leq B, 0\leq u<A\) and \(0\leq v<A\) . Show that \(uy+vx+uv<3AB\) .
html: Let \(u, v, x, y, A\) and \(B\) be real numbers. Suppose we know that \(0<A\leq 1, B\geq 1, x,y\leq B, 0\leq u<A\) and \(0\leq v<A\). Show that \(uy+vx+uv<3AB\).

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: In this calculation, the rules for preservation of inequalities under multiplication are used repeatedly. For example, in the first step, \(uy+vx+uv\leq uB+vB+uv\) , the given facts that \(x\leq B\) and \(y\leq B\) are used, together with the rule that inequalities \(≤\) are preserved under multiplication with a nonnegative constant (here that constant is \(u\) ). In the second step, \(uB+vB+uv\leq AB+AB+Av\) , the given facts \(u< A\) and \(v< A\) are multiplied by nonnegative constants \(B\) and \(v\) .
html: In this calculation, the rules for preservation of inequalities under multiplication are used repeatedly. For example, in the first step, \(uy+vx+uv\leq uB+vB+uv\), the given facts that \(x\leq B\) and \(y\leq B\) are used, together with the rule that inequalities \(≤\) are preserved under multiplication with a nonnegative constant (here that constant is \(u\)). In the second step, \(uB+vB+uv\leq AB+AB+Av\), the given facts \(u< A\) and \(v< A\) are multiplied by nonnegative constants \(B\) and \(v\).

text: Importantly, it is generally acceptable to omit a formal proof of the nonnegativity of the constants which you are multiplying by, if that proof is “obvious”. For example, in this case the nonnegativity of \(B\) comes from the given hypothesis that \(B\geq 1\) . The Lean tactic rel is also set up to infer these “obvious” proofs of positivity and nonnegativity.
html: Importantly, it is generally acceptable to omit a formal proof of the nonnegativity of the constants which you are multiplying by, if that proof is “obvious”. For example, in this case the nonnegativity of \(B\) comes from the given hypothesis that \(B\geq 1\). The Lean tactic rel is also set up to infer these “obvious” proofs of positivity and nonnegativity.

text: Here is an example with a small subtlety.
html: Here is an example with a small subtlety.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Show that if \(t\) is a real number and \(t\geq 10\) then \(t^2-3t+17\geq 5\) .
html: Show that if \(t\) is a real number and \(t\geq 10\) then \(t^2-3t+17\geq 5\).

text: On seeing the hypothesis \(t\geq 10\) , you might be tempted to “substitute” it directly into the expression \(t^2-3t+17\) .
html: On seeing the hypothesis \(t\geq 10\), you might be tempted to “substitute” it directly into the expression \(t^2-3t+17\).

text: This isn’t a valid solution! From \(t\geq 10\) we can deduce \(t^2\geq 10^2\) (there is a general rule that squaring preserves an inequality of nonnegative numbers), and that \(3t\geq 3\cdot 10\) (by multiplying the inequality by a nonnegative constant). But the second inequality would be reversed under negation, \(-3t\leq -3\cdot 10\) , so we are not able to make a determination about the relative sizes of \(t^2-3t+17\) and \(10^2-3\cdot 10+17\) .
html: This isn’t a valid solution! From \(t\geq 10\) we can deduce \(t^2\geq 10^2\) (there is a general rule that squaring preserves an inequality of nonnegative numbers), and that \(3t\geq 3\cdot 10\) (by multiplying the inequality by a nonnegative constant). But the second inequality would be reversed under negation, \(-3t\leq -3\cdot 10\), so we are not able to make a determination about the relative sizes of \(t^2-3t+17\) and \(10^2-3\cdot 10+17\).

text: Here is a valid solution to this problem. Instead of dropping down immediately from \(t^2\) to \(10^2\) , we go down “halfway”, to \(10t\) . This can then counteract the \(-3t\) term, combining with it to give \(7t\) , which has a positive rather than negative coefficient, allowing for a valid further substitution of the given fact \(t\geq 10\) .
html: Here is a valid solution to this problem. Instead of dropping down immediately from \(t^2\) to \(10^2\), we go down “halfway”, to \(10t\). This can then counteract the \(-3t\) term, combining with it to give \(7t\), which has a positive rather than negative coefficient, allowing for a valid further substitution of the given fact \(t\geq 10\).

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: Problem

text: Let \(m\) and \(n\) be integers, and suppose that \(m ^ 2 + n \le 2\) . Show that \(n \le 2\) .
html: Let \(m\) and \(n\) be integers, and suppose that \(m ^ 2 + n \le 2\). Show that \(n \le 2\).

text: We solve this problem by seeing that squares are positive, so \(n\) must be smaller than \(m ^ 2 + n\) . (Pedantically, “smaller than or equal to”.) So since we have an upper bound, 2, for \(m ^ 2 + n\) , this upper bound must also apply to the smaller number, \(n\) .
html: We solve this problem by seeing that squares are positive, so \(n\) must be smaller than \(m ^ 2 + n\). (Pedantically, “smaller than or equal to”.) So since we have an upper bound, 2, for \(m ^ 2 + n\), this upper bound must also apply to the smaller number, \(n\).

text: The proofs by calculation of Chapter 1 were all, from a certain point of view, one-step proofs. In this chapter we gradually introduce the ingredients for multi-step proofs. These include establishing “intermediate” facts which get referred back to later, invoking named lemmas previously proved by yourself or other people, and taking apart complicated mathematical statements which have been built up from simpler ones using the logical symbols \(\lor\) , \(\land\) and \(\exists\) .
html: The proofs by calculation of Chapter 1 were all, from a certain point of view, one-step proofs. In this chapter we gradually introduce the ingredients for multi-step proofs. These include establishing “intermediate” facts which get referred back to later, invoking named lemmas previously proved by yourself or other people, and taking apart complicated mathematical statements which have been built up from simpler ones using the logical symbols \(\lor\), \(\land\) and \(\exists\).

text: This chapter also introduces the key interactivity feature of the Lean language: the live-updating infoview keeping track of your current hypotheses and goals.
html: This chapter also introduces the key interactivity feature of the Lean language: the live-updating infoview keeping track of your current hypotheses and goals.

text: The work of this chapter continues (after a break) in Chapter 4 .
html: The work of this chapter continues (after a break) in Chapter 4.

text: Every proof we’ve seen so far has been a single calculation. More typically, though, a proof will have a more complex structure, with facts established at an early stage which are not used immediately, but instead brought in later.
html: Every proof we’ve seen so far has been a single calculation. More typically, though, a proof will have a more complex structure, with facts established at an early stage which are not used immediately, but instead brought in later.

text: For example, here’s the algebra problem from Example 1.3.3 .
html: For example, here’s the algebra problem from Example 1.3.3.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be real numbers and suppose that \(a-5b=4\) and \(b+2=3\) . Show that \(a=9\) .
html: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be real numbers and suppose that \(a-5b=4\) and \(b+2=3\). Show that \(a=9\).

text: We previously solved it by a single long calculation,
html: We previously solved it by a single long calculation,

text: But another way to express the solution – maybe more natural, and closer to how you might have learned to solve it in high school – is to first solve for \(b\) , and then substitute that in to help solve for \(a\) .
html: But another way to express the solution – maybe more natural, and closer to how you might have learned to solve it in high school – is to first solve for \(b\), and then substitute that in to help solve for \(a\).

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: Since \(b+2=3\) , we have \(b=1\) . Therefore
html: Since \(b+2=3\), we have \(b=1\). Therefore

text: This is the first time we have seen a proof with words. The sentence
html: This is the first time we have seen a proof with words. The sentence

text: Since \(b+2=3\) , we have \(b=1\) .
html: Since \(b+2=3\), we have \(b=1\).

text: is a use of the reasoning discussed in Section 1.5 : The fact \(b=1\) is deduced from the hypothesis \(b+2=3\) by mentally subtracting 2 from both sides, and this is considered sufficiently obvious that we don’t explain the reasoning apart from indicating which of the hypotheses is being invoked.
html: is a use of the reasoning discussed in Section 1.5: The fact \(b=1\) is deduced from the hypothesis \(b+2=3\) by mentally subtracting 2 from both sides, and this is considered sufficiently obvious that we don’t explain the reasoning apart from indicating which of the hypotheses is being invoked.

text: We have now added one more fact, \(b=1\) , to our list of known facts in this problem, and we carry out a calculational proof in which this fact is used (it is substituted, together with \(a-5b=4\) , at the step \((a - 5b) + 5b = 4 + 5 \cdot 1\) ). The word “Therefore” (synonyms: “Thus”, “So”) introduces this calculational proof; its meaning is that the fact just proved will be used in the reasoning which follows.
html: We have now added one more fact, \(b=1\), to our list of known facts in this problem, and we carry out a calculational proof in which this fact is used (it is substituted, together with \(a-5b=4\), at the step \((a - 5b) + 5b = 4 + 5 \cdot 1\)). The word “Therefore” (synonyms: “Thus”, “So”) introduces this calculational proof; its meaning is that the fact just proved will be used in the reasoning which follows.

text: Here is how this argument looks in Lean. We state \(b=1\) with the keyword have hb . This is immediately followed by the reasoning to justify this fact: adding/subtracting something from the hypothesis \(b+2=3\) , which in this problem is named h2 . Then we give a calculational proof as usual, in which the fact \(b=1\) (under the name hb ) gets used at some point.
html: Here is how this argument looks in Lean. We state \(b=1\) with the keyword have hb. This is immediately followed by the reasoning to justify this fact: adding/subtracting something from the hypothesis \(b+2=3\), which in this problem is named h2. Then we give a calculational proof as usual, in which the fact \(b=1\) (under the name hb) gets used at some point.

text: It’s not so hard to understand this proof step by step by reading the Lean code. But Lean actually provides a powerful tool to help us understand multi-step proofs: the Lean Infoview window, which we will now use for the first time. Let’s walk through what the infoview can tell us as we work through this problem.
html: It’s not so hard to understand this proof step by step by reading the Lean code. But Lean actually provides a powerful tool to help us understand multi-step proofs: the Lean Infoview window, which we will now use for the first time. Let’s walk through what the infoview can tell us as we work through this problem.

text: Let’s redo another example, this time Example 1.4.2 . The problem was:
html: Let’s redo another example, this time Example 1.4.2. The problem was:

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Let \(r\) and \(s\) be rational numbers, and suppose that \(s+3\geq r\) and \(s+r \leq 3\) . Show that \(r\leq 3\) .
html: Let \(r\) and \(s\) be rational numbers, and suppose that \(s+3\geq r\) and \(s+r \leq 3\). Show that \(r\leq 3\).

text: We did it before by a single, clever, calculation, but the following solution, though longer, might be easier to come up with.
html: We did it before by a single, clever, calculation, but the following solution, though longer, might be easier to come up with.

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: From \(s + 3 \geq r\) we have \(r \leq 3 + s\) , and from \(s + r \leq 3\) we have \(r \leq 3 - s\) . Therefore
html: From \(s + 3 \geq r\) we have \(r \leq 3 + s\), and from \(s + r \leq 3\) we have \(r \leq 3 - s\). Therefore

text: There are two intermediate steps in this problem: proving that \(r \leq 3 + s\) and that \(r \leq 3 - s\) .
html: There are two intermediate steps in this problem: proving that \(r \leq 3 + s\) and that \(r \leq 3 - s\).

text: We have that

text: so \(t=3\) . Thus \(t\geq 2\) .

text: The first step of this proof is a calculation establishing the intermediate statement \(t\cdot t=3t\) . Then, with the phrase
html: The first step of this proof is a calculation establishing the intermediate statement \(t\cdot t=3t\). Then, with the phrase
so \(t=3\)

text: we establish another intermediate statement, \(t=3\) , by cancelling \(t\) from the left and right sides of \(t\cdot t=3t\) . Finally, we deduce the goal, \(t=3\) .
html: we establish another intermediate statement, \(t=3\), by cancelling \(t\) from the left and right sides of \(t\cdot t=3t\). Finally, we deduce the goal, \(t=3\).

text: In Lean, cancellation reasoning is done with the cancel tactic. In the proof below, you will see that before the line cancel t at h3 the goal state contains the hypothesis
html: In Lean, cancellation reasoning is done with the cancel tactic. In the proof below, you will see that before the line cancel t at h3 the goal state contains the hypothesis

text: There is a mathematical subtlety here. You can only cancel a common factor from both sides of an equation if that common factor is known to be nonzero. In this problem, Lean can deduce that the common factor, t , is nonzero. Why?
html: There is a mathematical subtlety here. You can only cancel a common factor from both sides of an equation if that common factor is known to be nonzero. In this problem, Lean can deduce that the common factor, t, is nonzero. Why?

text: We have that

so \(b=0\).

text: This section covers the existential quantifier , the logical concept expressed in English as

text: Show that there exists an integer \(n\) , such that \(12n=84\) .

text: The integer \(7\) has this property. Indeed, \(12 \cdot 7=84\) .

text: I remember once going to see him [Ramanujan] when he was lying ill at Putney. I had ridden in taxi cab number 1729 and remarked that the number seemed to me rather a dull one, and that I hoped it was not an unfavorable omen. “No,” he replied, “it is a very interesting number; it is the smallest number expressible as the sum of two cubes in two different ways.”
html: I remember once going to see him [Ramanujan] when he was lying ill at Putney. I had ridden in taxi cab number 1729 and remarked that the number seemed to me rather a dull one, and that I hoped it was not an unfavorable omen. “No,” he replied, “it is a very interesting number; it is the smallest number expressible as the sum of two cubes in two different ways.”

text: —G. H. Hardy, Ramanujan: Twelve lectures on subjects suggested by his life and work
html: —G. H. Hardy, Ramanujan: Twelve lectures on subjects suggested by his life and work

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Show that there exist natural numbers \(a\) , \(b\) , \(c\) and \(d\) , such that \(a^3+b^3=1729=c^3+d^3\) , but \(a\ne c\) and \(a\ne d\) . 3
html: Show that there exist natural numbers \(a\), \(b\), \(c\) and \(d\), such that \(a^3+b^3=1729=c^3+d^3\), but \(a\ne c\) and \(a\ne d\). 3

text: Solution
html: Solution
\(1^3+12^3=1729=9^3+10^3\), but \(1\ne 9\) and \(1\ne 10\).

text: Put your cursor at the start of the line have hb : b = 1 and look at the Lean Infoview window. We see this: a b : ℝ h1 : a - 5 * b = 4 h2 : b + 2 = 3 ⊢ a = 9 This is simply a vertically-displayed version of the problem we started with: example { a b : ℝ } ( h1 : a - 5 * b = 4 ) ( h2 : b + 2 = 3 ) : a = 9 := It lists all the variables and hypotheses given to us in the problem, and, next to the symbol ⊢ , it displays our goal : to show a = 9 .
html:
• Put your cursor at the start of the line

    have hb : b = 1

    and look at the Lean Infoview window. We see this:

    a b : ℝ
h1 : a - 5 * b = 4
h2 : b + 2 = 3
⊢ a = 9

    This is simply a vertically-displayed version of the problem we started with:

    example {a b : ℝ} (h1 : a - 5 * b = 4) (h2 : b + 2 = 3) :
  a = 9 :=

    It lists all the variables and hypotheses given to us in the problem, and, next to the symbol ⊢, it displays our goal: to show a = 9.

text: Next, move your cursor to the start of the lines calc a = ( a - 5 * b ) + 5 * b := by ring The newly-proved fact hb , that b = 1 , has been added to the list of hypotheses in the infoview. a b : ℝ h1 : a - 5 * b = 4 h2 : b + 2 = 3 hb : b = 1 ⊢ a = 9
html:
• Next, move your cursor to the start of the lines

    calc
  a = (a - 5 * b) + 5 * b := by ring

    The newly-proved fact hb, that b = 1, has been added to the list of hypotheses in the infoview.

    a b : ℝ
h1 : a - 5 * b = 4
h2 : b + 2 = 3
hb : b = 1
⊢ a = 9

text: Finally, move your cursor to the start of the line below the last line of code, _ = 9 := by ring The infoview now displays no tasks for us. Instead it displays the message No goals which serves as a visual confirmation that the calc block solved the goal, thus completing the problem.

text: Let \(x\) be a rational number whose square is 4, and which is greater than 1. Show that \(x=2\) . Suggested steps: Prove that \(x(x+2)=2(x+2)\) , then cancel to deduce that \(x=2\) . example { x : ℚ } ( h1 : x ^ 2 = 4 ) ( h2 : 1 < x ) : x = 2 := by sorry

text: Let \(n\) be an integer satisfying \(n^2+4=4n\) . Prove that \(n=2\) . Suggested steps: Prove that \((n-2)^2=0\) , cancel the square to deduce that \(n-2=0\) , then finish off. example { n : ℤ } ( hn : n ^ 2 + 4 = 4 * n ) : n = 2 := by sorry

text: Let \(a\) , \(b\) and \(c\) be real numbers, and suppose that \(a\le b+c\) , \(b\le a+c\) and \(c\le a+b\) . Show that there exist nonnegative real numbers \(x\) , \(y\) and \(z\) such that \(a=y+z\) , \(b=x+z\) and \(c=x+y\) . example { a b c : ℝ } ( ha : a ≤ b + c ) ( hb : b ≤ a + c ) ( hc : c ≤ a + b ) : ∃ x y z , x ≥ 0 ∧ y ≥ 0 ∧ z ≥ 0 ∧ a = y + z ∧ b = x + z ∧ c = x + y := by sorry
html:
• Let \(a\), \(b\) and \(c\) be real numbers, and suppose that \(a\le b+c\), \(b\le a+c\) and \(c\le a+b\). Show that there exist nonnegative real numbers \(x\), \(y\) and \(z\) such that \(a=y+z\), \(b=x+z\) and \(c=x+y\).

    example {a b c : ℝ} (ha : a ≤ b + c) (hb : b ≤ a + c) (hc : c ≤ a + b) :
    ∃ x y z, x ≥ 0 ∧ y ≥ 0 ∧ z ≥ 0 ∧ a = y + z ∧ b = x + z ∧ c = x + y := by
  sorry

text: The problems in this chapter concern some elementary properties of natural numbers and integers: parity (whether a number is even or odd ), divisibility , and congruence modulo \(n\) .
html: The problems in this chapter concern some elementary properties of natural numbers and integers: parity (whether a number is even or odd), divisibility, and congruence modulo \(n\).

text: There are no new logical symbols like \(\lor\) or \(\exists\) in this chapter, and no major additions (such as the use of intermediate steps or lemmas) to our reasoning toolkit. Thus this chapter functions as a breather between the hard work of Chapter 2 and Chapter 4 , a chance to consolidate what you have learned.
html: There are no new logical symbols like \(\lor\) or \(\exists\) in this chapter, and no major additions (such as the use of intermediate steps or lemmas) to our reasoning toolkit. Thus this chapter functions as a breather between the hard work of Chapter 2 and Chapter 4, a chance to consolidate what you have learned.

text: When a mathematical term is introduced for the first time, it is given a definition.
html: When a mathematical term is introduced for the first time, it is given a definition.

text: Definition
html: Definition

text: An integer \(a\) is odd , if there exists an integer \(k\) , such that \(a=2k+1\) .
html: An integer \(a\) is odd, if there exists an integer \(k\), such that \(a=2k+1\).

text: Here’s how we make a definition in Lean.
html: Here’s how we make a definition in Lean.

text: On paper, you need to memorize every definition; this is crucial to be able to work with them. In Lean, when you see a term whose definition you’d like to double-check, you can right-click (Windows) / two-finger-click (Mac) to bring up an option “Go to Definition”. This will send you to the place in the Lean code where the definition was introduced. (The keyboard shortcut Ctrl - - will bring you back from that definition to where you were working before.)
html: On paper, you need to memorize every definition; this is crucial to be able to work with them. In Lean, when you see a term whose definition you’d like to double-check, you can right-click (Windows) / two-finger-click (Mac) to bring up an option “Go to Definition”. This will send you to the place in the Lean code where the definition was introduced. (The keyboard shortcut Ctrl-- will bring you back from that definition to where you were working before.)

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Show that \(7\) is odd.
html: Show that \(7\) is odd.

text: Solution
html: Solution
\(7=2\cdot 3+1\), so \(7\) is odd.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Show that \(-3\) is odd.
html: Show that \(-3\) is odd.

text: You should show that \(-3=2k+1\) for some integer \(k\) . But what is the \(k\) that works?
html: You should show that \(-3=2k+1\) for some integer \(k\). But what is the \(k\) that works?

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Prove that if \(n\) is an odd integer, then \(3n+2\) is odd.
html: Prove that if \(n\) is an odd integer, then \(3n+2\) is odd.

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: Let n be odd. Then there exists an integer \(k\) such that \(n = 2k+1\) . Thus
html: Let n be odd. Then there exists an integer \(k\) such that \(n = 2k+1\). Thus

text: so \(3n+2\) is odd.
html: so \(3n+2\) is odd.

text: This is our first encounter with something which might be confusing. The variable inside an existential is a “throwaway” variable, which exists only for the duration of the sentence. So the same variable might occur within the goal state repeatedly, without meaning the same thing. Here the k in the hypothesis hn is different from the k in the goal; it’s just the default name when the definition “odd” is unfolded.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Let \(n\) be an integer. Prove that if \(n\) is odd, then \(7n-4\) is odd.
html: Let \(n\) be an integer. Prove that if \(n\) is odd, then \(7n-4\) is odd.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Prove that if the integers \(x\) and \(y\) are odd, then \(x+y+1\) is odd.
html: Prove that if the integers \(x\) and \(y\) are odd, then \(x+y+1\) is odd.

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: Since \(x\) and \(y\) are odd, there exists an integer \(a\) such that \(x=2a+1\) , and there exists an integer \(b\) such that \(y=2b+1\) . Then
html: Since \(x\) and \(y\) are odd, there exists an integer \(a\) such that \(x=2a+1\), and there exists an integer \(b\) such that \(y=2b+1\). Then

text: so \(x+y+1\) is odd.
html: so \(x+y+1\) is odd.

html: Problem

text: Prove that if the integers \(x\) and \(y\) are odd, then \(xy+2y\) is odd.

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: Since \(x\) and \(y\) are odd, there exists an integer \(a\) such that \(x=2a+1\) , and there exists an integer \(b\) such that \(y=2b+1\) . Then
html: Since \(x\) and \(y\) are odd, there exists an integer \(a\) such that \(x=2a+1\), and there exists an integer \(b\) such that \(y=2b+1\). Then

text: so \(xy+2y\) is odd.

text: You can probably guess the definition of “even”.
html: You can probably guess the definition of “even”.

text: Definition
html: Definition

text: An integer \(a\) is even , if there exists an integer \(k\) , such that \(a=2k\) .
html: An integer \(a\) is even, if there exists an integer \(k\), such that \(a=2k\).

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Let \(m\) be an integer. Prove that if \(m\) is odd, then \(3m-5\) is even.
html: Let \(m\) be an integer. Prove that if \(m\) is odd, then \(3m-5\) is even.

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: Since m is odd, there exists an integer \(t\) such that \(m = 2t+1\) . Thus
html: Since m is odd, there exists an integer \(t\) such that \(m = 2t+1\). Thus

text: so \(3m-5\) is even.
html: so \(3m-5\) is even.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Let \(n\) be an even integer. Prove that \(n ^ 2 + 2 n - 5\) is odd.
html: Let \(n\) be an even integer. Prove that \(n ^ 2 + 2 n - 5\) is odd.

text: In fact every integer is either even or odd; we will discuss how to prove this later, in Example 4.2.9 .
html: In fact every integer is either even or odd; we will discuss how to prove this later, in Example 4.2.9.

text: In Lean this fact can be invoked as the lemma Int.even_or_odd :
html: In Lean this fact can be invoked as the lemma Int.even_or_odd:

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Let \(n\) be an integer. Prove that \(n ^ 2 + n + 4\) is even.
html: Let \(n\) be an integer. Prove that \(n ^ 2 + n + 4\) is even.

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: We consider two cases, depending on whether \(n\) is even or odd.
html: We consider two cases, depending on whether \(n\) is even or odd.

text: If \(n\) is even, then there exists an integer \(x\) such that \(n=2x\) . Then
html: If \(n\) is even, then there exists an integer \(x\) such that \(n=2x\). Then

text: so \(n ^ 2 + n + 4\) is even.
html: so \(n ^ 2 + n + 4\) is even.

text: If \(n\) is odd, then there exists an integer \(x\) such that \(n=2x+1\) . Then
html: If \(n\) is odd, then there exists an integer \(x\) such that \(n=2x+1\). Then

text: so \(n ^ 2 + n + 4\) is again even.
html: so \(n ^ 2 + n + 4\) is again even.

text: Another mathematical definition you may have seen before is the definition of divisibility.
html: Another mathematical definition you may have seen before is the definition of divisibility.

text: Definition
html: Definition

text: A natural number \(b\) is divisible by another natural number \(a\) , if there exists a natural number \(c\) , such that \(b=ac\) .
html: A natural number \(b\) is divisible by another natural number \(a\), if there exists a natural number \(c\), such that \(b=ac\).

text: For example,
html: For example,

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Show that the natural number 88 is divisible by 11.
html: Show that the natural number 88 is divisible by 11.

text: Solution
html: Solution
\(88 = 11 \cdot 8\).

text: Divisibility is a very important concept, and there are several different names for it. All the following mean the same thing:
html: Divisibility is a very important concept, and there are several different names for it. All the following mean the same thing:

text: We will most frequently use the last of these, “ \(a\) divides \(b\) ,” since it’s the most compact. There is also a standard notation, \(a \mid b\) , which we will also use frequently.
html: We will most frequently use the last of these, “\(a\) divides \(b\),” since it’s the most compact. There is also a standard notation, \(a \mid b\), which we will also use frequently.

text: In Lean, the definition of divisibility is already in the library, together with many theorems about it. We will typically work with this definition in Lean using the notation, ∣ , which you can type in Lean as \| or \mid . (In general, hover over a symbol in VSCode with your mouse to find out how to type it.) Warning: this is different from the visually similar symbol | which appears on your keyboard and which we use in obtain statements.
html: In Lean, the definition of divisibility is already in the library, together with many theorems about it. We will typically work with this definition in Lean using the notation, ∣, which you can type in Lean as \| or \mid. (In general, hover over a symbol in VSCode with your mouse to find out how to type it.) Warning: this is different from the visually similar symbol | which appears on your keyboard and which we use in obtain statements.

text: As in Section 3.1 with dsimp [Odd] , it is possible to unfold the definition of divisibility in the middle of a Lean proof to remind yourself of what it means in the current context. You can write either dsimp [Dvd.dvd] (this being the text name of the Lean notation \(\mid\) for divisibility) or dsimp [(· ∣ ·)] – unfortunately dsimp [∣] without the dots and parentheses does not work. As in the Section 3.1 examples, this unfolding is optional – the proof still works without it.

You can write either dsimp [Dvd.dvd] (this being the text name of the Lean notation \(\mid\) for divisibility) or dsimp [(· ∣ ·)] – unfortunately dsimp [∣] without the dots and parentheses does not work. As in the Section 3.1 examples, this unfolding is optional – the proof still works without it.

text: Another feature of this definition is that, although we stated it above for natural numbers, we will also often want to consider divisibility of integers. Here is the corresponding definition for integers:
html: Another feature of this definition is that, although we stated it above for natural numbers, we will also often want to consider divisibility of integers. Here is the corresponding definition for integers:

text: Definition
html: Definition

text: An integer \(b\) is divisible by another integer \(a\) , if there exists an integer \(c\) , such that \(b=ac\) .
html: An integer \(b\) is divisible by another integer \(a\), if there exists an integer \(c\), such that \(b=ac\).

text: We also use all the same variant terminology and the same notation \(a \mid b\) for integer divisibility.
html: We also use all the same variant terminology and the same notation \(a \mid b\) for integer divisibility.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Show that the integer 6 is divisible by -2.
html: Show that the integer 6 is divisible by -2.

text: Solution
html: Solution
\(6 = -2 \cdot -3\).

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be integers and suppose that \(a \mid b\) . Show that \(a \mid b^2+2b\) .
html: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be integers and suppose that \(a \mid b\). Show that \(a \mid b^2+2b\).

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: Since \(a \mid b\) , there exists an integer \(k\) such that \(b=ak\) . Then
html: Since \(a \mid b\), there exists an integer \(k\) such that \(b=ak\). Then

text: So \(a \mid b^2+2b\) .
html: So \(a \mid b^2+2b\).

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Let \(a\) , \(b\) and \(c\) be natural numbers and suppose that \(a \mid b\) and \(b ^2\mid c\) . Show that \(a^2 \mid c\) .
html: Let \(a\), \(b\) and \(c\) be natural numbers and suppose that \(a \mid b\) and \(b ^2\mid c\). Show that \(a^2 \mid c\).

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: Since \(a \mid b\) , there exists a natural number \(x\) such that \(b=ax\) . Since \(b^2 \mid c\) , there exists a natural number \(y\) such that \(c=b^2y\) . Then
html: Since \(a \mid b\), there exists a natural number \(x\) such that \(b=ax\). Since \(b^2 \mid c\), there exists a natural number \(y\) such that \(c=b^2y\). Then

text: So \(a ^2 \mid c\) .
html: So \(a ^2 \mid c\).

text: Problem

text: Let \(x\) , \(y\) and \(z\) be natural numbers and suppose that \(xy \mid z\) . Show that \(x \mid z\) .

html: Solution

text: You might wonder how to show that a number is not divisible by another number. A convenient test here is a theorem which we will prove later in the book, in Example 4.5.8 : if an integer \(a\) is strictly between two consecutive multiples of an integer \(b\) , then it is not a multiple of \(b\) . More formally, if there exists an integer \(q\) such that \(bq<a<b(q + 1)\) , then \(a\) is not a multiple of \(b\) . Here is an example applying this test:

A convenient test here is a theorem which we will prove later in the book, in Example 4.5.8: if an integer \(a\) is strictly between two consecutive multiples of an integer \(b\), then it is not a multiple of \(b\). More formally, if there exists an integer \(q\) such that \(bq<a<b(q + 1)\), then \(a\) is not a multiple of \(b\). Here is an example applying this test:

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Show that 12 is not divisible by 5.
html: Show that 12 is not divisible by 5.

text: Solution
html: Solution
\(5 \cdot 2 < 12 < 5 \cdot (2 + 1)\).

text: Definition
html: Definition

text: The integers \(a\) and \(b\) are congruent modulo \(n\) , if \(n\mid (a - b)\) .
html: The integers \(a\) and \(b\) are congruent modulo \(n\), if \(n\mid (a - b)\).

text: We use the notation \(a\equiv b \mod n\) to denote that \(a\) and \(b\) are congruent modulo \(n\) .
html: We use the notation \(a\equiv b \mod n\) to denote that \(a\) and \(b\) are congruent modulo \(n\).

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Show that \(11\equiv 3 \mod 4\) .
html: Show that \(11\equiv 3 \mod 4\).

text: Solution
html: Solution
\(11-3=4\cdot 2\), so \(4\mid(11-3)\).

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Show that \(-5\equiv 1 \mod 3\) .
html: Show that \(-5\equiv 1 \mod 3\).

text: Solution
html: Solution
\(-5-1=3\cdot -2\), so \(3\mid(-5-1)\).

text: Lemma (addition rule for modular arithmetic)
html: Lemma (addition rule for modular arithmetic)

text: Let \(a\) , \(b\) , \(c\) , \(d\) and \(n\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv b \mod n\) and \(c\equiv d \mod n\) . Then \(a+c\equiv b+d \mod n\) .
html: Let \(a\), \(b\), \(c\), \(d\) and \(n\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv b \mod n\) and \(c\equiv d \mod n\). Then \(a+c\equiv b+d \mod n\).

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: Since \(a\equiv b \mod n\) , there exists an integer \(x\) such that \(a-b=nx\) . Since \(c\equiv d \mod n\) , there exists an integer \(y\) such that \(c-d=ny\) . Then
html: Since \(a\equiv b \mod n\), there exists an integer \(x\) such that \(a-b=nx\). Since \(c\equiv d \mod n\), there exists an integer \(y\) such that \(c-d=ny\). Then

text: so \(a+c\equiv b+d \mod n\) .
html: so \(a+c\equiv b+d \mod n\).

text: Lemma (subtraction rule for modular arithmetic)
html: Lemma (subtraction rule for modular arithmetic)

text: Let \(a\) , \(b\) , \(c\) , \(d\) and \(n\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv b \mod n\) and \(c\equiv d \mod n\) . Then \(a-c\equiv b-d \mod n\) .
html: Let \(a\), \(b\), \(c\), \(d\) and \(n\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv b \mod n\) and \(c\equiv d \mod n\). Then \(a-c\equiv b-d \mod n\).

text: Lemma (negation rule for modular arithmetic)
html: Lemma (negation rule for modular arithmetic)

text: Let \(a\) , \(b\) and \(n\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv b \mod n\) . Then \(-a\equiv -b \mod n\) .
html: Let \(a\), \(b\) and \(n\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv b \mod n\). Then \(-a\equiv -b \mod n\).

text: Lemma (multiplication rule for modular arithmetic)
html: Lemma (multiplication rule for modular arithmetic)

text: Let \(a\) , \(b\) , \(c\) , \(d\) and \(n\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv b \mod n\) and \(c\equiv d \mod n\) . Then \(ac\equiv bd \mod n\) .
html: Let \(a\), \(b\), \(c\), \(d\) and \(n\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv b \mod n\) and \(c\equiv d \mod n\). Then \(ac\equiv bd \mod n\).

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: Since \(a\equiv b \mod n\) , there exists an integer \(x\) such that \(a-b=nx\) . Since \(c\equiv d \mod n\) , there exists an integer \(y\) such that \(c-d=ny\) . Then
html: Since \(a\equiv b \mod n\), there exists an integer \(x\) such that \(a-b=nx\). Since \(c\equiv d \mod n\), there exists an integer \(y\) such that \(c-d=ny\). Then

text: so \(ac\equiv bd \mod n\) .
html: so \(ac\equiv bd \mod n\).

text: Warning: There is no “division rule” for modular arithmetic!
html: Warning: There is no “division rule” for modular arithmetic!

text: After solving this and the many similar problems in Section 3.3 , you probably feel like you can check the correctness of any statement of this style by sight. And that’s great! When we have built up enough theory that a particular class of statement can be checked by sight, we will stop requiring detailed proofs. So for now on we’ll take such statements for granted.

When we have built up enough theory that a particular class of statement can be checked by sight, we will stop requiring detailed proofs. So for now on we’ll take such statements for granted.

text: This is also usually the point at which it’s easy to write a Lean tactic which checks the correctness of a class of statement. I’ve done exactly this, updating tactic rel to cover this kind of statement. We won’t discuss tactic-writing in this book, but effectively, the tactic now throws the lemmas Int.ModEq.add , Int.ModEq.neg , Int.ModEq.sub , Int.ModEq.mul , Int.ModEq.pow , Int.ModEq.refl and the provided hypotheses at the statement until (a) the goal is solved or (b) none of these lemmas apply any more. And that’s exactly what we’re doing in our heads when we check the paper statement of the problem.
