text: In the course of Chapter 2 , we studied the logical symbols \(\lor\) , \(\land\) and \(\exists\) , which allow complicated mathematical statements to be built up from simpler ones. For each such symbol, we learned its “grammar”: the rule for using it when it appears in a hypothesis and the rule for using it when it appears in the goal. This grammar is called natural deduction .
html: In the course of Chapter 2, we studied the logical symbols \(\lor\), \(\land\) and \(\exists\), which allow complicated mathematical statements to be built up from simpler ones. For each such symbol, we learned its “grammar”: the rule for using it when it appears in a hypothesis and the rule for using it when it appears in the goal. This grammar is called natural deduction.

text: This chapter finishes the work started in Chapter 2 . We learn the grammar of the remaining logical symbols: \(\forall\) , \(\to\) , and \(\lnot\) . We also learn the grammar of two other logical symbols, \(\leftrightarrow\) and \(\exists!\) , which are less fundamental because they can be defined in terms of the other ones.
html: This chapter finishes the work started in Chapter 2. We learn the grammar of the remaining logical symbols: \(\forall\), \(\to\), and \(\lnot\). We also learn the grammar of two other logical symbols, \(\leftrightarrow\) and \(\exists!\), which are less fundamental because they can be defined in terms of the other ones.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Let \(a\) be a real number and suppose that for all real numbers \(x\) , it is true that \(a\le x^2-2x\) . Show that \(a\le -1\) .
html: Let \(a\) be a real number and suppose that for all real numbers \(x\), it is true that \(a\le x^2-2x\). Show that \(a\le -1\).

text: Fig. 4.1 The parabola \(y= x^2-2x\) .
html: Fig. 4.1 The parabola \(y= x^2-2x\).

text: Specifying that a formula or predicate is meant to be true for all values of a variable \(x\) , like we did with \(a\le x^2-2x\) in the above problem, is called universally quantifying over the variable \(x\) . It is expressed symbolically as ∀ .
html: Specifying that a formula or predicate is meant to be true for all values of a variable \(x\), like we did with \(a\le x^2-2x\) in the above problem, is called universally quantifying over the variable \(x\). It is expressed symbolically as ∀.

text: To use a hypothesis with a universal quantifier, you may want to “specialize” its use to one particular variable. For example, in the solution below, we use the special case of the hypothesis in which \(x\) is set to 1.
html: To use a hypothesis with a universal quantifier, you may want to “specialize” its use to one particular variable. For example, in the solution below, we use the special case of the hypothesis in which \(x\) is set to 1.

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: In Lean, perform this specialization using the apply tactic.
html: In Lean, perform this specialization using the apply tactic.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Let \(n\) be a natural number which is a factor of every natural number \(m\) . Show that \(n=1\) .
html: Let \(n\) be a natural number which is a factor of every natural number \(m\). Show that \(n=1\).

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: Since \(n\) is a factor of every natural number, it is a factor of \(1\) . Also notice that \(1\) is positive. So we can invoke the size bounds on factors discussed in Example 3.2.7 and Example 3.2.8 : \(n\le 1\) and \(1 \le n\) . Therefore \(n=1\) .
html: Since \(n\) is a factor of every natural number, it is a factor of \(1\). Also notice that \(1\) is positive. So we can invoke the size bounds on factors discussed in Example 3.2.7 and Example 3.2.8: \(n\le 1\) and \(1 \le n\). Therefore \(n=1\).

text: For the Lean proof, we recall that the bound from Example 3.2.7 is in Lean as Nat.le_of_dvd , and the bound from Example 3.2.8 is in Lean as Nat.pos_of_dvd_of_pos .
html: For the Lean proof, we recall that the bound from Example 3.2.7 is in Lean as Nat.le_of_dvd, and the bound from Example 3.2.8 is in Lean as Nat.pos_of_dvd_of_pos.

html: Problem

text: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be real numbers and suppose that every real number \(x\) is either at least \(a\) or at most \(b\) . Show that \(a \le b\) .
html: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be real numbers and suppose that every real number \(x\) is either at least \(a\) or at most \(b\). Show that \(a \le b\).

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: Consider the real number \(\frac {a+b}{2}\) . It is either at least \(a\) or at most \(b\) .
html: Consider the real number \(\frac {a+b}{2}\). It is either at least \(a\) or at most \(b\).

text: Case 1: \(\frac {a+b}{2} \geq a\) . Then
html: Case 1: \(\frac {a+b}{2} \geq a\). Then

text: Case 2: \(\frac {a+b}{2} \leq b\) . Then

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Let \(a\) be real number whose square is at most 2, and which is greater than or equal to any real number whose square is at most 2. 1 Let \(b\) be another real number with the same two properties. Prove that \(a=b\) .
html: Let \(a\) be real number whose square is at most 2, and which is greater than or equal to any real number whose square is at most 2. 1 Let \(b\) be another real number with the same two properties. Prove that \(a=b\).

text: Consider the hypothesis in this problem that
html: Consider the hypothesis in this problem that

text: \(a\) is greater than or equal to any real number whose square is at most 2.
html: \(a\) is greater than or equal to any real number whose square is at most 2.

text: Implicitly, there is a universal quantification (“any real number”), and also an implication , so a more pedantic version of this hypothesis would be
html: Implicitly, there is a universal quantification (“any real number”), and also an implication, so a more pedantic version of this hypothesis would be

text: for all real numbers \(y\) , if \(y^2\le 2\) then \(y\le a\) .
html: for all real numbers \(y\), if \(y^2\le 2\) then \(y\le a\).

text: We can specialize such a hypothesis to any particular choice of \(y\) for which the antecedent \(y^2\le 2\) is true.
html: We can specialize such a hypothesis to any particular choice of \(y\) for which the antecedent \(y^2\le 2\) is true.

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: Since \(a^2\le 2\) , and \(b\) is greater than or equal to any real number whose square is at most 2, \(a \le b\) .

text: Definition
html: Definition

text: A natural number \(p\) is prime , if it is at least \(2\) , and the only factors of \(p\) are \(1\) and \(p\) .
html: A natural number \(p\) is prime, if it is at least \(2\), and the only factors of \(p\) are \(1\) and \(p\).

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Show that 2 is prime.
html: Show that 2 is prime.

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: Clearly \(2 \le 2\) . Let \(m\) be a factor of \(2\) . Since \(2\) is positive, by the size bounds on factors discussed in Example 3.2.7 and Example 3.2.8 , we have that \(m \le 2\) and \(1 \le m\) . The only natural numbers \(m\) satisfying \(m \le 2\) and \(1 \le m\) are \(1\) and \(2\) , so as required \(m=1\) or \(m=2\) .

Since \(2\) is positive, by the size bounds on factors discussed in Example 3.2.7 and Example 3.2.8, we have that \(m \le 2\) and \(1 \le m\). The only natural numbers \(m\) satisfying \(m \le 2\) and \(1 \le m\) are \(1\) and \(2\), so as required \(m=1\) or \(m=2\).

text: One new technique used in this solution is to observe from numeric bounds on a natural number (like \(m \le 2\) and \(1 \le m\) here) that there are finitely many possibilities (here, \(m=1\) or \(m=2\) .) In Lean, we use the tactic interval_cases for this kind of argument. It also works for integers, but it doesn’t work for rational numbers or real numbers – why?

In Lean, we use the tactic interval_cases for this kind of argument. It also works for integers, but it doesn’t work for rational numbers or real numbers – why?

text: Conversely, suppose that \(n\equiv 1\mod 2\) . Then \(2\mid n -1\) , so there exists an integer \(k\) such \(n-1=2k\) . Thus \(n=2k+1\) and so \(n\) is odd.
html: Conversely, suppose that \(n\equiv 1\mod 2\). Then \(2\mid n -1\), so there exists an integer \(k\) such \(n-1=2k\). Thus \(n=2k+1\) and so \(n\) is odd.

text: Theorem

text: Every integer is either even or odd.

text: Sometimes, we encounter a situation with two contradictory hypotheses. At this point, there is no need to prove anything more. Two contradictory hypotheses mean that the situation we had hypothesized can’t actually happen.
html: Sometimes, we encounter a situation with two contradictory hypotheses. At this point, there is no need to prove anything more. Two contradictory hypotheses mean that the situation we had hypothesized can’t actually happen.

text: It is quite common to encounter this when giving a proof by cases. You might reduce the problem to some list of cases, prove your goal in some of those cases, and prove that the other cases are impossible.
html: It is quite common to encounter this when giving a proof by cases. You might reduce the problem to some list of cases, prove your goal in some of those cases, and prove that the other cases are impossible.

text: We defined prime numbers in Example 4.1.8 , and proved that 2 was prime. Let’s now prove a slight reformulation of the definition, which will be convenient in showing that other numbers are prime.

text: Lemma
html: Lemma

text: Let \(p\) be a natural number greater than or equal to 2. Suppose that for all natural numbers \(m\) for which \(1<m<p\) , it is not true that \(m\) is a factor of \(p\) . Show that \(p\) is prime.
html: Let \(p\) be a natural number greater than or equal to 2. Suppose that for all natural numbers \(m\) for which \(1<m<p\), it is not true that \(m\) is a factor of \(p\). Show that \(p\) is prime.

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: Since we are given that \(2 \le p\) , what’s left is to prove the second part of the definition “prime”: let \(m\) be a factor of \(p\) ( \(\star\) ); we must show that \(m=1\) or \(m=p\) .
html: Since we are given that \(2 \le p\), what’s left is to prove the second part of the definition “prime”: let \(m\) be a factor of \(p\) (\(\star\)); we must show that \(m=1\) or \(m=p\).

text: Since \(m\) is a factor of \(p\) , we have that \(1 \le m\) . So either \(m=1\) or \(1<m\) ; we consider cases accordingly.
html: Since \(m\) is a factor of \(p\), we have that \(1 \le m\). So either \(m=1\) or \(1<m\); we consider cases accordingly.

text: Case 1 ( \(m=1\) ): This immediately gives our goal that \(m=1\) or \(m=p\) .
html: Case 1 (\(m=1\)): This immediately gives our goal that \(m=1\) or \(m=p\).

text: Case 2 ( \(1<m\) ): Since \(m\) is a factor of \(p\) , we have that \(m \le p\) . So either \(m=p\) or \(m<p\) ; we consider cases accordingly.
html: Case 2 (\(1<m\)): Since \(m\) is a factor of \(p\), we have that \(m \le p\). So either \(m=p\) or \(m<p\); we consider cases accordingly.

text: Case 2(i) ( \(m=p\) ): This immediately gives our goal that \(m=1\) or \(m=p\) .
html: Case 2(i) (\(m=p\)): This immediately gives our goal that \(m=1\) or \(m=p\).

text: Case 2(ii) ( \(m<p\) ): We have now established that \(1<m<p\) , and by one of the facts given in the problem statement, this means that \(m\) is not a factor of \(p\) . This contradicts the earlier statement ( \(\star\) ).
html: Case 2(ii) (\(m<p\)): We have now established that \(1<m<p\), and by one of the facts given in the problem statement, this means that \(m\) is not a factor of \(p\). This contradicts the earlier statement (\(\star\)).

text: I’ve filled in about half of this proof in Lean; fill in the rest, including the final contradiction. If you need to look up the Lean names for the size bounds on factors, they were proved in Example 3.2.7 and Example 3.2.8 .

text: Theorem

text: This is the lemma we have invoked in Lean as Int.not_dvd_of_exists_lt_and_lt .

html: Proof

text: Suppose for the sake of contradiction that \(a\) is a multiple of \(b\) . Then there exists an integer \(k\) such that \(a=bk\) . Also, let \(q\) be an integer such that \(b q<a<b(q + 1)\) .
html: Suppose for the sake of contradiction that \(a\) is a multiple of \(b\). Then there exists an integer \(k\) such that \(a=bk\). Also, let \(q\) be an integer such that \(b q<a<b(q + 1)\).

text: We first note that
html: We first note that

html: and so (since \(b>0\)) we have \(k < q+1\).

text: We next observe that
html: We next observe that

text: We also establish a test for primality which will be more efficient than the test from Example 4.4.4 .
html: We also establish a test for primality which will be more efficient than the test from Example 4.4.4.

text: Theorem
html: Theorem

text: Let \(p\) be a natural number which is at least 2. Let \(T\) be another natural number, whose square is greater than \(p\) , and suppose that every natural number \(m\) for which \(1<m<T\) is not a factor of \(p\) . Then \(p\) is prime.
html: Let \(p\) be a natural number which is at least 2. Let \(T\) be another natural number, whose square is greater than \(p\), and suppose that every natural number \(m\) for which \(1<m<T\) is not a factor of \(p\). Then \(p\) is prime.

text: (Notice that in the Example 4.4.4 test we had to check that every number up to \(p\) was not a factor of \(p\) ; with this test we only need to check the numbers up to approximately the square root of \(p\) .)
html: (Notice that in the Example 4.4.4 test we had to check that every number up to \(p\) was not a factor of \(p\); with this test we only need to check the numbers up to approximately the square root of \(p\).)

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: By the prime test from Example 4.4.4 , it suffices to show that every natural number \(m\) with \(1<m<p\) is not a factor of \(p\) . Let \(m\) be such a natural number. If \(m < T\) then by hypothesis \(m\) is not a factor of \(p\) .
html: By the prime test from Example 4.4.4, it suffices to show that every natural number \(m\) with \(1<m<p\) is not a factor of \(p\). Let \(m\) be such a natural number. If \(m < T\) then by hypothesis \(m\) is not a factor of \(p\).

text: So suppose that \(T \le m\) , and that \(m\) is a factor of \(p\) . Then there exists a natural number \(l\) such that \(p= ml\) . The natural number \(l\) is a factor of \(p\) , too.
html: So suppose that \(T \le m\), and that \(m\) is a factor of \(p\). Then there exists a natural number \(l\) such that \(p= ml\). The natural number \(l\) is a factor of \(p\), too.

text: We claim that \(1<l\) . It will suffice to show that \(m \cdot 1 < ml\) , and indeed,
html: We claim that \(1<l\). It will suffice to show that \(m \cdot 1 < ml\), and indeed,

text: We also claim that \(l<T\) . It will suffice to show that \(Tl < T \cdot T\) , and indeed,
html: We also claim that \(l<T\). It will suffice to show that \(Tl < T \cdot T\), and indeed,

text: Since we have established that \(1<l<T\) , by hypothesis \(l\) is not a factor of \(p\) , contradiction. So \(m\) must not be a factor of \(p\) after all.
html: Since we have established that \(1<l<T\), by hypothesis \(l\) is not a factor of \(p\), contradiction. So \(m\) must not be a factor of \(p\) after all.

text: We record this for future use under the Lean name better_prime_test .
html: We record this for future use under the Lean name better_prime_test.

text: Give a different proof of a problem from the exercises to Section 2.1 : Let \(x\) be a rational number whose square is 4, and which is greater than 1. Show that \(x=2\) . Instead of using the cancel tactic, give a direct proof using the ideas of this section: break into two cases, and then rule out one of them. (You may find it convenient to deduce a numeric contradiction.) example { x : ℚ } ( h1 : x ^ 2 = 4 ) ( h2 : 1 < x ) : x = 2 := by have h3 := calc ( x + 2 ) * ( x - 2 ) = x ^ 2 + 2 * x - 2 * x - 4 := by ring _ = 0 := by addarith [ h1 ] rw [ mul_eq_zero ] at h3 sorry
html:
• Give a different proof of a problem from the exercises to Section 2.1: Let \(x\) be a rational number whose square is 4, and which is greater than 1. Show that \(x=2\).

    Instead of using the cancel tactic, give a direct proof using the ideas of this section: break into two cases, and then rule out one of them. (You may find it convenient to deduce a numeric contradiction.)

    example {x : ℚ} (h1 : x ^ 2 = 4) (h2 : 1 < x) : x = 2 := by
  have h3 :=
    calc
      (x + 2) * (x - 2) = x ^ 2 + 2 * x - 2 * x - 4 := by ring
      _ = 0 := by addarith [h1]
  rw [mul_eq_zero] at h3
  sorry

text: In the course of Chapter 2 and Chapter 4 we learned the “grammar” of the various logical symbols, like \(\land\) , \(\forall\) and \(\to\) . In those chapters, logical reasoning took place in fairly concrete mathematical situations: problems about equalities and inequalities in the natural numbers, the rational numbers, and so on.
html: In the course of Chapter 2 and Chapter 4 we learned the “grammar” of the various logical symbols, like \(\land\), \(\forall\) and \(\to\). In those chapters, logical reasoning took place in fairly concrete mathematical situations: problems about equalities and inequalities in the natural numbers, the rational numbers, and so on.

text: In this chapter, we take a more abstract point of view, studying the process of logical reasoning in its own right. The central concept is the concept of logical equivalence : transformations to the logical structure of a statement which are always valid, because the “before” and “after” can be deduced from each other using only abstract logical reasoning, not anything specific to the mathematical situation at hand.
html: In this chapter, we take a more abstract point of view, studying the process of logical reasoning in its own right. The central concept is the concept of logical equivalence: transformations to the logical structure of a statement which are always valid, because the “before” and “after” can be deduced from each other using only abstract logical reasoning, not anything specific to the mathematical situation at hand.

text: The most important logical equivalences are those covered in the final section of the chapter, Section 5.3 . These are logical equivalences which move a negation symbol ( \(\lnot\) ) to a deeper position in a logical statement. Taken together, these transformations give us a way to delay and minimize our encounters with \(\lnot\) , the most awkward logical symbol.
html: The most important logical equivalences are those covered in the final section of the chapter, Section 5.3. These are logical equivalences which move a negation symbol (\(\lnot\)) to a deeper position in a logical statement. Taken together, these transformations give us a way to delay and minimize our encounters with \(\lnot\), the most awkward logical symbol.

text: If you abstract away the numbers, definitions, equations and inequalities, you are left with pure logic problems. And the pure logic tactics like obtain , apply , constructor , and so on can still be used.
html: If you abstract away the numbers, definitions, equations and inequalities, you are left with pure logic problems. And the pure logic tactics like obtain, apply, constructor, and so on can still be used.

text: It’s hardly worth trying to write proofs like this in words. The \(P\) and \(Q\) are abstract propositions ( Prop ), and this is just a game of manipulation.
html: It’s hardly worth trying to write proofs like this in words. The \(P\) and \(Q\) are abstract propositions (Prop), and this is just a game of manipulation.

text: We can think of propositional logic statements in the following way. Imagine that each variable, like \(P\) , can be either “true” or “false”. There are fixed rules for how truth and falsehood combine under logical operations. For example, \(P \land Q\) is true if \(P\) and \(Q\) are both true, and otherwise it is false. We can record this information in a table called a truth table :
html: We can think of propositional logic statements in the following way. Imagine that each variable, like \(P\), can be either “true” or “false”. There are fixed rules for how truth and falsehood combine under logical operations. For example, \(P \land Q\) is true if \(P\) and \(Q\) are both true, and otherwise it is false. We can record this information in a table called a truth table:

text: Likewise here is the rule for \(\lnot P\) : it is the opposite of \(P\) .
html: Likewise here is the rule for \(\lnot P\): it is the opposite of \(P\).

text: Using the rules for the basic operations, we can work out the truth table for a more complex statement step by step from the operations it’s composed of. For example, here’s how to find the truth table for \(\lnot(P \land \lnot Q)\) : first compute the table for \(\lnot Q\) , then for \(P \land \lnot Q\) , then finally for \(\lnot(P \land \lnot Q)\) .
html: Using the rules for the basic operations, we can work out the truth table for a more complex statement step by step from the operations it’s composed of. For example, here’s how to find the truth table for \(\lnot(P \land \lnot Q)\): first compute the table for \(\lnot Q\), then for \(P \land \lnot Q\), then finally for \(\lnot(P \land \lnot Q)\).

text: You should practise doing this by hand, but the Lean command #truth_table will also do it automatically.
html: You should practise doing this by hand, but the Lean command #truth_table will also do it automatically.

text: A tradition which goes back to the ancient Greeks is to give a slightly silly name to a class of numbers, in order to provide shorter theorem statements while studying it. In that vein, I present, just for this section … the superpowered numbers!
html: A tradition which goes back to the ancient Greeks is to give a slightly silly name to a class of numbers, in order to provide shorter theorem statements while studying it. In that vein, I present, just for this section … the superpowered numbers!

text: Definition
html: Definition

text: A natural number \(k\) is superpowered , if for every natural number \(n\) , the number \(k^{k^n} + 1\) is prime.
html: A natural number \(k\) is superpowered, if for every natural number \(n\), the number \(k^{k^n} + 1\) is prime.

text: Is 0 superpowered? \(0^{0^0}+1=1\) , \(0^{0^1}+1=2\) , \(0^{0^2}+1=2\) , \(0^{0^3}+1=2\) . We can also do these calculations in Lean:
html: Is 0 superpowered? \(0^{0^0}+1=1\), \(0^{0^1}+1=2\), \(0^{0^2}+1=2\), \(0^{0^3}+1=2\). We can also do these calculations in Lean:

text: The first is not prime, the others are, but taking them together, the “for all” in the definition is false. Formally:
html: The first is not prime, the others are, but taking them together, the “for all” in the definition is false. Formally:

text: Lemma
html: Lemma

text: 0 is not superpowered.
html: 0 is not superpowered.

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: Suppose 0 were superpowered. Then in particular \(0^{0^0}+1=1\) would be prime, which is a contradiction, since 1 is not prime.
html: Suppose 0 were superpowered. Then in particular \(0^{0^0}+1=1\) would be prime, which is a contradiction, since 1 is not prime.

text: To write this in Lean, we use the lemma not_prime_one , from an exercise to Section 4.5 .
html: To write this in Lean, we use the lemma not_prime_one, from an exercise to Section 4.5.

text: Don’t worry too much about the unfamiliar tactic conv in the above proof – we will not encounter it outside of this section. Just compare the goal state before and after the use of the tactic and check that you agree intuitively with the transformation which occurred.
html: Don’t worry too much about the unfamiliar tactic conv in the above proof – we will not encounter it outside of this section. Just compare the goal state before and after the use of the tactic and check that you agree intuitively with the transformation which occurred.

text: Is 1 superpowered?
html: Is 1 superpowered?

text: Lemma
html: Lemma

text: 1 is superpowered.
html: 1 is superpowered.

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: Let \(n\) be a natural number. Then \(1^{1^n}+1=1^1+1=2\) , which is prime.
html: Let \(n\) be a natural number. Then \(1^{1^n}+1=1^1+1=2\), which is prime.

text: To write this in Lean, we use the lemma prime_two , from Example 4.1.8 .
html: To write this in Lean, we use the lemma prime_two, from Example 4.1.8.

text: Is 2 superpowered?
html: Is 2 superpowered?

text: All these numbers are prime, as it happens. But checking 257 is prime using our usual lemma better_prime_test will be a good 30 lines of calculations in Lean, and as for 65537, I certainly don’t have the patience. The next one will be even worse. Let’s leave the question of 2 open for now.
html: All these numbers are prime, as it happens. But checking 257 is prime using our usual lemma better_prime_test will be a good 30 lines of calculations in Lean, and as for 65537, I certainly don’t have the patience. The next one will be even worse. Let’s leave the question of 2 open for now.

text: Is 3 superpowered?
html: Is 3 superpowered?

text: Nope! It fails even at the first step.
html: Nope! It fails even at the first step.

text: Lemma
html: Lemma

text: 3 is not superpowered.
html: 3 is not superpowered.

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: Suppose 3 were superpowered. Then in particular \(3^{3^0}+1=4\) would be prime, which is a contradiction, since \(4=2\cdot 2\) .
html: Suppose 3 were superpowered. Then in particular \(3^{3^0}+1=4\) would be prime, which is a contradiction, since \(4=2\cdot 2\).

text: Remember to use the lemma not_prime in Lean to prove that a number is not prime by producing a factor for it.
html: Remember to use the lemma not_prime in Lean to prove that a number is not prime by producing a factor for it.

text: All this was warmup. Here is the question I really want to study.
html: All this was warmup. Here is the question I really want to study.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Prove that there exists a natural number \(k\) , such that \(k\) is superpowered and \(k+1\) is not.
html: Prove that there exists a natural number \(k\), such that \(k\) is superpowered and \(k+1\) is not.

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: We consider two cases, depending on whether or not 2 is superpowered.
html: We consider two cases, depending on whether or not 2 is superpowered.

text: If 2 is superpowered, then \(k=2\) has the desired property, since 2 is superpowered and 3 is not superpowered.
html: If 2 is superpowered, then \(k=2\) has the desired property, since 2 is superpowered and 3 is not superpowered.

text: If not, then \(k=1\) has the desired property, since 1 is superpowered and 2 is not superpowered. 1
html: If not, then \(k=1\) has the desired property, since 1 is superpowered and 2 is not superpowered. 1

text: The point about this proof is that it works even though we don’t know whether 2 is superpowered or not. Either way, we have a way of solving the problem.
html: The point about this proof is that it works even though we don’t know whether 2 is superpowered or not. Either way, we have a way of solving the problem.

text: The fact that any statement (such as “2 is superpowered”) must be either true or false is an axiom of mathematics, called the law of the excluded middle . Therefore, this is always a valid case division in a proof, although it is relatively rare to need to do it.
html: The fact that any statement (such as “2 is superpowered”) must be either true or false is an axiom of mathematics, called the law of the excluded middle. Therefore, this is always a valid case division in a proof, although it is relatively rare to need to do it.

text: In Lean, you can perform a case division on the truth or falsehood of a statement by using the tactic by_cases . In the proof that follows, using this tactic takes us from a goal state of
html: In Lean, you can perform a case division on the truth or falsehood of a statement by using the tactic by_cases. In the proof that follows, using this tactic takes us from a goal state of

text: to a goal state with two goals, one under the assumption Superpowered 2 and one under the assumption ¬ Superpowered 2 .
html: to a goal state with two goals, one under the assumption Superpowered 2 and one under the assumption ¬ Superpowered 2.

text: Here is the full proof in Lean.
html: Here is the full proof in Lean.

text: As noted above, it’s relatively rare to need to use the law of the excluded middle in a proof. But here is one more example where it’s needed, this time from propositional logic: “two wrongs make a right”.
html: As noted above, it’s relatively rare to need to use the law of the excluded middle in a proof. But here is one more example where it’s needed, this time from propositional logic: “two wrongs make a right”.

text: Footnotes
html: Footnotes

text: Experts will notice that this proof adapts the idea from a more famous problem: show that there exists an irrational power of an irrational number which is rational.
html: Experts will notice that this proof adapts the idea from a more famous problem: show that there exists an irrational power of an irrational number which is rational.

text: An important family of logical equivalences allows us to “push” negations inwards in a logical statement. For example, we proved a rule for negating \(\exists\) in Example 5.1.6 (that \(\lnot\exists x, P(x)\) is logically equivalent to \(\forall x, \lnot P(x)\) ), and a rule for negating \(\lor\) in the exercises to Section 5.1 (that \(\lnot(P \lor Q)\) is logically equivalent to \(\lnot P \land \lnot Q\) ).
html: An important family of logical equivalences allows us to “push” negations inwards in a logical statement. For example, we proved a rule for negating \(\exists\) in Example 5.1.6 (that \(\lnot\exists x, P(x)\) is logically equivalent to \(\forall x, \lnot P(x)\)), and a rule for negating \(\lor\) in the exercises to Section 5.1 (that \(\lnot(P \lor Q)\) is logically equivalent to \(\lnot P \land \lnot Q\)).

text: Let’s do one more of this form, a rule for negating \(\land\) . This one requires the use of the law of the excluded middle. I have done the first half and left the second half for you.
html: Let’s do one more of this form, a rule for negating \(\land\). This one requires the use of the law of the excluded middle. I have done the first half and left the second half for you.

text: By applying these rules in sequence, any mathematical statement can be brought to a form with “negations on the inside”. This is generally the most convenient form for proofs (compare the relative awkwardness of the contradiction proofs in Section 4.4 and Section 4.5 with the proofs in earlier sections).
html: By applying these rules in sequence, any mathematical statement can be brought to a form with “negations on the inside”. This is generally the most convenient form for proofs (compare the relative awkwardness of the contradiction proofs in Section 4.4 and Section 4.5 with the proofs in earlier sections).

text: Here is an example of this process.
html: Here is an example of this process.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Show that \(\lnot(\forall m :\mathbb{Z}, m\ne 2 \to \exists n:\mathbb{Z},n^2 = m)\) is logically equivalent to \(\exists m :\mathbb{Z}, m\ne 2\land \forall n :\mathbb{Z},n^2 ≠ m\) .
html: Show that \(\lnot(\forall m :\mathbb{Z}, m\ne 2 \to \exists n:\mathbb{Z},n^2 = m)\) is logically equivalent to \(\exists m :\mathbb{Z}, m\ne 2\land \forall n :\mathbb{Z},n^2 ≠ m\).

text: We can prove this in Lean with a calculation using the rel tactic, rewriting at each step by one of the rules in Table 5.1 .
html: We can prove this in Lean with a calculation using the rel tactic, rewriting at each step by one of the rules in Table 5.1.

text: Try it yourself!
html: Try it yourself!

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Show that \(\lnot(\forall n :\mathbb{Z}, \exists m : \mathbb{Z}, n^2 < m < (n+1)^2)\) is logically equivalent to \(\exists n :\mathbb{Z}, \forall m : \mathbb{Z}, n^2 \geq m \lor m \geq (n+1)^2\) .
html: Show that \(\lnot(\forall n :\mathbb{Z}, \exists m : \mathbb{Z}, n^2 < m < (n+1)^2)\) is logically equivalent to \(\exists n :\mathbb{Z}, \forall m : \mathbb{Z}, n^2 \geq m \lor m \geq (n+1)^2\).

text: In this problem, in addition to the rules from Table 5.1 , you’ll need to use the lemma not_lt to convert the negation of a \(<\) to a \(\geq\) .
html: In this problem, in addition to the rules from Table 5.1, you’ll need to use the lemma not_lt to convert the negation of a \(<\) to a \(\geq\).

text: Also notice that \(n^2 < m < (n+1)^2\) is shorthand for \(n^2 < m \land m < (n+1)^2\) . We have encountered this point before, in Example 1.4.4 .
html: Also notice that \(n^2 < m < (n+1)^2\) is shorthand for \(n^2 < m \land m < (n+1)^2\). We have encountered this point before, in Example 1.4.4.

text: Let’s show how the process of pushing negations inwards is useful in regular proofs. We return to the problem from Example 4.5.4 .
html: Let’s show how the process of pushing negations inwards is useful in regular proofs. We return to the problem from Example 4.5.4.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Show that there does not exist a natural number \(n\) , such that \(n^2=2\) .
html: Show that there does not exist a natural number \(n\), such that \(n^2=2\).

text: At the time, we observed that the solution to this problem seemed very similar to that of Example 2.3.2 .
html: At the time, we observed that the solution to this problem seemed very similar to that of Example 2.3.2.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Let \(n\) be any natural number. Show that \(n ^ 2 \ne 2\) .
html: Let \(n\) be any natural number. Show that \(n ^ 2 \ne 2\).

text: Now we can understand why: the statements of the two problems are logically equivalent! The mathematical ideas in the two solutions are the same, but Example 2.3.2 ’s solution is conceptually simpler because it doesn’t involve a contradiction. We can give a more comprehensible solution to Example 4.5.4 by re-stating it in the form Example 2.3.2 and then writing out Example 2.3.2 ’s solution.
html: Now we can understand why: the statements of the two problems are logically equivalent! The mathematical ideas in the two solutions are the same, but Example 2.3.2’s solution is conceptually simpler because it doesn’t involve a contradiction. We can give a more comprehensible solution to Example 4.5.4 by re-stating it in the form Example 2.3.2 and then writing out Example 2.3.2’s solution.

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: It suffices to prove that for any natural number \(n\) , we have \(n ^ 2 \ne 2\) .
html: It suffices to prove that for any natural number \(n\), we have \(n ^ 2 \ne 2\).

text: We consider separately the cases \(n \le 1\) and \(2 \le n\) .
html: We consider separately the cases \(n \le 1\) and \(2 \le n\).

text: Case 1 ( \(n \le 1\) ): It suffices to prove that \(n ^ 2 < 2\) . Indeed,
html: Case 1 (\(n \le 1\)): It suffices to prove that \(n ^ 2 < 2\). Indeed,

text: Case 2 ( \(2 \le n\) ): It suffices to prove that \(n ^ 2 > 2\) . Indeed,
html: Case 2 (\(2 \le n\)): It suffices to prove that \(n ^ 2 > 2\). Indeed,

text: Let us call a real number \(x\) tribalanced , if for every natural number \(n\) , the inequality \(\left(1+\frac{x}{n}\right)^n<3\) holds. Show that there exists a real number \(x\) , such that \(x\) is tribalanced and \(x+1\) is not. def Tribalanced ( x : ℝ ) : Prop := ∀ n : ℕ , ( 1 + x / n ) ^ n < 3 example : ∃ x : ℝ , Tribalanced x ∧ ¬ Tribalanced ( x + 1 ) := by sorry

text: In case you’re still wondering: 2 is not superpowered. The question was raised in 1650 by the mathematician Pierre de Fermat, who observed, as we did, that 3, 5, 17, 257 and 65537 are all prime. It was settled in 1732, when Leonhard Euler showed that the next number in the sequence, \(2^{2^5}+1=4294967297\) , is equal to \(641 \times 6700417\) and therefore not prime. Use Euler’s discovery to give a proof without case division to the problem in Example 5.2.5 . example : ∃ k : ℕ , Superpowered k ∧ ¬ Superpowered ( k + 1 ) := by sorry

• Work out the following negations in your head, then check your work using the Lean output.

    #push_neg ¬(∀ n : ℕ, n > 0 → ∃ k l : ℕ, k < n ∧ l < n ∧ k ≠ l)
#push_neg ¬(∀ m : ℤ, m ≠ 2 → ∃ n : ℤ, n ^ 2 = m)
#push_neg ¬(∃ x : ℝ, ∀ y : ℝ, ∃ m : ℤ, x < y * m ∧ y * m < m)
#push_neg ¬(∃ x : ℝ, ∀ q : ℝ, q > x → ∃ m : ℕ, q ^ m > x)

text: Show that it is not true that for all real numbers \(x\) , we have \(x^2\geq x\) . (We solved this already in Example 4.5.1 , but this time, give a proof which begins with push_neg .) example : ¬ ( ∀ x : ℝ , x ^ 2 ≥ x ) := by push_neg sorry
html:
• Show that it is not true that for all real numbers \(x\), we have \(x^2\geq x\).

    (We solved this already in Example 4.5.1, but this time, give a proof which begins with push_neg.)

    example : ¬ (∀ x : ℝ, x ^ 2 ≥ x) := by
  push_neg
  sorry

text: Show that there does not exist a real number \(t\) , such that \(t \le 4\) and \(t\geq 5\) . (We solved this already in the exercises to Section 4.5 , but this time, give a proof which begins with push_neg .) example : ¬ ( ∃ t : ℝ , t ≤ 4 ∧ t ≥ 5 ) := by push_neg sorry
html:
• Show that there does not exist a real number \(t\), such that \(t \le 4\) and \(t\geq 5\).

    (We solved this already in the exercises to Section 4.5, but this time, give a proof which begins with push_neg.)

    example : ¬ (∃ t : ℝ, t ≤ 4 ∧ t ≥ 5) := by
  push_neg
  sorry

text: Show that 7 is not even. (We solved this already in the exercises to Section 4.5 , but this time, give a proof which begins with push_neg .) example : ¬ Int.Even 7 := by dsimp [ Int.Even ] push_neg sorry
html:
• Show that 7 is not even.

    (We solved this already in the exercises to Section 4.5, but this time, give a proof which begins with push_neg.)

    example : ¬ Int.Even 7 := by
  dsimp [Int.Even]
  push_neg
  sorry

text: Let \(p\) and \(k\) be natural numbers, with \(k\ne 1\) , \(k\ne p\) , and \(k\mid p\) . Show that \(p\) is not prime. (We solved this already in Example 4.5.7 , but this time, give a proof which begins with push_neg .) example { p : ℕ } ( k : ℕ ) ( hk1 : k ≠ 1 ) ( hkp : k ≠ p ) ( hk : k ∣ p ) : ¬ Prime p := by dsimp [ Prime ] push_neg sorry
html:
• Let \(p\) and \(k\) be natural numbers, with \(k\ne 1\), \(k\ne p\), and \(k\mid p\). Show that \(p\) is not prime.

    (We solved this already in Example 4.5.7, but this time, give a proof which begins with push_neg.)

    example {p : ℕ} (k : ℕ) (hk1 : k ≠ 1) (hkp : k ≠ p) (hk : k ∣ p) : ¬ Prime p := by
  dsimp [Prime]
  push_neg
  sorry

text: Show that it is not true that there exists an integer \(a\) , such that for all integers \(n\) , \(2a^3 ≥ na+7\) . Suggested structure: Start your proof by negation-normalizing. You might find it interesting to compare this fact with Exercise 8 in Section 2.5 . How is it possible that this statement is false and that one is true? example : ¬ ∃ a : ℤ , ∀ n : ℤ , 2 * a ^ 3 ≥ n * a + 7 := by sorry
html:

text: Let \(p \geq 2\) be a natural number which is not prime. Show that there exists a natural number \(2 \le m < p\) which is a factor of \(p\) . We record this lemma for future use under the name exists_factor_of_not_prime . Suggested structure: Set up an intermediate goal that it is not true that any natural number \(2 \le m < p\) is not a factor of \(p\) , and prove it by contradiction using the lemma prime_test from Example 4.4.4 . Then negation-normalize that result. example { p : ℕ } ( hp : ¬ Prime p ) ( hp2 : 2 ≤ p ) : ∃ m , 2 ≤ m ∧ m < p ∧ m ∣ p := by have H : ¬ ( ∀ ( m : ℕ ), 2 ≤ m → m < p → ¬ m ∣ p ) · intro H sorry sorry
html:
• Let \(p \geq 2\) be a natural number which is not prime. Show that there exists a natural number \(2 \le m < p\) which is a factor of \(p\).

    We record this lemma for future use under the name exists_factor_of_not_prime.

    Suggested structure: Set up an intermediate goal that it is not true that any natural number \(2 \le m < p\) is not a factor of \(p\), and prove it by contradiction using the lemma prime_test from Example 4.4.4. Then negation-normalize that result.

    example {p : ℕ} (hp : ¬ Prime p) (hp2 : 2 ≤ p) : ∃ m, 2 ≤ m ∧ m < p ∧ m ∣ p := by
  have H : ¬ (∀ (m : ℕ), 2 ≤ m → m < p → ¬m ∣ p)
  · intro H
    sorry
  sorry
1

text: This chapter introduces induction , a proof method which applies to the natural numbers and to other discrete types such as integers or pairs of natural numbers. We also introduce recursion , a method for defining sequences (and, more generally, functions from discrete types); induction is the canonical method for proving results about recursively-defined objects.
html: This chapter introduces induction, a proof method which applies to the natural numbers and to other discrete types such as integers or pairs of natural numbers. We also introduce recursion, a method for defining sequences (and, more generally, functions from discrete types); induction is the canonical method for proving results about recursively-defined objects.

text: In Section 6.1 - Section 6.3 , we use only the most traditional form of induction, proving a result for a natural number by relating it to the result for the previous natural number, and small variants on this form of induction. In Section 6.4 - Section 6.7 , we introduce strong induction , and, even more generally, well-founded induction . These induction principles are more flexible.
html: In Section 6.1 - Section 6.3, we use only the most traditional form of induction, proving a result for a natural number by relating it to the result for the previous natural number, and small variants on this form of induction. In Section 6.4 - Section 6.7, we introduce strong induction, and, even more generally, well-founded induction. These induction principles are more flexible.

text: Let \(n\) be a natural number. Show that \(2 ^n\ge n+1\) .
html: Let \(n\) be a natural number. Show that \(2 ^n\ge n+1\).

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: We prove this by induction on \(n\) . The base case, \(2^0\geq 0+1\) , is clear.
html: We prove this by induction on \(n\). The base case, \(2^0\geq 0+1\), is clear.

text: Suppose now that for some natural number \(k\) , it is true that \(2 ^k\ge k+1\) . Then
html: Suppose now that for some natural number \(k\), it is true that \(2 ^k\ge k+1\). Then

html: In Lean, the tactic simple_induction will set up an induction proof. Here, before the line simple_induction n with k IH, the goal state displays a single goal,

text: and after the tactic is used the goal state displays two goals, one for the base case and one for the inductive step.
html: and after the tactic is used the goal state displays two goals, one for the base case and one for the inductive step.

text: Theorem
html: Theorem

text: Let \(n\) be a natural number. Then \(n\) is either even or odd.
html: Let \(n\) be a natural number. Then \(n\) is either even or odd.

text: (Compare Example 3.1.9 , Example 4.2.9 .)
html: (Compare Example 3.1.9, Example 4.2.9.)

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: We prove this by induction on \(n\) .
html: We prove this by induction on \(n\).

text: The base case is to show that 0 is either even or odd. We will show that it is even. Indeed, \(0=2\cdot 0\) .
html: The base case is to show that 0 is either even or odd. We will show that it is even. Indeed, \(0=2\cdot 0\).

text: Suppose now that for some natural number \(k\) , it is true that \(k\) is either even or odd.
html: Suppose now that for some natural number \(k\), it is true that \(k\) is either even or odd.

text: Case 1 ( \(k\) is even): Then there exists an integer \(x\) such that \(k=2x\) , and so \(k+1 = 2x+1\) , so \(k+1\) is odd.
html: Case 1 (\(k\) is even): Then there exists an integer \(x\) such that \(k=2x\), and so \(k+1 = 2x+1\), so \(k+1\) is odd.

text: Case 2 ( \(k\) is odd): Then there exists an integer \(x\) such that \(k=2x+1\) , and
html: Case 2 (\(k\) is odd): Then there exists an integer \(x\) such that \(k=2x+1\), and

text: so \(k+1\) is even.
html: so \(k+1\) is even.

text: Theorem
html: Theorem

text: Let \(a, b, d\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv b \mod d\) . Let \(n\) be a natural number. Then \(a^n\equiv b ^ n \mod d\) .
html: Let \(a, b, d\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv b \mod d\). Let \(n\) be a natural number. Then \(a^n\equiv b ^ n \mod d\).

text: This is the power rule for modular arithmetic, the lemma Int.modEq.pow , which we stated without proof in Example 3.3.9 . It is one of the lemmas which are bundled together to form the tactic rel ’s capability for modular arithmetic.
html: This is the power rule for modular arithmetic, the lemma Int.modEq.pow, which we stated without proof in Example 3.3.9. It is one of the lemmas which are bundled together to form the tactic rel’s capability for modular arithmetic.

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: We prove this by induction on \(n\) .
html: We prove this by induction on \(n\).

text: We first note that \(a^0-b^0 = d\cdot 0\) , so \(d\mid a^0-b^0\) , so \(a^0\equiv b ^ 0 \mod d\) . This is the base case.
html: We first note that \(a^0-b^0 = d\cdot 0\), so \(d\mid a^0-b^0\), so \(a^0\equiv b ^ 0 \mod d\). This is the base case.

text: Now, let \(k\) be a natural number, and suppose that \(a^k\equiv b ^ k \mod d\) . Then there exists an integer \(x\) such that \(a^k- b ^ k =dx\) . Also, by hypothesis, \(a\equiv b \mod d\) , so there exists an integer \(y\) such that \(a-b=dy\) . We then have
html: Now, let \(k\) be a natural number, and suppose that \(a^k\equiv b ^ k \mod d\). Then there exists an integer \(x\) such that \(a^k- b ^ k =dx\). Also, by hypothesis, \(a\equiv b \mod d\), so there exists an integer \(y\) such that \(a-b=dy\). We then have

text: so \(d\mid a^{k+1}-b^{k+1}\) , so \(a^{k+1}\equiv b ^ {k+1} \mod d\) .
html: so \(d\mid a^{k+1}-b^{k+1}\), so \(a^{k+1}\equiv b ^ {k+1} \mod d\).

text: Induction from a nonzero starting point is a particularly useful technique for “sufficiently large” problems.

text: We have been encountering increasingly complicated induction principles, starting from “simple induction” in Example 6.1.1 and eventually reaching rather niche induction principles like “two-step induction from a specified starting point” in Example 6.3.5 . Rather than develop more and more exotic induction principles as our problems become yet more complicated, let’s explain a more general method: strong induction . This method lets us prove a proposition for the natural numbers one by one, relying at each step not just on the immediately previous step, but on any previous step.
html: We have been encountering increasingly complicated induction principles, starting from “simple induction” in Example 6.1.1 and eventually reaching rather niche induction principles like “two-step induction from a specified starting point” in Example 6.3.5. Rather than develop more and more exotic induction principles as our problems become yet more complicated, let’s explain a more general method: strong induction. This method lets us prove a proposition for the natural numbers one by one, relying at each step not just on the immediately previous step, but on any previous step.

text: Theorem
html: Theorem

text: Let \(n \geq 2\) be a natural number. Then there exists a prime number \(p\) which is a factor of \(n\) .
html: Let \(n \geq 2\) be a natural number. Then there exists a prime number \(p\) which is a factor of \(n\).

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: We prove this by strong induction on \(n\) . Let \(n\) be a natural number and suppose that for all natural numbers \(2 \le m < n\) , there exists a prime number \(p\) which is a factor of \(m\) . \((\star)\)
html: We prove this by strong induction on \(n\). Let \(n\) be a natural number and suppose that for all natural numbers \(2 \le m < n\), there exists a prime number \(p\) which is a factor of \(m\). \((\star)\)

text: If \(n\) is prime, then \(n\) itself is a prime factor of \(n\) , and we are done.
html: If \(n\) is prime, then \(n\) itself is a prime factor of \(n\), and we are done.

text: If \(n\) is not prime, then since \(n \geq 2\) , there exists a natural number \(2 \le m < n\) which is a factor of \(n\) . (This was proved in one of the exercises in Section 5.3 .) By the inductive hypothesis \((\star)\) , there exists a prime number \(p\) which is a factor of \(m\) .
html: If \(n\) is not prime, then since \(n \geq 2\), there exists a natural number \(2 \le m < n\) which is a factor of \(n\). (This was proved in one of the exercises in Section 5.3.) By the inductive hypothesis \((\star)\), there exists a prime number \(p\) which is a factor of \(m\).

text: Since \(m\mid n\) , there exists a natural number \(x\) such that \(n = mx\) . Since \(p \mid m\) , there exists a natural number \(y\) such that \(m = py\) . Then
html: Since \(m\mid n\), there exists a natural number \(x\) such that \(n = mx\). Since \(p \mid m\), there exists a natural number \(y\) such that \(m = py\). Then

text: For any integers \(n\) and \(d\) , with \(d\) positive, we have that \(\operatorname{mod}(n, d)\) is nonnegative.

text: We prove this by strong induction relative to the expression \(2n - d\) . Suppose that for all integers \(m\) and \(c\) with \(c\) positive and \(|2m - c|<|2n-d|\) , it is true that \(\operatorname{mod}(m, c)\) is nonnegative.
html: We prove this by strong induction relative to the expression \(2n - d\). Suppose that for all integers \(m\) and \(c\) with \(c\) positive and \(|2m - c|<|2n-d|\), it is true that \(\operatorname{mod}(m, c)\) is nonnegative.

text: Case 1 ( \(nd<0\) ): Then by the inductive hypothesis \(\operatorname{mod}(n, d)=\operatorname{mod}(n + d, d)\geq 0\) .
html: Case 1 (\(nd<0\)): Then by the inductive hypothesis \(\operatorname{mod}(n, d)=\operatorname{mod}(n + d, d)\geq 0\).

text: Case 2 ( \(0<d(n-d)\) ): Then by the inductive hypothesis \(\operatorname{mod}(n, d)=\operatorname{mod}(n - d, d)\geq 0\) .
html: Case 2 (\(0<d(n-d)\)): Then by the inductive hypothesis \(\operatorname{mod}(n, d)=\operatorname{mod}(n - d, d)\geq 0\).

text: Case 3 ( \(n=d\) ): Then \(\operatorname{mod}(n, d)= 0\) , so \(\operatorname{mod}(n, d)\geq 0\) .
html: Case 3 (\(n=d\)): Then \(\operatorname{mod}(n, d)= 0\), so \(\operatorname{mod}(n, d)\geq 0\).

text: Case 4 ( \(0\le nd\le d^2\) and \(n\ne d\) ): Then since \(0\le nd\) and by hypothesis \(0<d\) , we have that \(\operatorname{mod}(n, d)=n\geq 0\) .
html: Case 4 (\(0\le nd\le d^2\) and \(n\ne d\)): Then since \(0\le nd\) and by hypothesis \(0<d\), we have that \(\operatorname{mod}(n, d)=n\geq 0\).

text: Theorem
html: Theorem

text: For any integers \(n\) and \(d\) , with \(d\) positive, we have that \(\operatorname{mod}(n, d)<d\) .

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: We prove this by strong induction relative to the expression \(2n - d\) . Suppose that for all integers \(m\) and \(c\) with \(c\) positive and \(|2m - c|<|2n-d|\) , it is true that \(\operatorname{mod}(m, c)<c\) .
html: We prove this by strong induction relative to the expression \(2n - d\). Suppose that for all integers \(m\) and \(c\) with \(c\) positive and \(|2m - c|<|2n-d|\), it is true that \(\operatorname{mod}(m, c)<c\).

text: Case 1 ( \(nd<0\) ): Then by the inductive hypothesis \(\operatorname{mod}(n, d)=\operatorname{mod}(n + d, d)<d\) .
html: Case 1 (\(nd<0\)): Then by the inductive hypothesis \(\operatorname{mod}(n, d)=\operatorname{mod}(n + d, d)<d\).

text: Case 2 ( \(0<d(n-d)\) ): Then by the inductive hypothesis \(\operatorname{mod}(n, d)=\operatorname{mod}(n - d, d)<d\) .
html: Case 2 (\(0<d(n-d)\)): Then by the inductive hypothesis \(\operatorname{mod}(n, d)=\operatorname{mod}(n - d, d)<d\).

text: Case 3 ( \(n=d\) ): Then \(\operatorname{mod}(n, d)= 0<d\) by hypothesis.
html: Case 3 (\(n=d\)): Then \(\operatorname{mod}(n, d)= 0<d\) by hypothesis.

text: Case 4 ( \(0\le nd\le d^2\) and \(n\ne d\) ): We have that \(n-d\le 0\) , since \(d(n-d)\le 0\) and by hypothesis \(0<d\) . Therefore \(n\le d\) . Also, by hypothesis, \(n\ne d\) . Combining these, we have that \(n< d\) .

text: We prove this by strong induction on \(b\) . Suppose that for all integers \(x\) and \(y\) with \(|y|<|b|\) , it is true that \(0 \le \operatorname{gcd}(x, y)\) .

text: Case 4 ( \(b=0\) , \(a<0\) ): Then we have that \(\operatorname{gcd}(a,b)=-a\geq 0\) .

html: and by the inductive hypothesis \(L(b,r)b+R(b,r)r=\operatorname{gcd}(b,r)\). So

text: Case 3 ( \(b=0\) , \(0\le a\) ): Then by the recurrence definitions, \(\operatorname{gcd}(a,b)=a\) , \(L(a,b)=1\) and \(R(a,b)=0\) , so
html: Case 3 (\(b=0\), \(0\le a\)): Then by the recurrence definitions, \(\operatorname{gcd}(a,b)=a\), \(L(a,b)=1\) and \(R(a,b)=0\), so

text: Prove that for all natural numbers \(a\) and \(b\) , \(P_{a,b} =P_{b,a}\) . theorem pascal_symm ( m n : ℕ ) : pascal m n = pascal n m := by match m , n with | 0 , 0 => sorry | a + 1 , 0 => sorry | 0 , b + 1 => sorry | a + 1 , b + 1 => sorry termination_by _ a b => a + b
html:
• Prove that for all natural numbers \(a\) and \(b\), \(P_{a,b} =P_{b,a}\).

    theorem pascal_symm (m n : ℕ) : pascal m n = pascal n m := by
  match m, n with
  | 0, 0 => sorry
  | a + 1, 0 => sorry
  | 0, b + 1 => sorry
  | a + 1, b + 1 => sorry
termination_by _ a b => a + b

  sorry

text: Consider the function \(T\) defined recursively on the integers by, \[\begin{split}T(n)= \begin{cases} T(1-n)+2n-1,&0< n\\ T(-n),&n< 0\\ 0&n=0. \end{cases}\end{split}\] This recursive definition is well-founded, since its self-references are strictly decreasing in \(|3n-1|\) . Prove that for all integers \(n\) , \(T(n)=n^2\) . def T ( n : ℤ ) : ℤ := if 0 < n then T ( 1 - n ) + 2 * n - 1 else if 0 < - n then T ( - n ) else 0 termination_by T n => 3 * n - 1 theorem T_eq ( n : ℤ ) : T n = n ^ 2 := by sorry
html:
• Consider the function \(T\) defined recursively on the integers by,

    \[\begin{split}T(n)= \begin{cases} T(1-n)+2n-1,&0< n\\ T(-n),&n< 0\\ 0&n=0. \end{cases}\end{split}\]

    This recursive definition is well-founded, since its self-references are strictly decreasing in \(|3n-1|\).

    Prove that for all integers \(n\), \(T(n)=n^2\).

    def T (n : ℤ) : ℤ :=
  if 0 < n then
    T (1 - n) + 2 * n - 1
  else if 0 < - n then
    T (-n)
  else
    0
termination_by T n => 3 * n - 1

theorem T_eq (n : ℤ) : T n = n ^ 2 := by
  sorry

text: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be integers, with \(b\) positive. Prove that there exists a unique integer \(r\) in the range \(0 \le r < b\) , such that \(a\equiv r\mod b\) . This theorem is an upgrade to Example 6.6.5 , stating uniqueness as well as existence. We stated it without proof in Example 4.3.4 (with the Lean name Int.existsUnique_modEq_lt ) and have been implicitly using it whenever we deduce a contradiction involving “obvious non-congruences”, such as in Example 4.4.3 . Suggested approach: Write the following as a stand-alone theorem uniqueness and prove it, somewhat along the lines of the special case proved in Example 4.3.4 : Let \(a\) and \(b\) be integers, with \(b\) positive. Let \(r\) and \(s\) be integers, both in the range \(0 \le r < b\) , \(0 \le s < b\) and both congruent to \(a\) modulo \(b\) . Show that they are equal. Then put all the pieces together, combining that with the argument from Example 6.6.5 . theorem uniqueness ( a b : ℤ ) ( h : 0 < b ) { r s : ℤ } ( hr : 0 ≤ r ∧ r < b ∧ a ≡ r [ ZMOD b ]) ( hs : 0 ≤ s ∧ s < b ∧ a ≡ s [ ZMOD b ]) : r = s := by sorry example ( a b : ℤ ) ( h : 0 < b ) : ∃! r : ℤ , 0 ≤ r ∧ r < b ∧ a ≡ r [ ZMOD b ] := by sorry
html:
• Let \(a\) and \(b\) be integers, with \(b\) positive. Prove that there exists a unique integer \(r\) in the range \(0 \le r < b\), such that \(a\equiv r\mod b\).

    This theorem is an upgrade to Example 6.6.5, stating uniqueness as well as existence. We stated it without proof in Example 4.3.4 (with the Lean name Int.existsUnique_modEq_lt) and have been implicitly using it whenever we deduce a contradiction involving “obvious non-congruences”, such as in Example 4.4.3.

    Suggested approach: Write the following as a stand-alone theorem uniqueness and prove it, somewhat along the lines of the special case proved in Example 4.3.4:

    Let \(a\) and \(b\) be integers, with \(b\) positive. Let \(r\) and \(s\) be integers, both in the range \(0 \le r < b\), \(0 \le s < b\) and both congruent to \(a\) modulo \(b\). Show that they are equal.

    Then put all the pieces together, combining that with the argument from Example 6.6.5.

    theorem uniqueness (a b : ℤ) (h : 0 < b) {r s : ℤ}
    (hr : 0 ≤ r ∧ r < b ∧ a ≡ r [ZMOD b])
    (hs : 0 ≤ s ∧ s < b ∧ a ≡ s [ZMOD b]) : r = s := by
  sorry

example (a b : ℤ) (h : 0 < b) : ∃! r : ℤ, 0 ≤ r ∧ r < b ∧ a ≡ r [ZMOD b] := by
  sorry

text: \(\underline{\text{If }0<b\text{ then }-b<\operatorname{mod}(a,b)<b}\) : By Example 6.6.3 and Example 6.6.4 , \(0\le \operatorname{mod}(a,b)<b\) , which establishes the upper bound immediately and the lower bound since \[\begin{split}-b&<0\\ &\le \operatorname{mod}(a,b).\end{split}\]
html:
• \(\underline{\text{If }0<b\text{ then }-b<\operatorname{mod}(a,b)<b}\): By Example 6.6.3 and Example 6.6.4, \(0\le \operatorname{mod}(a,b)<b\), which establishes the upper bound immediately and the lower bound since

    \[\begin{split}-b&<0\\ &\le \operatorname{mod}(a,b).\end{split}\]

text: \(\underline{\text{If }b<0\text{ then }b<\operatorname{mod}(a,-b)<-b}\) : We have that \(0<-b\) , so by Example 6.6.3 and Example 6.6.4 , \(0\le \operatorname{mod}(a,-b)<-b\) , which establishes the upper bound immediately and the lower bound since \[\begin{split}b&<0\\ &\le \operatorname{mod}(a,-b).\end{split}\]
html:
• \(\underline{\text{If }b<0\text{ then }b<\operatorname{mod}(a,-b)<-b}\): We have that \(0<-b\), so by Example 6.6.3 and Example 6.6.4, \(0\le \operatorname{mod}(a,-b)<-b\), which establishes the upper bound immediately and the lower bound since

    \[\begin{split}b&<0\\ &\le \operatorname{mod}(a,-b).\end{split}\]

text: Show that \(\operatorname{gcd}(a,b)\) is not just a common divisor of \(a\) and \(b\) (see Example 6.7.3 ), but their greatest common divisor : if \(d\) is an integer which divides both \(a\) and \(b\) , then it divides \(\operatorname{gcd}(a,b)\) . This problem does not need an induction; it is a direct corollary of Bézout’s identity ( Example 6.7.6 ). theorem gcd_maximal { d a b : ℤ } ( ha : d ∣ a ) ( hb : d ∣ b ) : d ∣ gcd a b := by sorry
html:
• Show that \(\operatorname{gcd}(a,b)\) is not just a common divisor of \(a\) and \(b\) (see Example 6.7.3), but their greatest common divisor: if \(d\) is an integer which divides both \(a\) and \(b\), then it divides \(\operatorname{gcd}(a,b)\).

    This problem does not need an induction; it is a direct corollary of Bézout’s identity (Example 6.7.6).

    theorem gcd_maximal {d a b : ℤ} (ha : d ∣ a) (hb : d ∣ b) : d ∣ gcd a b := by

text: This chapter is different in style from the other chapters of the book. The facts here are famous theorems, and their proofs require one-off, clever ideas. These particular ideas will not turn up again on homework or exams. Think of this chapter instead as a capstone: we explore what kinds of mathematical statements are in reach with the reasoning tools and theory we have developed so far in the book.

These particular ideas will not turn up again on homework or exams. Think of this chapter instead as a capstone: we explore what kinds of mathematical statements are in reach with the reasoning tools and theory we have developed so far in the book.

text: Theorem
html: Theorem

text: There are infinitely many prime numbers.
html: There are infinitely many prime numbers.

text: This is a an extremely old theorem, with the first known proof written in Euclid’s Elements in about 300 BC.
html: This is a an extremely old theorem, with the first known proof written in Euclid’s Elements in about 300 BC.

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: We show that for a given natural number \(N\) , there exists a prime number \(p \geq N\) .
html: We show that for a given natural number \(N\), there exists a prime number \(p \geq N\).

text: Consider \(N!\) , the factorial of \(N\) . By one of the exercises to Section 6.2 , \(0<N!\) , so \(2 \le N! + 1\) . Therefore, by Example 6.4.2 , there exists a prime number \(p\) which is a factor of \(N! + 1\) .
html: Consider \(N!\), the factorial of \(N\). By one of the exercises to Section 6.2, \(0<N!\), so \(2 \le N! + 1\). Therefore, by Example 6.4.2, there exists a prime number \(p\) which is a factor of \(N! + 1\).

text: Let \(k\) be a natural number such that \(N! + 1=pk\) . This number \(k\) cannot be zero, since if it were, we would have
html: Let \(k\) be a natural number such that \(N! + 1=pk\). This number \(k\) cannot be zero, since if it were, we would have

text: contradiction. Thus \(k>0\) , so \(k\) is of the form \(l+1\) for some natural number \(l\) , and \(N! + 1=p(l+1)\) .
html: contradiction. Thus \(k>0\), so \(k\) is of the form \(l+1\) for some natural number \(l\), and \(N! + 1=p(l+1)\).

text: We now show that \(p\) is not a factor of \(N!\) , by showing that \(N!\) lies between the consecutive multiples \(pl\) and \(p(l+1)\) of \(p\) . (This is the test from Example 4.5.8 .) Indeed,
html: We now show that \(p\) is not a factor of \(N!\), by showing that \(N!\) lies between the consecutive multiples \(pl\) and \(p(l+1)\) of \(p\). (This is the test from Example 4.5.8.) Indeed,

text: so \(pl< N!\) , and
html: so \(pl< N!\), and

text: If \(p\le N\) , then by Example 6.2.5 , \(p\) would be a factor of \(N!\) , which contradicts what we just proved. Therefore \(p > N\) . This gives the required prime number greater than or equal to \(N\) .
html: If \(p\le N\), then by Example 6.2.5, \(p\) would be a factor of \(N!\), which contradicts what we just proved. Therefore \(p > N\). This gives the required prime number greater than or equal to \(N\).

text: In Lean, the exercise to Section 6.2 has the name factorial_pos , Example 6.4.2 has the name exists_prime_factor , Example 4.5.8 has the name Nat.not_dvd_of_exists_lt_and_lt , and Example 6.2.5 has the name dvd_factorial .
html: In Lean, the exercise to Section 6.2 has the name factorial_pos, Example 6.4.2 has the name exists_prime_factor, Example 4.5.8 has the name Nat.not_dvd_of_exists_lt_and_lt, and Example 6.2.5 has the name dvd_factorial.

text: Theorem (Gauss’ lemma)
html: Theorem (Gauss’ lemma)

text: Let \(a\) , \(b\) and \(d\) be integers. Suppose that \(ab\) is a multiple of \(d\) and \(\operatorname{gcd}(a,d)=1\) . Then \(b\) is a multiple of \(d\) .
html: Let \(a\), \(b\) and \(d\) be integers. Suppose that \(ab\) is a multiple of \(d\) and \(\operatorname{gcd}(a,d)=1\). Then \(b\) is a multiple of \(d\).

text: This lemma is the “final form” of the argument we have carried out in special cases in Example 3.5.1 , Example 3.5.2 , etc. Like in those special cases, the trick is to find a “Bézout identity” relating \(a\) and \(d\) : multiples of \(a\) and \(d\) which differ by 1. In the special cases we could find such multiples explicitly. In the general situation the existence of such multiples is guaranteed by Example 6.7.6 .
html: This lemma is the “final form” of the argument we have carried out in special cases in Example 3.5.1, Example 3.5.2, etc. Like in those special cases, the trick is to find a “Bézout identity” relating \(a\) and \(d\): multiples of \(a\) and \(d\) which differ by 1. In the special cases we could find such multiples explicitly. In the general situation the existence of such multiples is guaranteed by Example 6.7.6.

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: By Example 6.7.6 (Bézout’s identity) there exist integers \(x\) and \(y\) such that \(xa + yd = \operatorname{gcd}(a, d)\) . Since \(ab\) is a multiple of \(d\) , there exists an integer \(z\) such that \(ab=dz\) . We then have that
html: By Example 6.7.6 (Bézout’s identity) there exist integers \(x\) and \(y\) such that \(xa + yd = \operatorname{gcd}(a, d)\). Since \(ab\) is a multiple of \(d\), there exists an integer \(z\) such that \(ab=dz\). We then have that

text: so \(b\) is a multiple of \(d\) .
html: so \(b\) is a multiple of \(d\).

text: Theorem (Euclid’s lemma)
html: Theorem (Euclid’s lemma)

text: Let \(a\) , \(b\) and \(p\) be natural numbers, with \(p\) prime. Suppose that \(ab\) is a multiple of \(p\) . Then either \(a\) or \(b\) is a multiple of \(p\) .
html: Let \(a\), \(b\) and \(p\) be natural numbers, with \(p\) prime. Suppose that \(ab\) is a multiple of \(p\). Then either \(a\) or \(b\) is a multiple of \(p\).

text: This lemma also dates back to Euclid’s Elements .
html: This lemma also dates back to Euclid’s Elements.

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: By Example 6.7.2 , \(\operatorname{gcd}(a,p)\geq 0\) , so \(\operatorname{gcd}(a,p)\) (a priori an integer) can be considered as a natural number. Let us call that natural number \(d\) . Then
html: By Example 6.7.2, \(\operatorname{gcd}(a,p)\geq 0\), so \(\operatorname{gcd}(a,p)\) (a priori an integer) can be considered as a natural number. Let us call that natural number \(d\). Then

text: A priori these divisibility statements are all statements about \(a\) , \(b\) , \(p\) and \(d\) considered as integers, but these are equivalent to the corresponding divisibility statements about natural numbers.
html: A priori these divisibility statements are all statements about \(a\), \(b\), \(p\) and \(d\) considered as integers, but these are equivalent to the corresponding divisibility statements about natural numbers.

text: We now begin the proof proper. Since \(p\) is prime and \(d\mid p\) , either \(d=1\) or \(d=p\) .
html: We now begin the proof proper. Since \(p\) is prime and \(d\mid p\), either \(d=1\) or \(d=p\).

text: Case 1 ( \(d=1\) ): Then by ( \(\star\) ), \(p \mid b\) .
html: Case 1 (\(d=1\)): Then by (\(\star\)), \(p \mid b\).

text: Case 2 ( \(d=p\) ): Then since \(d\mid a\) , \(p\mid a\) .
html: Case 2 (\(d=p\)): Then since \(d\mid a\), \(p\mid a\).

text: To write this proof in Lean, we need some tricks to handle the interaction between integers and natural numbers. These will not be used again and it’s fine not to read them very closely.
html: To write this proof in Lean, we need some tricks to handle the interaction between integers and natural numbers. These will not be used again and it’s fine not to read them very closely.

text: Corollary
html: Corollary

text: Let \(a\) , \(p\) and \(k\) be natural numbers, with \(p\) prime and \(k\geq 1\) . If \(a^k\) is a multiple of \(p\) , then \(a\) is a multiple of \(p\) .
html: Let \(a\), \(p\) and \(k\) be natural numbers, with \(p\) prime and \(k\geq 1\). If \(a^k\) is a multiple of \(p\), then \(a\) is a multiple of \(p\).

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: We prove this by induction on \(k\) , starting at 1.
html: We prove this by induction on \(k\), starting at 1.

text: Base case: If \(a^1\) is a multiple of \(p\) , then since \(a^1=a\) , we conclude that \(a\) is a multiple of \(p\) .
html: Base case: If \(a^1\) is a multiple of \(p\), then since \(a^1=a\), we conclude that \(a\) is a multiple of \(p\).

text: Inductive step: Let \(t\) be a natural number and suppose that if \(a^t\) is a multiple of \(p\) then \(a\) is. ( \(\star\) )
html: Inductive step: Let \(t\) be a natural number and suppose that if \(a^t\) is a multiple of \(p\) then \(a\) is. (\(\star\))

text: Now suppose that \(a^{t+1}\) is a multiple of \(p\) . Since \(a^{t+1}=a\cdot a^t\) , this means that \(a \cdot a^t\) is a multiple of \(p\) .
html: Now suppose that \(a^{t+1}\) is a multiple of \(p\). Since \(a^{t+1}=a\cdot a^t\), this means that \(a \cdot a^t\) is a multiple of \(p\).

text: Therefore, by Euclid’s lemma, either \(a\) is a multiple of \(p\) , in which case we are done, or \(a^t\) is a multiple of \(p\) , in which case by the inductive hypothesis ( \(\star\) ) we are done.
html: Therefore, by Euclid’s lemma, either \(a\) is a multiple of \(p\), in which case we are done, or \(a^t\) is a multiple of \(p\), in which case by the inductive hypothesis (\(\star\)) we are done.

text: Theorem
html: Theorem

text: There do not exist natural numbers \(a\) and \(b\) with \(b\ne 0\) , for which \(a^2=2b^2\) .
html: There do not exist natural numbers \(a\) and \(b\) with \(b\ne 0\), for which \(a^2=2b^2\).

text: This is another theorem which dates back to the ancient Greeks, in this case to the followers of Pythagoras , c. 450 BC.
html: This is another theorem which dates back to the ancient Greeks, in this case to the followers of Pythagoras, c. 450 BC.

text: This theorem constitutes most of the work of proving that the square root of two is irrational , that is, not a rational number ( \(\mathbb{Q}\) ). We can’t draw that conclusion here since we haven’t yet defined rational numbers precisely, but we will circle back to it later in the book.
html: This theorem constitutes most of the work of proving that the square root of two is irrational, that is, not a rational number (\(\mathbb{Q}\)). We can’t draw that conclusion here since we haven’t yet defined rational numbers precisely, but we will circle back to it later in the book.

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: We will show a logically equivalent fact: that for all natural numbers \(a\) and \(b\) with \(b\ne 0\) , it holds that \(a^2\ne 2b^2\) . We prove this by strong induction on \(b\) .
html: We will show a logically equivalent fact: that for all natural numbers \(a\) and \(b\) with \(b\ne 0\), it holds that \(a^2\ne 2b^2\). We prove this by strong induction on \(b\).

text: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be natural numbers and suppose that for all natural numbers \(r\) , \(s\) with \(s<b\) and \(s\ne 0\) , it holds that \(r^2\ne 2s^2\) .
html: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be natural numbers and suppose that for all natural numbers \(r\), \(s\) with \(s<b\) and \(s\ne 0\), it holds that \(r^2\ne 2s^2\).

text: Suppose that \(a^2= 2b^2\) . Then \(a^2\) is even, so by one of the exercises to Section 6.1 , \(a\) is even. Let \(k\) be a natural number such that \(a=2k\) . Then
html: Suppose that \(a^2= 2b^2\). Then \(a^2\) is even, so by one of the exercises to Section 6.1, \(a\) is even. Let \(k\) be a natural number such that \(a=2k\). Then

text: so \(b^2=2k^2\) . Then
html: so \(b^2=2k^2\). Then
so \(k>0\), so \(k\ne 0\).

text: We therefore invoke the inductive hypothesis with \(r=b\) , \(s=k\) . (Note that
html: We therefore invoke the inductive hypothesis with \(r=b\), \(s=k\). (Note that

text: so \(k<b\) , so the induction is well-founded.) The inductive hypothesis gives that \(b^2\ne 2k^2\) , contradiction. So it cannot be that \(a^2\ne 2b^2\) .
html: so \(k<b\), so the induction is well-founded.) The inductive hypothesis gives that \(b^2\ne 2k^2\), contradiction. So it cannot be that \(a^2\ne 2b^2\).

text: In Lean we break this argument into three pieces. The lemma irrat_aux_wf is the justification for the well-foundedness of the strong induction. Like in Example 6.7.1 , we tag the well-foundedness lemma with @[decreasing] to make it available later for the strong induction.
html: In Lean we break this argument into three pieces. The lemma irrat_aux_wf is the justification for the well-foundedness of the strong induction. Like in Example 6.7.1, we tag the well-foundedness lemma with @[decreasing] to make it available later for the strong induction.

text: The lemma irrat_aux is the claim which is proved by strong induction. It contains the central argument of the proof. In Lean, the exercise to Section 6.1 which is used in this step has the name Nat.even_of_pow_even .
html: The lemma irrat_aux is the claim which is proved by strong induction. It contains the central argument of the proof. In Lean, the exercise to Section 6.1 which is used in this step has the name Nat.even_of_pow_even.

text: Finally, the main theorem is actually logically equivalent to irrat_aux , and its proof consists of establishing this logical equivalence.
html: Finally, the main theorem is actually logically equivalent to irrat_aux, and its proof consists of establishing this logical equivalence.

text: That was fun … let’s do it again! Here is a different proof of the same theorem. Or, nearly the same theorem – this proof works better for integers. (It would be fiddly but not fundamentally difficult to convert the integer version to the natural number version or vice versa, using the techniques from Section 7.2 .)
html: That was fun … let’s do it again! Here is a different proof of the same theorem. Or, nearly the same theorem – this proof works better for integers. (It would be fiddly but not fundamentally difficult to convert the integer version to the natural number version or vice versa, using the techniques from Section 7.2.)

text: Theorem
html: Theorem

text: There do not exist integers \(a\) and \(b\) with \(b\ne 0\) , for which \(a^2=2b^2\) .
html: There do not exist integers \(a\) and \(b\) with \(b\ne 0\), for which \(a^2=2b^2\).

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: Suppose that \(a\) and \(b\) were integers with \(b\ne 0\) and \(a^2=2b^2\) .
html: Suppose that \(a\) and \(b\) were integers with \(b\ne 0\) and \(a^2=2b^2\).

text: Denote by \(d\) the greatest common divisor of \(a\) and \(b\) . By Example 6.7.3 , \(d\mid a\) and \(d\mid b\) . Let \(k\) and \(l\) be integers such that \(a=dk\) and \(b=dl\) .
html: Denote by \(d\) the greatest common divisor of \(a\) and \(b\). By Example 6.7.3, \(d\mid a\) and \(d\mid b\). Let \(k\) and \(l\) be integers such that \(a=dk\) and \(b=dl\).

text: Also by Example 6.7.6 (Bézout’s identity) there exist integers \(x\) and \(y\) such that \(xa+yb=d\) .
html: Also by Example 6.7.6 (Bézout’s identity) there exist integers \(x\) and \(y\) such that \(xa+yb=d\).

text: The key calculation is the following ( \(\dagger\) ).:
html: The key calculation is the following (\(\dagger\)).:

text: We have that \(d\ne 0\) , since if not,
html: We have that \(d\ne 0\), since if not,

text: contradiction. Hence by ( \(\dagger\) ),
html: contradiction. Hence by (\(\dagger\)),

text: But by Example 2.3.5 , no integer squares to 2, so this is impossible.
html: But by Example 2.3.5, no integer squares to 2, so this is impossible.

text: Note the use of Brahmagupta’s identity ( Example 1.1.3 ) above in the middle of the key calculation.
html: Note the use of Brahmagupta’s identity (Example 1.1.3) above in the middle of the key calculation.

text: by Example 6.7.3 , \(d\mid a\) and \(d\mid p\) ;
html: • by Example 6.7.3, \(d\mid a\) and \(d\mid p\);

text: ( \(\star\) ) by Gauss’ lemma, if \(p\mid ab\) and \(d=1\) then \(p \mid b\) .
html: • (\(\star\)) by Gauss’ lemma, if \(p\mid ab\) and \(d=1\) then \(p \mid b\).

text: When invoking lemmas about integers ( gcd_dvd_left , gcd_dvd_right , gauss_lemma , gcd_nonneg ) on natural number inputs, we cast the natural number inputs to integers, like (a:ℤ) – this is not always necessary but it avoids ambiguity. (The casts then display in the infoview with arrows, as ↑a , etc.)
html: • When invoking lemmas about integers (gcd_dvd_left, gcd_dvd_right, gauss_lemma, gcd_nonneg) on natural number inputs, we cast the natural number inputs to integers, like (a:ℤ) – this is not always necessary but it avoids ambiguity. (The casts then display in the infoview with arrows, as ↑a, etc.)

text: Once we have the hypothesis 0 ≤ gcd (a:ℤ) (p:ℤ) , we can use the Lean tactic lift to introduce a natural number d whose cast to the integers is gcd (a:ℤ) (p:ℤ) .
html: • Once we have the hypothesis 0 ≤ gcd (a:ℤ) (p:ℤ), we can use the Lean tactic lift to introduce a natural number d whose cast to the integers is gcd (a:ℤ) (p:ℤ).

text: Finally, we can run the tactic norm_cast on all our hypotheses, which converts statements about natural-numbers-cast-to-integers into the corresponding statements about natural numbers, if this is mathematically valid. For example, ↑d ∣ ↑a is converted to d ∣ a .
html: • Finally, we can run the tactic norm_cast on all our hypotheses, which converts statements about natural-numbers-cast-to-integers into the corresponding statements about natural numbers, if this is mathematically valid. For example, ↑d ∣ ↑a is converted to d ∣ a.

text: So far in the book, we have studied properties of numbers (whether a number is odd, positive, prime; whether one number is divisible by another) and operations on numbers (addition, greatest common divisor).
html: So far in the book, we have studied properties of numbers (whether a number is odd, positive, prime; whether one number is divisible by another) and operations on numbers (addition, greatest common divisor).

text: In this chapter we go up a level of abstraction, and study properties of and operations on functions. These new properties include: whether a function is injective , surjective , bijective ; whether one function is inverse to another; and the operation of composition .
html: In this chapter we go up a level of abstraction, and study properties of and operations on functions. These new properties include: whether a function is injective, surjective, bijective; whether one function is inverse to another; and the operation of composition.

text: We also expand our horizon beyond the numeric types ( \(\mathbb{N}\) , \(\mathbb{Z}\) , \(\mathbb{Q}\) , \(\mathbb{R}\) ) which have formed the setting of the early part of the book. We now start to work with a broader range of types, including function types, finite inductive types, and product types.
html: We also expand our horizon beyond the numeric types (\(\mathbb{N}\), \(\mathbb{Z}\), \(\mathbb{Q}\), \(\mathbb{R}\)) which have formed the setting of the early part of the book. We now start to work with a broader range of types, including function types, finite inductive types, and product types.

text: We have studied particular functions before. For example, the Fibonacci sequence from Example 6.3.3 is a function from \(\mathbb{N}\) to \(\mathbb{Z}\) : it takes in a natural number, for example 5, and gives out an integer, in this case 8 (the index-5 term of the sequence).
html: We have studied particular functions before. For example, the Fibonacci sequence from Example 6.3.3 is a function from \(\mathbb{N}\) to \(\mathbb{Z}\): it takes in a natural number, for example 5, and gives out an integer, in this case 8 (the index-5 term of the sequence).

text: The domain of a function is the type where it takes its input values, and the codomain of a function is the type where it takes its output values. For example, the domain of the Fibonacci sequence is \(\mathbb{N}\) and its codomain is \(\mathbb{Z}\) . The type of functions with domain \(\mathbb{N}\) and codomain \(\mathbb{Z}\) is denoted \(\mathbb{N}\to \mathbb{Z}\) . Lean will confirm for us that the Fibonacci sequence F has this type:
html: The domain of a function is the type where it takes its input values, and the codomain of a function is the type where it takes its output values. For example, the domain of the Fibonacci sequence is \(\mathbb{N}\) and its codomain is \(\mathbb{Z}\). The type of functions with domain \(\mathbb{N}\) and codomain \(\mathbb{Z}\) is denoted \(\mathbb{N}\to \mathbb{Z}\). Lean will confirm for us that the Fibonacci sequence F has this type:
