text: Now that we have the notation \(X\to Y\) for “the type of functions from \(X\) to \(Y\) ”, we can introduce properties of functions, in the same way that we have previously introduced properties (like “odd” and “prime”) of numbers. Here is our first one.
html: Now that we have the notation \(X\to Y\) for “the type of functions from \(X\) to \(Y\)”, we can introduce properties of functions, in the same way that we have previously introduced properties (like “odd” and “prime”) of numbers. Here is our first one.

text: Definition
html: Definition

text: A function \(f : X \to Y\) is injective , if for all \(x_1\) and \(x_2\) of type \(X\) , if \(f(x_1)=f(x_2)\) , then \(x_1=x_2\) .
html: A function \(f : X \to Y\) is injective, if for all \(x_1\) and \(x_2\) of type \(X\), if \(f(x_1)=f(x_2)\), then \(x_1=x_2\).

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Show that the function \(q:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\) from Example 8.1.1 is injective.
html: Show that the function \(q:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\) from Example 8.1.1 is injective.

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: Let \(x_1\) and \(x_2\) be real numbers and suppose that \(q(x_1)=q(x_2)\) . Then \(x_1+1=x_2+1\) , so \(x_1=x_2\) .

text: We have so far seen numeric types, like the integers \(\mathbb{Z}\) and the real numbers \(\mathbb{R}\) , and function types, like \(\mathbb{Z}\to \mathbb{R}\) (the functions from \(\mathbb{Z}\) to \(\mathbb{R}\) ).

text: Consider the following finite inductive types Celestial and Subatomic:

text: Prove that the function \(f\) is not bijective.

text: Theorem
html: Theorem

text: A function \(f:X\to Y\) is bijective, if and only if for all \(y\) of type \(Y\) , there exists a unique \(x\) of type \(X\) , such that \(f(x)=y\) .
html: A function \(f:X\to Y\) is bijective, if and only if for all \(y\) of type \(Y\), there exists a unique \(x\) of type \(X\), such that \(f(x)=y\).

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: First, suppose that \(f\) is bijective. Let \(y\) be of type \(Y\) . Since \(f\) is surjective, there exists \(x\) of type \(X\) , such that \(f(x)=y\) . We will show that this \(x\) is unique. Indeed, for any other \(x'\) with \(f(x')=y\) , we have that
html: First, suppose that \(f\) is bijective. Let \(y\) be of type \(Y\). Since \(f\) is surjective, there exists \(x\) of type \(X\), such that \(f(x)=y\). We will show that this \(x\) is unique. Indeed, for any other \(x'\) with \(f(x')=y\), we have that

text: and so by the injectivity of \(f\) , \(x'=x\) .
html: and so by the injectivity of \(f\), \(x'=x\).

text: Conversely, suppose that for all \(y\) of type \(Y\) , there exists a unique \(x\) of type \(X\) , such that \(f(x)=y\) . ( \(\star\) ) We must show that \(f\) is injective and surjective.
html: Conversely, suppose that for all \(y\) of type \(Y\), there exists a unique \(x\) of type \(X\), such that \(f(x)=y\). (\(\star\)) We must show that \(f\) is injective and surjective.

text: For the injectivity, let \(x_1\) and \(x_2\) be of type \(X\) , and suppose that \(f(x_1)=f(x_2)\) . By ( \(\star\) ) applied with \(y=f(x_1)\) , there exists a unique \(x\) of type \(X\) for which \(f(x)=f(x_1)\) . So, by the uniqueness, \(x_1=x\) , and also, since \(f(x_2)=f(x_1)=f(x)\) , \(x_2=x\) . Combining these, \(x_1=x_2\) .
html: For the injectivity, let \(x_1\) and \(x_2\) be of type \(X\), and suppose that \(f(x_1)=f(x_2)\). By (\(\star\)) applied with \(y=f(x_1)\), there exists a unique \(x\) of type \(X\) for which \(f(x)=f(x_1)\). So, by the uniqueness, \(x_1=x\), and also, since \(f(x_2)=f(x_1)=f(x)\), \(x_2=x\). Combining these, \(x_1=x_2\).

text: For the surjectivity, let \(y\) be of type \(Y\) . By ( \(\star\) ), there exists (a unique) \(x\) of type \(X\) for which \(f(x)=y\) .
html: For the surjectivity, let \(y\) be of type \(Y\). By (\(\star\)), there exists (a unique) \(x\) of type \(X\) for which \(f(x)=y\).

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Show that for all functions \(f\) from the Celestial type ( Example 8.2.4 ) to itself, if \(f\) is injective then it is bijective.
html: Show that for all functions \(f\) from the Celestial type (Example 8.2.4) to itself, if \(f\) is injective then it is bijective.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Show that it is not true that for all functions \(f:\mathbb{N}\to \mathbb{N}\) , if \(f\) is injective then it is bijective.
html: Show that it is not true that for all functions \(f:\mathbb{N}\to \mathbb{N}\), if \(f\) is injective then it is bijective.

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: We must show that there exists a function \(f:\mathbb{N}\to \mathbb{N}\) , which is injective but not bijective.
html: We must show that there exists a function \(f:\mathbb{N}\to \mathbb{N}\), which is injective but not bijective.

text: Indeed, consider the function \(f(n)=n+1\) . This function is injective, since for all natural numbers \(n_1\) and \(n_2\) , if \(n_1+1=n_2+1\) then \(n_1=n_2\) .
html: Indeed, consider the function \(f(n)=n+1\). This function is injective, since for all natural numbers \(n_1\) and \(n_2\), if \(n_1+1=n_2+1\) then \(n_1=n_2\).

text: However, this function is not surjective, and therefore not bijective. To see this, observe that for all natural numbers \(n\) , we have \(f(n)=n+1>0\) and therefore \(f(n)\ne 0\) .
html: However, this function is not surjective, and therefore not bijective. To see this, observe that for all natural numbers \(n\), we have \(f(n)=n+1>0\) and therefore \(f(n)\ne 0\).

text: Definition
html: Definition

text: The composition of the function \(g : Y \to Z\) with the function \(f : X \to Y\) is the function from \(X\) to \(Z\) which sends \(x\) to \(g(f(x))\) .
html: The composition of the function \(g : Y \to Z\) with the function \(f : X \to Y\) is the function from \(X\) to \(Z\) which sends \(x\) to \(g(f(x))\).

text: The composition of \(g : Y \to Z\) with \(f : X \to Y\) is denoted \(g \circ f\) (in Lean, g ∘ f ).
html: The composition of \(g : Y \to Z\) with \(f : X \to Y\) is denoted \(g \circ f\) (in Lean, g ∘ f).

text: The identity function \(\operatorname{Id}_X:X\to X\) is the function which sends each \(x\) of type \(X\) to itself.
html: The identity function \(\operatorname{Id}_X:X\to X\) is the function which sends each \(x\) of type \(X\) to itself.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Let \(s:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\) be the function defined by, \(s(x)=5-x\) .
html: Let \(s:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\) be the function defined by, \(s(x)=5-x\).

text: Show that \(s \circ s = \operatorname{Id}_\mathbb{R}\) .
html: Show that \(s \circ s = \operatorname{Id}_\mathbb{R}\).

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: Let \(x\) be a real number. We must show that \(5 - (5 - x) = x\) , which is true.
html: Let \(x\) be a real number. We must show that \(5 - (5 - x) = x\), which is true.

text: Definition

text: A function \(g : Y \to X\) is an inverse of \(f : X \to Y\) , if \(g \circ f=\operatorname{Id}_X\) and \(f \circ g=\operatorname{Id}_Y\) .

text: Proposition
html: Proposition

text: Let \(f : X \to Y\) be a bijective function. Then there exists a function \(g : Y \to X\) which is inverse to \(f\) .
html: Let \(f : X \to Y\) be a bijective function. Then there exists a function \(g : Y \to X\) which is inverse to \(f\).

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: Define a function \(g : Y \to X\) as follows: given \(y\) of type \(Y\) , by the surjectivity of \(f\) there exists an \(x\) of type \(X\) such that \(f(x)=y\) , and we set \(g(y)\) to be this \(x\) . Thus, for all \(y\) , it is true that \(f(g(y))=y\) . ( \(\star\) )
html: Define a function \(g : Y \to X\) as follows: given \(y\) of type \(Y\), by the surjectivity of \(f\) there exists an \(x\) of type \(X\) such that \(f(x)=y\), and we set \(g(y)\) to be this \(x\). Thus, for all \(y\), it is true that \(f(g(y))=y\). (\(\star\))

text: This immediately shows that \(f \circ g = \operatorname{Id}_Y\) . To show that \(g \circ f = \operatorname{Id}_X\) , let \(x\) be of type \(X\) . We have, by ( \(\star\) ), that
html: This immediately shows that \(f \circ g = \operatorname{Id}_Y\). To show that \(g \circ f = \operatorname{Id}_X\), let \(x\) be of type \(X\). We have, by (\(\star\)), that

html: so by the injectivity of \(f\), it follows that \((g \circ f)(x) = \operatorname{Id}_X(x)\).

text: Theorem
html: Theorem

text: Let \(f : X \to Y\) be a function. Then \(f\) is bijective, if and only if there exists a function \(g : Y \to X\) which is inverse to \(f\) .

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: We will show that the function is not surjective. In fact, we will show that for all \((m,n)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}^2\) , it is not true that \((m + n, m - n)=(0,1)\) . So let \((m,n)\) be in \(\mathbb{Z}^2\) and suppose that \((m + n, m - n)=(0,1)\) . Then \(m+n=0\) and \(m-n=1\) , so
html: We will show that the function is not surjective. In fact, we will show that for all \((m,n)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}^2\), it is not true that \((m + n, m - n)=(0,1)\). So let \((m,n)\) be in \(\mathbb{Z}^2\) and suppose that \((m + n, m - n)=(0,1)\). Then \(m+n=0\) and \(m-n=1\), so

text: The last part of the problem could play out in several different ways. The hypotheses \(m+n=0\) and \(m-n=1\) are pretty clearly contradictory (for integers), so there are several variant ways you could produce the contradiction instead of the numeric contradiction \(0\equiv 1\mod 2\) which I produced here.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Consider the function \((x,y)\mapsto x + y\) from \(\mathbb{R}^2\) to \(\mathbb{R}\) . Show that this function is
html: Consider the function \((x,y)\mapsto x + y\) from \(\mathbb{R}^2\) to \(\mathbb{R}\). Show that this function is

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: 1. We will show that there exist points \((x_1,y_1)\) and \((x_2,y_2)\) in \(\mathbb{R}^2\) such that \(x_1+y_1=x_2+y_2\) and \((x_1,y_1)\ne (x_2,y_2)\) . Indeed, consider the points \((0,0)\) and \((1,-1)\) . We have that \(0+0=1+-1\) and \((0,0)\ne (1,-1)\) .
html: 1. We will show that there exist points \((x_1,y_1)\) and \((x_2,y_2)\) in \(\mathbb{R}^2\) such that \(x_1+y_1=x_2+y_2\) and \((x_1,y_1)\ne (x_2,y_2)\). Indeed, consider the points \((0,0)\) and \((1,-1)\). We have that \(0+0=1+-1\) and \((0,0)\ne (1,-1)\).

text: 2. Let \(a\) be a real number. We must show that there exists a point \((x,y)\) in \(\mathbb{R}^2\) such that \(x+y=a\) . Indeed, \(a+0=a\) , so \((a,0)\) has this property.
html: 2. Let \(a\) be a real number. We must show that there exists a point \((x,y)\) in \(\mathbb{R}^2\) such that \(x+y=a\). Indeed, \(a+0=a\), so \((a,0)\) has this property.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Consider the function \((m,n)\mapsto 5m+8n\) from \(\mathbb{Z}^2\) to \(\mathbb{Z}\) . Show that this function is
html: Consider the function \((m,n)\mapsto 5m+8n\) from \(\mathbb{Z}^2\) to \(\mathbb{Z}\). Show that this function is

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: 1. We will show that there exist pairs \((m_1,n_1)\) and \((m_2,n_2)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}^2\) such that \(5m_1+8n_1=5m_2+8n_2\) and \((m_1,n_1)\ne (m_2,n_2)\) . Indeed, consider the pairs \((0,0)\) and \((8,-5)\) . We have that \(5\cdot 0+8\cdot 0=5\cdot 8+8\cdot -5\) and \((0,0)\ne (8,-5)\) .
html: 1. We will show that there exist pairs \((m_1,n_1)\) and \((m_2,n_2)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}^2\) such that \(5m_1+8n_1=5m_2+8n_2\) and \((m_1,n_1)\ne (m_2,n_2)\). Indeed, consider the pairs \((0,0)\) and \((8,-5)\). We have that \(5\cdot 0+8\cdot 0=5\cdot 8+8\cdot -5\) and \((0,0)\ne (8,-5)\).

text: 2. Let \(a\) be an integer. We must show that there exists a pair \((m,n)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}^2\) such that \(5m+8n=a\) . Indeed, \(5(-3a)+8(2a)=a\) , so \((-3a,2a)\) has this property.
html: 2. Let \(a\) be an integer. We must show that there exists a pair \((m,n)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}^2\) such that \(5m+8n=a\). Indeed, \(5(-3a)+8(2a)=a\), so \((-3a,2a)\) has this property.

text: (Where did the idea for the -3 and the 2 come from in the second part of this proof? Compare with Example 3.5.1 and Example 3.5.3 .)
html: (Where did the idea for the -3 and the 2 come from in the second part of this proof? Compare with Example 3.5.1 and Example 3.5.3.)

text: Theorem
html: Theorem

text: There exists a bijection from \(\mathbb{N}^2\) to \(\mathbb{N}\) .
html: There exists a bijection from \(\mathbb{N}^2\) to \(\mathbb{N}\).

text: First recall the sequence \(A_n\) from Example 6.2.4 ; we prove a lemma about it.
html: First recall the sequence \(A_n\) from Example 6.2.4; we prove a lemma about it.

text: And a corollary, more difficult.
html: And a corollary, more difficult.

text: We use the sequence \(A_n\) to define the function \(p:\mathbb{N}^2\to \mathbb{N}\) which will be the bijection we seek: \(p(a,b)=A_{a+b}+b\) .
html: We use the sequence \(A_n\) to define the function \(p:\mathbb{N}^2\to \mathbb{N}\) which will be the bijection we seek: \(p(a,b)=A_{a+b}+b\).

text: Finally we prove that this function \(p\) is indeed bijective. We set up an “intertwining” map \(i\) for \(p\) , and invoke the lemma surjective_of_intertwining proved in the exercises to Section 8.1 .
html: Finally we prove that this function \(p\) is indeed bijective. We set up an “intertwining” map \(i\) for \(p\), and invoke the lemma surjective_of_intertwining proved in the exercises to Section 8.1.

text: Let \(f:\mathbb{Q}\to\mathbb{Q}\) be a function which is strictly monotone ; that is, for all real numbers \(x\) and \(y\) with \(x<y\) , it is true that \(f(x)<f(y)\) . text: Let \(f:X\to\mathbb{N}\) be a function, let \(x_0\) be of type \(X\) with \(f(x_0)=0\) , and let \(i:X\to X\) be a function such that, for all \(x\) , \(f(i(x))=f(x)+1\) . Show that \(f\) is surjective. I recommend induction. We record this theorem for future use under the name surjective_of_intertwining . example { f : X → ℕ } { x0 : X } ( h0 : f x0 = 0 ) { i : X → X } ( hi : ∀ x , f ( i x ) = f x + 1 ) : Surjective f := by sorry

text: Let \(m_1\) and \(m_2\) be integers and suppose that \(q(m_1)=q(m_2)\) . Then by definition \[(m_1+1,2-m_1)=(m_2+1,2-m_2),\] so \(m_1+1=m_2+1\) and \(2-m_1=2-m_2\) , so \(m_1=m_2\) .
html:
• Let \(m_1\) and \(m_2\) be integers and suppose that \(q(m_1)=q(m_2)\). Then by definition

    \[(m_1+1,2-m_1)=(m_2+1,2-m_2),\]

    so \(m_1+1=m_2+1\) and \(2-m_1=2-m_2\), so \(m_1=m_2\).

text: We will show that there exists \((a,b)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}^2\) such that for all integers \(m\) , \(q(m)\ne(a,b)\) . Indeed, we will show that \((0,1)\) has this property. Suppose that \(m\) were an integer with \(q(m)=(0,1)\) . Then by definition \[(m+1,2-m)=(0,1),\] so \(m+1=0\) and \(2-m=1\) , hence \[\begin{split}1&=(m+1)+(2-m)-2\\ &=0+1-2\\ &=-1,\end{split}\] contradiction.
html:
• We will show that there exists \((a,b)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}^2\) such that for all integers \(m\), \(q(m)\ne(a,b)\). Indeed, we will show that \((0,1)\) has this property. Suppose that \(m\) were an integer with \(q(m)=(0,1)\). Then by definition

    \[(m+1,2-m)=(0,1),\]

    so \(m+1=0\) and \(2-m=1\), hence

    \[\begin{split}1&=(m+1)+(2-m)-2\\ &=0+1-2\\ &=-1,\end{split}\]

    contradiction.

text: This chapter introduces the language of sets , a convenient way to reason about the objects in a type which have some property. This language includes the concept of membership in a set, the property of a set being a subset of another set, and a whole zoo of set operations, such as intersection , union and complement , each of which is a wrapper for a logical symbol relating the underlying properties.
html: This chapter introduces the language of sets, a convenient way to reason about the objects in a type which have some property. This language includes the concept of membership in a set, the property of a set being a subset of another set, and a whole zoo of set operations, such as intersection, union and complement, each of which is a wrapper for a logical symbol relating the underlying properties.

text: In the last section of the chapter, Section 9.3 , we study the collection of sets in a type as a type in its own right.
html: In the last section of the chapter, Section 9.3, we study the collection of sets in a type as a type in its own right.

text: In type theory, the logical foundation for this book, a set in a type \(X\) is specified by a predicate on \(X\) . For example, “the set of integers \(n\) such that \(n\le 3\) ” is a set in \(\mathbb{Z}\) .
html: In type theory, the logical foundation for this book, a set in a type \(X\) is specified by a predicate on \(X\). For example, “the set of integers \(n\) such that \(n\le 3\)” is a set in \(\mathbb{Z}\).

text: There is a standard notation for sets specified by predicates. For example, the set described above is written notationally as \(\{n:\mathbb{Z} \mid n\le 3\}\) . Here is that set in Lean.
html: There is a standard notation for sets specified by predicates. For example, the set described above is written notationally as \(\{n:\mathbb{Z} \mid n\le 3\}\). Here is that set in Lean.

text: Note that the infoview confirms that the type of the expression is Set ℤ , a set of integers.
html: Note that the infoview confirms that the type of the expression is Set ℤ, a set of integers.

text: A term of type \(X\) belongs to a set in \(X\) specified by some predicate, if the predicate holds for that term.
html: A term of type \(X\) belongs to a set in \(X\) specified by some predicate, if the predicate holds for that term.

text: Let \(U\) and \(V\) be sets in the type \(X\) . The set \(U\) is a subset of the set \(V\) , if for all \(x\) of type \(X\) , if \(x\in U\) , then \(x\in V\) .

text: The empty set in a type \(X\) is the set which has no elements. That’s a slightly informal description: here’s the strict definition.

text: Definition

text: The empty set in a type \(X\) , denoted \(\emptyset\) , is \(\{x : X \mid \operatorname{False}\}\) .
html: The empty set in a type \(X\), denoted \(\emptyset\), is \(\{x : X \mid \operatorname{False}\}\).

text: It is true by pure logic that no element of type \(X\) belongs to the empty set in \(X\) , and that the empty set in \(X\) is a subset of every set in \(X\) .
html: It is true by pure logic that no element of type \(X\) belongs to the empty set in \(X\), and that the empty set in \(X\) is a subset of every set in \(X\).

text: To show a set \(U\) in \(X\) is equal to the empty set, you must show that \(U\) has no elements.
html: To show a set \(U\) in \(X\) is equal to the empty set, you must show that \(U\) has no elements.

text: The universe set in a type \(X\) is the set which contains everything in \(X\) . That’s again a slightly informal description: here’s the strict definition.
html: The universe set in a type \(X\) is the set which contains everything in \(X\). That’s again a slightly informal description: here’s the strict definition.

text: Definition
html: Definition

text: The universe set in a type \(X\) is \(\{x : X \mid \operatorname{True}\}\) .
html: The universe set in a type \(X\) is \(\{x : X \mid \operatorname{True}\}\).

text: It is true by pure logic that all elements of type \(X\) belong to the universe set in \(X\) , and that every set in \(X\) is a subset of the universe set in \(X\) .
html: It is true by pure logic that all elements of type \(X\) belong to the universe set in \(X\), and that every set in \(X\) is a subset of the universe set in \(X\).

text: Note that in Lean the universe set is denoted univ . On paper we often refer to the universe set of \(X\) as \(X\) , too (even though this is not strictly correct).
html: Note that in Lean the universe set is denoted univ. On paper we often refer to the universe set of \(X\) as \(X\), too (even though this is not strictly correct).

text: To show a set \(U\) in \(X\) is equal to the universe set, you must show that \(U\) contains all elements of \(X\) . Here we adapt Example 9.2.1 to be a problem about the universe set.
html: To show a set \(U\) in \(X\) is equal to the universe set, you must show that \(U\) contains all elements of \(X\). Here we adapt Example 9.2.1 to be a problem about the universe set.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Show that \(\{x:\mathbb{R}\mid-1<x\}\cup \{x:\mathbb{R}\mid x < 1\}=\mathbb{R}\) .
html: Show that \(\{x:\mathbb{R}\mid-1<x\}\cup \{x:\mathbb{R}\mid x < 1\}=\mathbb{R}\).

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: We must show that for all real numbers \(t\) , either \(-1<t\) or \(t<1\) .
html: We must show that for all real numbers \(t\), either \(-1<t\) or \(t<1\).

text: Case 1 ( \(t\le 0\) ): Then \(t<1\) .
html: Case 1 (\(t\le 0\)): Then \(t<1\).
Case 2 (\(t> 0\)): Then \(-1<t\).

text: For the first five problems, I provide a tactic check_equality_of_explicit_sets which will prove the statement if you have formulated it correctly. This tactic simply runs ext , then dsimp , then exhaust :
html: For the first five problems, I provide a tactic check_equality_of_explicit_sets which will prove the statement if you have formulated it correctly. This tactic simply runs ext, then dsimp, then exhaust:

text: Let \(X\) be a type. The collection of all sets in \(X\) can itself be considered as a type. This type is sometimes denoted \(\mathcal{P}(X)\) . 1 For example, \(\{3,4,5\}\) , \(\{n:\mathbb{N}\mid 8<n\}\) , and \(\{k:\mathbb{N}\mid\exists \ a, a^2=k\}\) are all sets of natural numbers, which means they are all of type \(\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})\) .
html: Let \(X\) be a type. The collection of all sets in \(X\) can itself be considered as a type. This type is sometimes denoted \(\mathcal{P}(X)\). 1 For example, \(\{3,4,5\}\), \(\{n:\mathbb{N}\mid 8<n\}\), and \(\{k:\mathbb{N}\mid\exists \ a, a^2=k\}\) are all sets of natural numbers, which means they are all of type \(\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})\).

text: In Lean, for a type X , the type of sets in X is denoted Set X . Lean will confirm that the three objects described above all have type Set ℕ :
html: In Lean, for a type X, the type of sets in X is denoted Set X. Lean will confirm that the three objects described above all have type Set ℕ:

text: This operation can be iterated: you can have sets in the type of sets, and so on.
html: This operation can be iterated: you can have sets in the type of sets, and so on.

text: Exercise: write down an object of type Set (Set (Set ℕ)) .
html: Exercise: write down an object of type Set (Set (Set ℕ)).

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Show that \(\{n:\mathbb{N}\mid n\text{ is even}\}\notin\{s:\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})\mid 3 ∈s\}\) .
html: Show that \(\{n:\mathbb{N}\mid n\text{ is even}\}\notin\{s:\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})\mid 3 ∈s\}\).

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: We must show 3 is not even. It suffices to show that 3 is odd. Indeed, \(3=2\cdot 1+1\) .
html: We must show 3 is not even. It suffices to show that 3 is odd. Indeed, \(3=2\cdot 1+1\).

text: Since \(\mathcal{P}(X)\) , the type of sets in \(X\) , is itself a type, we can consider functions to and from it.
html: Since \(\mathcal{P}(X)\), the type of sets in \(X\), is itself a type, we can consider functions to and from it.

text: For example, given a set \(s\) of natural numbers, we can build a new set \(\{n:\mathbb{N}\mid n+1 \in s\}\) . Lean confirms that this operation (let us call it \(p\) ) is a function from \(\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})\) to \(\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})\) .
html: For example, given a set \(s\) of natural numbers, we can build a new set \(\{n:\mathbb{N}\mid n+1 \in s\}\). Lean confirms that this operation (let us call it \(p\)) is a function from \(\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})\) to \(\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})\).

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Show that the function \(p\) is not injective.
html: Show that the function \(p\) is not injective.

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: We must show that there exist sets \(s\) and \(t\) , such that (i) \(\{n:\mathbb{N}\mid n+1 \in s\} = \{n:\mathbb{N}\mid n+1 \in t\}\) , and (ii) \(s \ne t\) .
html: We must show that there exist sets \(s\) and \(t\), such that (i) \(\{n:\mathbb{N}\mid n+1 \in s\} = \{n:\mathbb{N}\mid n+1 \in t\}\), and (ii) \(s \ne t\).

text: Indeed, let us show that the sets \(\{0\}\) and \(\emptyset\) have this property. We must show, (i) \(\{n:\mathbb{N}\mid n+1 = 0\} = \emptyset\) , and (ii) \(\{0\} \ne \emptyset\) .
html: Indeed, let us show that the sets \(\{0\}\) and \(\emptyset\) have this property. We must show, (i) \(\{n:\mathbb{N}\mid n+1 = 0\} = \emptyset\), and (ii) \(\{0\} \ne \emptyset\).

text: For the first statement, let \(x\) be a natural number. We must show that \(x+1 \ne 0\) , which is true since \(x+1 > 0\) .
html: For the first statement, let \(x\) be a natural number. We must show that \(x+1 \ne 0\), which is true since \(x+1 > 0\).

text: For the second statement, we must show that there exists a natural number \(k\) such that \(k\in\{0\}\) and \(k\notin\emptyset\) , or vice versa. Indeed, \(k=0\) has this property.
html: For the second statement, we must show that there exists a natural number \(k\) such that \(k\in\{0\}\) and \(k\notin\emptyset\), or vice versa. Indeed, \(k=0\) has this property.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Consider the function \(q : \mathcal{P}(\mathbb{Z})\to \mathcal{P}(\mathbb{Z})\) defined by, \(q(s)=\{n:\mathbb{Z}\mid n+1\in s\}\) . Show that the function \(q\) is injective.
html: Consider the function \(q : \mathcal{P}(\mathbb{Z})\to \mathcal{P}(\mathbb{Z})\) defined by, \(q(s)=\{n:\mathbb{Z}\mid n+1\in s\}\). Show that the function \(q\) is injective.

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: We must show that for all sets \(s\) and \(t\) , if \(\{n:\mathbb{Z}\mid n+1 \in s\} = \{n:\mathbb{Z}\mid n+1 \in t\}\) , then \(s = t\) .
html: We must show that for all sets \(s\) and \(t\), if \(\{n:\mathbb{Z}\mid n+1 \in s\} = \{n:\mathbb{Z}\mid n+1 \in t\}\), then \(s = t\).

text: Indeed, let \(s\) and \(t\) be sets and suppose that \(\{n:\mathbb{Z}\mid n+1 \in s\} = \{n:\mathbb{Z}\mid n+1 \in t\}\) .
html: Indeed, let \(s\) and \(t\) be sets and suppose that \(\{n:\mathbb{Z}\mid n+1 \in s\} = \{n:\mathbb{Z}\mid n+1 \in t\}\).

text: Let \(k\) be an integer. We must show that \(k\in s\) if and only if \(k\in t\) . Indeed, by the assumption, \(k -1\in\{n:\mathbb{Z}\mid n+1 \in s\}\) if and only if \(k-1\in \{n:\mathbb{Z}\mid n+1 \in t\}\) . Simplifying, \(k -1+1 \in s\) if and only if \(k-1+1\in t\) , and so \(k \in s\) if and only if \(k\in t\) .
html: Let \(k\) be an integer. We must show that \(k\in s\) if and only if \(k\in t\). Indeed, by the assumption, \(k -1\in\{n:\mathbb{Z}\mid n+1 \in s\}\) if and only if \(k-1\in \{n:\mathbb{Z}\mid n+1 \in t\}\). Simplifying, \(k -1+1 \in s\) if and only if \(k-1+1\in t\), and so \(k \in s\) if and only if \(k\in t\).

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Let \(X\) be a type. Prove that there does not exist a surjective function from \(X\) to \(\mathcal{P}(X)\) .
html: Let \(X\) be a type. Prove that there does not exist a surjective function from \(X\) to \(\mathcal{P}(X)\).

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: Suppose for the sake of contradiction that some function \(f:X\to\mathcal{P}(X)\) were surjective. We introduce the following set in \(X\) :
html: Suppose for the sake of contradiction that some function \(f:X\to\mathcal{P}(X)\) were surjective. We introduce the following set in \(X\):

text: Since \(f\) is surjective, there exists some \(x\) of type \(X\) such that \(f(x)=s\) . We consider cases according to whether \(x\in s\) .
html: Since \(f\) is surjective, there exists some \(x\) of type \(X\) such that \(f(x)=s\). We consider cases according to whether \(x\in s\).

text: Case 1 ( \(x\in s\) ): Then by the definition of \(s\) , it is true that \(x\notin f(x)\) , so since \(f(x)=s\) , we have \(x\notin s\) , contradiction.
html: Case 1 (\(x\in s\)): Then by the definition of \(s\), it is true that \(x\notin f(x)\), so since \(f(x)=s\), we have \(x\notin s\), contradiction.

text:
Case 2 ( \(x
otin s\) ): Then by the definition of \(s\) , it is false that \(x
otin f(x)\) , so since \(f(x)=s\) , we have that it is false that \(x
otin s\) , contradiction.
html: Case 2 (\(x otin s\)): Then by the definition of \(s\), it is false that \(x otin f(x)\), so since \(f(x)=s\), we have that it is false that \(x otin s\), contradiction.

text: The idea behind this twisty proof is sometimes called the barber paradox . Here is the version featuring barbers. There is a town with a barber, and in this town, the barber shaves all men who don’t shave themselves. Paradox: does the barber shave himself?
html: The idea behind this twisty proof is sometimes called the barber paradox. Here is the version featuring barbers. There is a town with a barber, and in this town, the barber shaves all men who don’t shave themselves. Paradox: does the barber shave himself?

text: Let us define that a set \(s\) in a type \(X\) has size at least two , if there exist \(x_1\) and \(x_2\) in \(s\) which are different, and has size at least three , if there exist \(x_1\) , \(x_2\) and \(x_3\) in \(s\) which are all different. Let \(s\) and \(t\) be sets of size at least two in some type \(X\) , and suppose that \(s \cap t\) does not have size at least two. Show that \(s\cup t\) has size at least three. This problem features a ton of different cases. Use exhaust liberally to knock off sub-cases. def SizeAtLeastTwo ( s : Set X ) : Prop := ∃ x1 x2 : X , x1 ≠ x2 ∧ x1 ∈ s ∧ x2 ∈ s def SizeAtLeastThree ( s : Set X ) : Prop := ∃ x1 x2 x3 : X , x1 ≠ x2 ∧ x1 ≠ x3 ∧ x2 ≠ x3 ∧ x1 ∈ s ∧ x2 ∈ s ∧ x3 ∈ s example { s t : Set X } ( hs : SizeAtLeastTwo s ) ( ht : SizeAtLeastTwo t ) ( hst : ¬ SizeAtLeastTwo ( s ∩ t )) : SizeAtLeastThree ( s ∪ t ) := by sorry
html:
• Let us define that a set \(s\) in a type \(X\) has size at least two, if there exist \(x_1\) and \(x_2\) in \(s\) which are different, and has size at least three, if there exist \(x_1\), \(x_2\) and \(x_3\) in \(s\) which are all different.

    Let \(s\) and \(t\) be sets of size at least two in some type \(X\), and suppose that \(s \cap t\) does not have size at least two. Show that \(s\cup t\) has size at least three.

    This problem features a ton of different cases. Use exhaust liberally to knock off sub-cases.

    def SizeAtLeastTwo (s : Set X) : Prop := ∃ x1 x2 : X, x1 ≠ x2 ∧ x1 ∈ s ∧ x2 ∈ s
def SizeAtLeastThree (s : Set X) : Prop :=
  ∃ x1 x2 x3 : X, x1 ≠ x2 ∧ x1 ≠ x3 ∧ x2 ≠ x3 ∧ x1 ∈ s ∧ x2 ∈ s ∧ x3 ∈ s

example {s t : Set X} (hs : SizeAtLeastTwo s) (ht : SizeAtLeastTwo t)
    (hst : ¬ SizeAtLeastTwo (s ∩ t)) :
    SizeAtLeastThree (s ∪ t) := by
  sorry

text: Consider the function \(r : \mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})\to \mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})\) defined by, \(r(s)=s\cup \{3\}\) . Show that \(r\) is not injective. def r ( s : Set ℕ ) : Set ℕ := s ∪ { 3 } example : ¬ Injective r := by sorry
html:
• Consider the function \(r : \mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})\to \mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})\) defined by, \(r(s)=s\cup \{3\}\). Show that \(r\) is not injective.

    def r (s : Set ℕ) : Set ℕ := s ∪ {3}

example : ¬ Injective r := by
  sorry

text: Consider the sequence \(U_n\) of sets of integers defined recursively by, \[\begin{split}U_0&=\mathbb{Z} \\ \text{for }n:\mathbb{N},\quad U_{n+1} &=\{x:\mathbb{Z}\mid \exists \ y\in U_n, x = 2y \}\end{split}\] Show that for all natural numbers \(n\) , \(U_n=\{x:\mathbb{Z}\mid 2^n\mid x\}\) . def U : ℕ → Set ℤ | 0 => univ | n + 1 => { x : ℤ | ∃ y ∈ U n , x = 2 * y } example ( n : ℕ ) : U n = { x : ℤ | ( 2 : ℤ ) ^ n ∣ x } := by simple_induction n with k hk · rw [ U ] sorry · rw [ U ] ext x dsimp sorry
html:
• Consider the sequence \(U_n\) of sets of integers defined recursively by,

    \[\begin{split}U_0&=\mathbb{Z} \\ \text{for }n:\mathbb{N},\quad U_{n+1} &=\{x:\mathbb{Z}\mid \exists \ y\in U_n, x = 2y \}\end{split}\]

    Show that for all natural numbers \(n\), \(U_n=\{x:\mathbb{Z}\mid 2^n\mid x\}\).

    def U : ℕ → Set ℤ
  | 0 => univ
  | n + 1 => {x : ℤ | ∃ y ∈ U n, x = 2 * y}

example (n : ℕ) : U n = {x : ℤ | (2:ℤ) ^ n ∣ x} := by
  simple_induction n with k hk
  · rw [U]
    sorry
  · rw [U]
    ext x
    dsimp
    sorry
1

text: Just as sets provide a convenient language for properties of the objects in a type, relations provide a convenient language for the properties of the pairs of objects in a type. This might sound dry and abstract, but such properties turn up all over mathematics: the property of one real number being less than another; the property of one integer being congruent to another modulo 5; the property of one set being a subset of another; the property of one function being inverse to another.
html: Just as sets provide a convenient language for properties of the objects in a type, relations provide a convenient language for the properties of the pairs of objects in a type. This might sound dry and abstract, but such properties turn up all over mathematics: the property of one real number being less than another; the property of one integer being congruent to another modulo 5; the property of one set being a subset of another; the property of one function being inverse to another.

text: In this chapter we introduce some of the important properties which relations themselves can have: they can be reflexive , symmetric , antisymmetric or transitive , or any combination of these.
html: In this chapter we introduce some of the important properties which relations themselves can have: they can be reflexive, symmetric, antisymmetric or transitive, or any combination of these.

text: Definition
html: Definition

text: A relation \(\sim\) on a type \(X\) is reflexive , if for all \(x\) of type \(X\) , it is true that \(x\sim x\) .
html: A relation \(\sim\) on a type \(X\) is reflexive, if for all \(x\) of type \(X\), it is true that \(x\sim x\).

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Prove that the relation \(|\) on \(\mathbb{N}\) is reflexive.
html: Prove that the relation \(|\) on \(\mathbb{N}\) is reflexive.

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: We must show that for all natural numbers \(x\) , \(x\mid x\) . Indeed, let \(x\) be a natural number; then \(x=x\cdot 1\) .
html: We must show that for all natural numbers \(x\), \(x\mid x\). Indeed, let \(x\) be a natural number; then \(x=x\cdot 1\).

text: Definition
html: Definition

text: A relation \(\sim\) on a type \(X\) is symmetric , if for all \(x\) and \(y\) of type \(X\) , if \(x\sim y\) , then \(y\sim x\) .
html: A relation \(\sim\) on a type \(X\) is symmetric, if for all \(x\) and \(y\) of type \(X\), if \(x\sim y\), then \(y\sim x\).

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Prove that the relation \(|\) on \(\mathbb{N}\) is not symmetric.
html: Prove that the relation \(|\) on \(\mathbb{N}\) is not symmetric.

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: We must show that there exist natural numbers \(x\) and \(y\) such that, \(x\mid y\) and \(y\mid x\) . Indeed, we have \(2=1\cdot 2\) , so \(1\mid 2\) , and \(2\cdot 0<1<2\cdot 1\) , so \(2\not \mid 1\) .
html: We must show that there exist natural numbers \(x\) and \(y\) such that, \(x\mid y\) and \(y\mid x\). Indeed, we have \(2=1\cdot 2\), so \(1\mid 2\), and \(2\cdot 0<1<2\cdot 1\), so \(2\not \mid 1\).

text: Definition
html: Definition

text: A relation \(\sim\) on a type \(X\) is antisymmetric , if for all \(x\) and \(y\) of type \(X\) , if \(x\sim y\) and \(y\sim x\) , then \(x=y\) .
html: A relation \(\sim\) on a type \(X\) is antisymmetric, if for all \(x\) and \(y\) of type \(X\), if \(x\sim y\) and \(y\sim x\), then \(x=y\).

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Prove that the relation \(|\) on \(\mathbb{N}\) is antisymmetric.
html: Prove that the relation \(|\) on \(\mathbb{N}\) is antisymmetric.

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: We first note the following fact ( \(\star\) ): if \(m\) and \(n\) are natural numbers with \(m=0\) and \(m\mid n\) , then \(m=n\) . Indeed, since \(m\mid n\) there exists a natural number \(k\) such that \(n=mk\) , and then
html: We first note the following fact (\(\star\)): if \(m\) and \(n\) are natural numbers with \(m=0\) and \(m\mid n\), then \(m=n\). Indeed, since \(m\mid n\) there exists a natural number \(k\) such that \(n=mk\), and then

text: We now return to the original problem. We must show that for all natural numbers \(x\) and \(y\) , if \(x\mid y\) and \(y\mid x\) , then \(x=y\) . Indeed, let \(x\) and \(y\) be natural numbers and suppose \(x\mid y\) and \(y\mid x\) . If \(x=0\) then by ( \(\star\) ) we are done; likewise if \(y=0\) . Otherwise, \(x>0\) , so since \(y\mid x\) we have \(y\le x\) , and \(y>0\) , so since \(x\mid y\) we have \(x\le y\) , Putting these together, \(x=y\) .
html: We now return to the original problem. We must show that for all natural numbers \(x\) and \(y\), if \(x\mid y\) and \(y\mid x\), then \(x=y\). Indeed, let \(x\) and \(y\) be natural numbers and suppose \(x\mid y\) and \(y\mid x\). If \(x=0\) then by (\(\star\)) we are done; likewise if \(y=0\). Otherwise, \(x>0\), so since \(y\mid x\) we have \(y\le x\), and \(y>0\), so since \(x\mid y\) we have \(x\le y\), Putting these together, \(x=y\).

text: Definition
html: Definition

text: A relation \(\sim\) on a type \(X\) is transitive , if for all \(x\) , \(y\) and \(z\) of type \(X\) , if \(x\sim y\) and \(y\sim z\) , then \(x\sim z\) .
html: A relation \(\sim\) on a type \(X\) is transitive, if for all \(x\), \(y\) and \(z\) of type \(X\), if \(x\sim y\) and \(y\sim z\), then \(x\sim z\).

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Prove that the relation \(|\) on \(\mathbb{N}\) is transitive.
html: Prove that the relation \(|\) on \(\mathbb{N}\) is transitive.

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: We must show that for all natural numbers \(a\) , \(b\) and \(c\) , if \(a\mid b\) and \(b\mid c\) then \(a\mid c\) .
html: We must show that for all natural numbers \(a\), \(b\) and \(c\), if \(a\mid b\) and \(b\mid c\) then \(a\mid c\).

text: Indeed, let \(a\) , \(b\) and \(c\) be natural numbers with these properties. Since \(a\mid b\) there exists a natural number \(k\) such that \(b=ak\) , and since \(b\mid c\) there exists a natural number \(l\) such that \(c=bl\) . We then have
html: Indeed, let \(a\), \(b\) and \(c\) be natural numbers with these properties. Since \(a\mid b\) there exists a natural number \(k\) such that \(b=ak\), and since \(b\mid c\) there exists a natural number \(l\) such that \(c=bl\). We then have

text: so \(a\mid c\) .
html: so \(a\mid c\).

text: Definition
html: Definition

text: A relation is an equivalence relation , if it is reflexive, symmetric and transitive.
html: A relation is an equivalence relation, if it is reflexive, symmetric and transitive.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Let \(n\) be an integer. Show that the relation \(\sim\) on \(\mathbb{Z}\) defined by, \(x\sim y\) if \(x\equiv y\mod n\) is an equivalence relation.
html: Let \(n\) be an integer. Show that the relation \(\sim\) on \(\mathbb{Z}\) defined by, \(x\sim y\) if \(x\equiv y\mod n\) is an equivalence relation.

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: By Example 3.3.10 , the relation \(\sim\) is reflexive.
html: By Example 3.3.10, the relation \(\sim\) is reflexive.

text: By two of the exercises to Section 3.3 , the relation \(\sim\) is symmetric and transitive.
html: By two of the exercises to Section 3.3, the relation \(\sim\) is symmetric and transitive.

text: Let’s sketch (a portion of) this relation as a directed graph, say for \(n=3\) .
html: Let’s sketch (a portion of) this relation as a directed graph, say for \(n=3\).

text: It’s a mess, but it’s just possible to make out a pattern: the numbers form cliques, with, for example, … -5, -2, 1, 4, 7, … all connected to each other and to nothing else. We introduce the following visual shorthand for such a directed graph: when nodes are given different colours, this represents the directed graph in which all nodes of a given colour are connected bi-directionally to all nodes of that colour (including themselves) and to no other nodes.
html: It’s a mess, but it’s just possible to make out a pattern: the numbers form cliques, with, for example, … -5, -2, 1, 4, 7, … all connected to each other and to nothing else. We introduce the following visual shorthand for such a directed graph: when nodes are given different colours, this represents the directed graph in which all nodes of a given colour are connected bi-directionally to all nodes of that colour (including themselves) and to no other nodes.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Show that the relation \(\sim\) on \(\mathbb{Z}\) defined by \(a\sim b\) if \(a^2=b^2\) is an equivalence relation.
html: Show that the relation \(\sim\) on \(\mathbb{Z}\) defined by \(a\sim b\) if \(a^2=b^2\) is an equivalence relation.

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: For all integers \(x\) , we have \(x^2=x^2\) , so \(x\sim x\) . Therefore the relation \(\sim\) is reflexive.
html: For all integers \(x\), we have \(x^2=x^2\), so \(x\sim x\). Therefore the relation \(\sim\) is reflexive.

text: For all integers \(x\) and \(y\) , we have that if \(x\sim y\) then \(x^2=y^2\) , so \(y^2=x^2\) , so \(y\sim z\) . Therefore the relation \(\sim\) is symmetric.
html: For all integers \(x\) and \(y\), we have that if \(x\sim y\) then \(x^2=y^2\), so \(y^2=x^2\), so \(y\sim z\). Therefore the relation \(\sim\) is symmetric.

text: For all integers \(x\) , \(y\) and \(z\) , we have that if \(x\sim y\) and \(y\sim z\) then \(x^2=y^2\) and \(y^2=z^2\) , so
html: For all integers \(x\), \(y\) and \(z\), we have that if \(x\sim y\) and \(y\sim z\) then \(x^2=y^2\) and \(y^2=z^2\), so

text: so \(x\sim z\) . Therefore the relation \(\sim\) is transitive.
html: so \(x\sim z\). Therefore the relation \(\sim\) is transitive.

text: If you try to sketch the directed graph for this relation, you will see that it has the same “clique” behaviour as the previous relation. After sketching the directed graph, sketch the multicoloured “clique” version.
html: If you try to sketch the directed graph for this relation, you will see that it has the same “clique” behaviour as the previous relation. After sketching the directed graph, sketch the multicoloured “clique” version.

text: You have probably guessed that the colouring can be made consistent in this way for any equivalence relation. Let’s make this mathematically rigorous.
html: You have probably guessed that the colouring can be made consistent in this way for any equivalence relation. Let’s make this mathematically rigorous.

text: Let \(r\) be a relation on a type \(\alpha\) , denoted \(\sim\) in infix notation.
html: Let \(r\) be a relation on a type \(\alpha\), denoted \(\sim\) in infix notation.

text: Definition
html: Definition

text: For \(a\) in \(\alpha\) , the equivalence class of \(a\) (denoted \([a]\) ) is \(\{b:\alpha\mid a\sim b\}\) .
html: For \(a\) in \(\alpha\), the equivalence class of \(a\) (denoted \([a]\)) is \(\{b:\alpha\mid a\sim b\}\).

text: Theorem
html: Theorem

text: If the relation \(r\) is symmetric and transitive, then for all \(a_1\) and \(a_2\) , if \(a_1\sim a_2\) , then \([a_1]=[a_2]\) .
html: If the relation \(r\) is symmetric and transitive, then for all \(a_1\) and \(a_2\), if \(a_1\sim a_2\), then \([a_1]=[a_2]\).

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: We must show that for all \(b\) in \(\alpha\) , \(a_1\sim b\) if and only if \(a_2\sim b\) .
html: We must show that for all \(b\) in \(\alpha\), \(a_1\sim b\) if and only if \(a_2\sim b\).

text: First, suppose that \(a_1\sim b\) . Since \(a_1\sim a_2\) , by symmetry \(a_2\sim a_1\) , and so by transitivity \(a_2\sim a_1\sim b\) .
html: First, suppose that \(a_1\sim b\). Since \(a_1\sim a_2\), by symmetry \(a_2\sim a_1\), and so by transitivity \(a_2\sim a_1\sim b\).

text: Conversely, suppose that \(a_2\sim b\) . Then since \(a_1\sim a_2\) , by transitivity \(a_1\sim a_2\sim b\) .
html: Conversely, suppose that \(a_2\sim b\). Then since \(a_1\sim a_2\), by transitivity \(a_1\sim a_2\sim b\).

text: Theorem
html: Theorem

text: If the relation \(r\) is reflexive, then every \(a\) is an element of its own equivalence class: \(a\in [a]\) .
html: If the relation \(r\) is reflexive, then every \(a\) is an element of its own equivalence class: \(a\in [a]\).

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: We must show that for all \(a\) , it is true that \(a\sim a\) , and this is the definition of reflexivity.
html: We must show that for all \(a\), it is true that \(a\sim a\), and this is the definition of reflexivity.

text: Consider the relation \(=\) on \(\mathbb{Z}\) . It is an equivalence relation, by Example 10.1.2 .
html: Consider the relation \(=\) on \(\mathbb{Z}\). It is an equivalence relation, by Example 10.1.2.

text: Exercise: Sketch this relation, by drawing (a portion of) the underlying type \(\mathbb{Z}\) along a line, then colouring each equivalence class in a different colour.
html: Exercise: Sketch this relation, by drawing (a portion of) the underlying type \(\mathbb{Z}\) along a line, then colouring each equivalence class in a different colour.

text: Problem
html: Problem

text: Show that the relation \(\sim\) on \(\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{N}\) defined by, \((a,b)\sim(c,d)\) if \(a(d+1)=c(b+1)\) , is an equivalence relation.
html: Show that the relation \(\sim\) on \(\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{N}\) defined by, \((a,b)\sim(c,d)\) if \(a(d+1)=c(b+1)\), is an equivalence relation.

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: For all \((a,b)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{N}\) , \(a(b+1)=a(b+1)\) , so \((a,b)\sim (a,b)\) . Therefore \(\sim\) is reflexive.
html: For all \((a,b)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{N}\), \(a(b+1)=a(b+1)\), so \((a,b)\sim (a,b)\). Therefore \(\sim\) is reflexive.

text: For all \((a,b)\) and \((c,d)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{N}\) , if \((a,b)\sim (c,d)\) then \(a(d+1)=c(b+1)\) , so \(c(b+1)=a(d+1)\) , so \((c,d)\sim (a,b)\) . Therefore \(\sim\) is symmetric.
html: For all \((a,b)\) and \((c,d)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{N}\), if \((a,b)\sim (c,d)\) then \(a(d+1)=c(b+1)\), so \(c(b+1)=a(d+1)\), so \((c,d)\sim (a,b)\). Therefore \(\sim\) is symmetric.

text: For all \((a,b)\) , \((c,d)\) and \((e,f)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{N}\) , if \((a,b)\sim (c,d)\) and \((c,d)\sim (e,f)\) then \(a(d+1)=c(b+1)\) and \(c(f+1)=e(d+1)\) . We will show that \(a(f+1)=e(b+1)\) , which will imply that \((a,b)\sim (e,f)\) , which proves the transitivity of \(\sim\) .
html: For all \((a,b)\), \((c,d)\) and \((e,f)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{N}\), if \((a,b)\sim (c,d)\) and \((c,d)\sim (e,f)\) then \(a(d+1)=c(b+1)\) and \(c(f+1)=e(d+1)\). We will show that \(a(f+1)=e(b+1)\), which will imply that \((a,b)\sim (e,f)\), which proves the transitivity of \(\sim\).

text: Since \(d+1>0\) , it suffices to prove that \((d+1)\left[a(f+1)\right]=(d+1)\left[e(b+1)\right]\) . Let \(B:= b+1\) , \(D:= d+1\) , and \(F:= f+1\) : then we know that \(aD=cB\) and \(cF=eD\) and need to prove that \(D(aF)=D(eB)\) .
html: Since \(d+1>0\), it suffices to prove that \((d+1)\left[a(f+1)\right]=(d+1)\left[e(b+1)\right]\). Let \(B:= b+1\), \(D:= d+1\), and \(F:= f+1\): then we know that \(aD=cB\) and \(cF=eD\) and need to prove that \(D(aF)=D(eB)\).

text: Indeed,
html: Indeed,

text: Now, sketch the relation \(\sim\) , by drawing (a portion of) the underlying type \(\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{N}\) in the plane, then colouring each equivalence class in a different colour.
html: Now, sketch the relation \(\sim\), by drawing (a portion of) the underlying type \(\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{N}\) in the plane, then colouring each equivalence class in a different colour.

text: Theorem
html: Theorem

text: The relation \(\sim\) on the type of level-0 types defined by, \(\alpha\sim\beta\) if there exists a bijective function \(f:\alpha\to\beta\) , is an equivalence relation.
html: The relation \(\sim\) on the type of level-0 types defined by, \(\alpha\sim\beta\) if there exists a bijective function \(f:\alpha\to\beta\), is an equivalence relation.

text: Consider the following finite inductive type Little, and the following relation \(\sim\) on the Little type. Determine which of these properties hold for the relation \(\sim\) : reflexive; symmetric; antisymmetric; transitive. section inductive Little | meg | jo | beth | amy deriving DecidableEq open Little @[reducible] def s : Little → Little → Prop | meg , meg => True | meg , jo => True | meg , beth => True | meg , amy => True | jo , meg => True | jo , jo => True | jo , beth => True | jo , amy => False | beth , meg => True | beth , jo => True | beth , beth => False | beth , amy => True | amy , meg => True | amy , jo => False | amy , beth => True | amy , amy => True local infix : 50 " ∼ " => s example : Reflexive ( · ∼ · ) := by sorry example : ¬ Reflexive ( · ∼ · ) := by sorry example : Symmetric ( · ∼ · ) := by sorry example : ¬ Symmetric ( · ∼ · ) := by sorry example : AntiSymmetric ( · ∼ · ) := by sorry example : ¬ AntiSymmetric ( · ∼ · ) := by sorry example : Transitive ( · ∼ · ) := by sorry example : ¬ Transitive ( · ∼ · ) := by sorry
html:
• Consider the following finite inductive type Little, and the following relation \(\sim\) on the Little type. Determine which of these properties hold for the relation \(\sim\):

    1. reflexive;

    2. symmetric;

    3. antisymmetric;

    4. transitive.

    section
inductive Little
  | meg
  | jo
  | beth
  | amy
  deriving DecidableEq

open Little

@[reducible] def s : Little → Little → Prop
  | meg, meg => True
  | meg, jo => True
  | meg, beth => True
  | meg, amy => True
  | jo, meg => True
  | jo, jo => True
  | jo, beth => True
  | jo, amy => False
  | beth, meg => True
  | beth, jo => True
  | beth, beth => False
  | beth, amy => True
  | amy, meg => True
  | amy, jo => False
  | amy, beth => True
  | amy, amy => True

local infix:50 " ∼ " => s

    example : Reflexive (· ∼ ·) := by
  sorry

text: Consider the relation \(\sim\) on \(\mathbb{Z}^2\) defined by, \((a,b)\sim(c,d)\) if there exist positive integers \(m\) and \(n\) with \(mb(b^2-3a^2)=nd(d^2-3c^2)\) . Show that \(\sim\) is an equivalence relation. Sketch the relation \(\sim\) , by drawing (a portion of) the underlying type \(\mathbb{Z}^2\) in the plane, then colouring each equivalence class in a different colour. local infix : 50 "∼" => fun (( a , b ) : ℤ × ℤ ) ( c , d ) ↦ ∃ m n , m > 0 ∧ n > 0 ∧ m * b * ( b ^ 2 - 3 * a ^ 2 ) = n * d * ( d ^ 2 - 3 * c ^ 2 ) example : Reflexive ( · ∼ · ) := by sorry example : Symmetric ( · ∼ · ) := by sorry example : Transitive ( · ∼ · ) := by sorry
html:
• Consider the relation \(\sim\) on \(\mathbb{Z}^2\) defined by, \((a,b)\sim(c,d)\) if there exist positive integers \(m\) and \(n\) with \(mb(b^2-3a^2)=nd(d^2-3c^2)\).

    • Show that \(\sim\) is an equivalence relation.

    • Sketch the relation \(\sim\), by drawing (a portion of) the underlying type \(\mathbb{Z}^2\) in the plane, then colouring each equivalence class in a different colour.

    local infix:50 "∼" => fun ((a, b) : ℤ × ℤ) (c, d) ↦
  ∃ m n, m > 0 ∧ n > 0 ∧ m * b * (b ^ 2 - 3 * a ^ 2) = n * d * (d ^ 2 - 3 * c ^ 2)

text: The Mechanics of Proof
html: The Mechanics of Proof

text: This is a book dealing with how to write careful, rigorous mathematical proofs. The book is paired with code in the computer formalization language Lean . Head over to the associated GitHub repository, https://github.com/hrmacbeth/math2001 , to download this code to your own computer or to open it in the cloud on Gitpod.
html: This is a book dealing with how to write careful, rigorous mathematical proofs. The book is paired with code in the computer formalization language Lean. Head over to the associated GitHub repository, https://github.com/hrmacbeth/math2001, to download this code to your own computer or to open it in the cloud on Gitpod.

text: This book is aimed at the early university level and has been written for the course Math 2001, at Fordham University. Please reach out to the author, Heather Macbeth , with comments and corrections.

text: Cancels a common factor from LHS/RHS of equality/inequality, cancels an exponentiation common to both sides, etc.
html: Cancels a common factor from LHS/RHS of equality/inequality, cancels an exponentiation common to both sides, etc.

text: constructor (first use: Section 2.4 ; for ↔ goals: Section 4.2 )
html: constructor (first use: Section 2.4; for ↔ goals: Section 4.2)

text: Splits an “and” goal ( \(\land\) ) into sub-goals for its left and right parts.
html: Splits an “and” goal (\(\land\)) into sub-goals for its left and right parts.

text: contradiction (first use: Section 4.4 )
html: contradiction (first use: Section 4.4)

text: If there are two contradictory hypotheses available, this concludes the proof.
html: If there are two contradictory hypotheses available, this concludes the proof.

text: dsimp (first use: Section 3.1 )
html: dsimp (first use: Section 3.1)

text: Unfolds a definition. Typically used while working on a proof rather than in the final version; it is useful for inspecting a goal or hypothesis more carefully, but in most cases the proof will still work after a dsimp line is deleted.
html: Unfolds a definition. Typically used while working on a proof rather than in the final version; it is useful for inspecting a goal or hypothesis more carefully, but in most cases the proof will still work after a dsimp line is deleted.

text: * extra (first use: Section 1.4 ; for congruences: Section 3.4 )
html: * extra (first use: Section 1.4; for congruences: Section 3.4)

text: A comparison tactic for inequalities, or other relations such as congruences: checks an inequality whose two sides differ by the addition of a positive quantity, a congruence mod 3 whose two sides differ by a multiple of 3, etc.
html: A comparison tactic for inequalities, or other relations such as congruences: checks an inequality whose two sides differ by the addition of a positive quantity, a congruence mod 3 whose two sides differ by a multiple of 3, etc.

text: interval_cases (first use: Section 4.1 )
html: interval_cases (first use: Section 4.1)

text: Given a natural-number or integer variable \(n\) for which numeric upper and lower bounds are available, produce cases for each of the numeric possibilities for \(n\) .
html: Given a natural-number or integer variable \(n\) for which numeric upper and lower bounds are available, produce cases for each of the numeric possibilities for \(n\).

text: intro (first use: Section 4.1 ; for \(\lnot\) goals: Section 4.5 )
html: intro (first use: Section 4.1; for \(\lnot\) goals: Section 4.5)

text: Introduces a universally quantified variable ( \(\forall\) ) or the antecedent of an implication ( \(\to\) ) from the goal, or assumes (for the sake of contradiction) the positive version of a negation ( \(\lnot\) ) goal.
html: Introduces a universally quantified variable (\(\forall\)) or the antecedent of an implication (\(\to\)) from the goal, or assumes (for the sake of contradiction) the positive version of a negation (\(\lnot\)) goal.

text: left (first use: Section 2.3 )
html: left (first use: Section 2.3)

text: Selects the left alternative of an “or” goal ( \(\lor\) ).
html: Selects the left alternative of an “or” goal (\(\lor\)).

text: have (first use: Section 2.1 ; with a lemma: Section 2.3 ; introducing a new goal: Section 2.4 )
html: have (first use: Section 2.1; with a lemma: Section 2.3; introducing a new goal: Section 2.4)

text: Records a fact (followed by the proof of that fact), which then becomes available as an extra hypothesis.
html: Records a fact (followed by the proof of that fact), which then becomes available as an extra hypothesis.

text: mod_cases (first use: Section 3.4 )
html: mod_cases (first use: Section 3.4)

text: Introduces cases for a variable according to its residue modulo a specified number.
html: Introduces cases for a variable according to its residue modulo a specified number.

text: * numbers (first use: Section 1.4 ; with at for contradictions: Section 4.4 )
html: * numbers (first use: Section 1.4; with at for contradictions: Section 4.4)

text: Proves numeric facts, like \(3\cdot 12 < 13 + 25\) or \(3\cdot 5+1=4\cdot 4\) .
html: Proves numeric facts, like \(3\cdot 12 < 13 + 25\) or \(3\cdot 5+1=4\cdot 4\).

text: obtain (first use for \(\lor\) : Section 2.3 ; for \(\land\) : Section 2.4 ; for \(\exists\) : Section 2.5 )
html: obtain (first use for \(\lor\): Section 2.3; for \(\land\): Section 2.4; for \(\exists\): Section 2.5)

text: Takes apart a hypothesis of the form “or” ( \(\lor\) ), “and” ( \(\land\) ) or “there exists” ( \(\exists\) ).
html: Takes apart a hypothesis of the form “or” (\(\lor\)), “and” (\(\land\)) or “there exists” (\(\exists\)).

text: push_neg (first use: Section 5.3 )
html: push_neg (first use: Section 5.3)

text: Converts a hypothesis or goal to a logically equivalent form with negations pushed inwards as far as possible.
html: Converts a hypothesis or goal to a logically equivalent form with negations pushed inwards as far as possible.

text: rel (first use: Section 1.4 ; for congruences: Section 3.4 ; for logical equivalences: Section 5.3 )
html: rel (first use: Section 1.4; for congruences: Section 3.4; for logical equivalences: Section 5.3)

text: A “substitution-like” tactic for inequalities, or other relations such as congruences: looks for the left-hand side of a specified inequality (or congruence, …) fact in the goal, and replaces it with the right-hand side of that fact, if that replacement is “obviously” a valid inequality (or modular arithmetic, …) deduction. Compare with rw .
html: A “substitution-like” tactic for inequalities, or other relations such as congruences: looks for the left-hand side of a specified inequality (or congruence, …) fact in the goal, and replaces it with the right-hand side of that fact, if that replacement is “obviously” a valid inequality (or modular arithmetic, …) deduction. Compare with rw.

text: right (first use: Section 2.3 )
html: right (first use: Section 2.3)

text: Selects the right alternative of an “or” goal ( \(\lor\) ).
html: Selects the right alternative of an “or” goal (\(\lor\)).

text: ring (first use: Section 1.2 )
html: ring (first use: Section 1.2)

text: Solves algebraic equality goals such as \((x + y) ^ 2 = x ^ 2 + 2xy + y ^ 2\) , when the proof is effectively “expand out both sides and rearrange”.
html: Solves algebraic equality goals such as \((x + y) ^ 2 = x ^ 2 + 2xy + y ^ 2\), when the proof is effectively “expand out both sides and rearrange”.

text: rw (first use: Section 1.2 ; for ↔ hypotheses/lemmas: Section 4.2 )
html: rw (first use: Section 1.2; for ↔ hypotheses/lemmas: Section 4.2)

text: Substitution: looks for the left-hand side of a specified equality fact in the goal, and replaces it with the right-hand side of that fact.
html: Substitution: looks for the left-hand side of a specified equality fact in the goal, and replaces it with the right-hand side of that fact.

text: With ↔ hypotheses/lemmas, replaces the left-hand side of the specified ↔ fact with the right-hand side of that fact.
html: With ↔ hypotheses/lemmas, replaces the left-hand side of the specified ↔ fact with the right-hand side of that fact.

text: use (first use: Section 2.5 )
html: use (first use: Section 2.5)

text: Provides a witness to an existential goal ( \(\exists\) ).
html: Provides a witness to an existential goal (\(\exists\)).

text: Transitioning to mainstream Lean
html: Transitioning to mainstream Lean

text: If you have enjoyed this book, you may wish to work further with Lean, for example by working through the book Mathematics in Lean or by starting an independent formalization project .
html: If you have enjoyed this book, you may wish to work further with Lean, for example by working through the book Mathematics in Lean or by starting an independent formalization project.

text: You will discover that the Lean “dialect” used in this book differs from the mainstream mathematical Lean used in the library mathlib and its associated literature such as Mathematics in Lean . To help you adjust, in this appendix I outline the major differences.
html: You will discover that the Lean “dialect” used in this book differs from the mainstream mathematical Lean used in the library mathlib and its associated literature such as Mathematics in Lean. To help you adjust, in this appendix I outline the major differences.

text: Some tactics used in this book are deliberately weakened versions of tactics in mathlib: I have done this to block certain of Lean’s capabilities because prose proofs at the level of this book would typically write out the details in full. These deliberately weakened tactics include:

text: Another point not covered in this book is how to interact with the library. Since mathlib contains over a million lines of Lean code, it is not always easy to find out whether a lemma you want exists in the library! In this book, I avoid the issue by telling you in advance the name of any lemma I expect you to use.
html: Another point not covered in this book is how to interact with the library. Since mathlib contains over a million lines of Lean code, it is not always easy to find out whether a lemma you want exists in the library! In this book, I avoid the issue by telling you in advance the name of any lemma I expect you to use.

text: A few basic points to be aware of in interacting with the library:
html: A few basic points to be aware of in interacting with the library:

text: All this only scratches the surface: there are many more features of Lean to help you in your mathematical discoveries. Mathematics in Lean , the community website , and the community discussion board offer further pointers for exploration. Have fun!
html: All this only scratches the surface: there are many more features of Lean to help you in your mathematical discoveries. Mathematics in Lean, the community website, and the community discussion board offer further pointers for exploration. Have fun!

text: The online documentation is generally more readable than the source code: it is searchable and it has internal hyperlinks.
html: • The online documentation is generally more readable than the source code: it is searchable and it has internal hyperlinks.

text: Lemmas are often stated in extreme generality … \((a - b) + c = a - (b - c)\) is stated not for \(\mathbb{R}\) but for a SubtractionCommMonoid . You may have better luck interacting with the library after first courses on abstract algebra and point-set topology.
html: • Lemmas are often stated in extreme generality … \((a - b) + c = a - (b - c)\) is stated not for \(\mathbb{R}\) but for a SubtractionCommMonoid. You may have better luck interacting with the library after first courses on abstract algebra and point-set topology.

text: If you can guess the exact statement of a lemma, the tactic exact? will find it in the library.
html: • If you can guess the exact statement of a lemma, the tactic exact? will find it in the library.
